title: Prompt Contract
output:
Reading through both takes, they're not actually in tension — Codex laid out the contract in broad strokes (local-first, zero-account, 3-4 modes with real prompt packs, full pass-and-play loop, recap card + ShareLink, StoreKit paywall, haptics/motion, accessibility and state coverage, buildable Xcode project) and I spent my round sharpening the same contract with the specific failure modes that tend to sneak past a "looks done" build: unedited/AI-dumped prompt packs pretending to be curated, state bugs in the pass-and-play round machine (wrong player, stale tally, recap citing the wrong winner), a paywall that's a boolean pretending to be StoreKit, and the fact that the prompt's own invalidation criterion (cards generated but never shared) isn't something this local-first MVP can instrument — so nobody gets to claim "the viral loop works" as a completion criterion later, only "the share flow works and the card is genuinely good."

Nobody pushed back on any of that, and both of us landed on the same non-goals (no networking, no accounts, no LLM/AR/ML, no multi-device dependency) and the same tie-breaker rule: if time forces a choice between 4 thin modes and 3 well-made ones, cut to 3. This reads as real convergence, not premature agreement — the two rounds are complementary, not duplicative.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Original prompt**: preserved verbatim in `initial_prompt.md` and reproduced in full above — the Campfire spec (pass-and-play icebreaker game, 3-4 modes, curated prompt packs, recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall via StoreKit, fully local/offline core loop) plus the parent portfolio wrapper (context only — this workspace builds the `campfire` app, the "5 apps" framing does not add tasks here).

**Hard requirements**:
- Single-app, local-first SwiftUI build: zero accounts, zero networking, zero other installs required for the core loop, fully airplane-mode-proof.
- 3-4 game modes, each backed by real, editorially-reviewed prompt content — not placeholder text and not an unedited raw list.
- Complete pass-and-play flow: mode select → player setup (count/names) → rounds with prompts → per-round vote/answer tally → end-of-session recap.
- Recap card that renders as an actual shareable artifact via `ShareLink`, good enough that a reasonable person would want to send it.
- Campfire+ paywall wired to real StoreKit 2 entitlement checks (testable via StoreKit configuration/sandbox), gating pack unlocks, custom prompts, and unlimited saved recaps — with restore-purchases handled and a defined (non-crashing, non-silent) behavior when a free user taps a locked pack.
- Haptics tuned per game mode; playful spring/bounce motion and a confetti-style reveal at round end.
- Full state coverage: empty, loading, success, and error states wherever reachable; accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, Dynamic Type, WCAG AA contrast); robust local persistence for packs and session history.
- A real, compiling Xcode project with full source in `app_build` — no app is "done" without this.

**Non-goals**: no core networking or multi-device sync in v1, no accounts/identity, no server-hosted pack downloads, no LLM-generated prompts (explicitly excluded — a model dumping prompts without an editorial pass for tone/repetition/appropriateness is a silent violation of this even if no code looks wrong), no AR/ML, no generic/empty-copy recap card, no half-built placeholder screens.

**Production-readiness definition**: compiles and runs deterministically on-device under iOS 17+ with actor-safe state management; a full session (all modes) can be played start to finish in airplane mode with no degradation; recap card generation and the share sheet actually work; StoreKit paywall behavior is real and testable in sandbox (any inability to exercise it against a live App Store Connect product must be stated explicitly, not hidden behind "IAP implemented"); prompt packs have had a manual content-quality pass, not just a line count; round-state logic has been exercised across edge cases (2-player minimum, large player count, mid-round backgrounding, zero-completed-round recap) without producing wrong-player or wrong-winner bugs.

**Decision rules for later phases**:
- Any scope change must be logged as an explicit named assumption, never silently substituted.
- If a choice reduces share likelihood or adds startup friction, reject it.
- If 4 modes can't all be done well in the time available, cut to 3 finished modes over 4 thin ones.
- The prompt's own invalidation criterion ("cards generated but never shared") is aspirational/post-launch and unmeasurable by this local-first MVP with no instrumentation — no later phase may claim "the viral loop is validated"; the bar this build can actually meet is "the share flow works and the card is worth sending."
- If a requirement and an implementation shortcut conflict, the requirement wins and the tradeoff gets recorded in the phase log.
- The app cannot be marked done as production-ready if the share loop, offline behavior, or buildability is unmet.

# campfire — Prompt Contract

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:13:13._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Preserve the user's original prompt before any discussion, convert it into a clear execution contract, list hard requirements, non-goals, success metrics, production-readiness expectations, and decision rules for every later phase.

From this, the contract for this phase is:

Hard requirements are: preserve and execute the CAMPFIRE definition as the active child project in this portfolio context; build local-first and offline by default with zero-account, zero-download-party-play core; ship 3 to 4 game modes with real, quality-controlled prompt packs; implement pass-and-play flow from setup through voting, scoring, and recap; generate a shareable recap card and ShareLink delivery; implement Campfire+ entitlement flow with StoreKit; include haptics and motion that feel party-like; keep accessibility, loading/empty/success/error states, and robust local persistence in scope; and target a production-grade SwiftUI app, not a prototype, with a buildable Xcode project in the required output structure.

Non-goals are: no core-networking, no mandatory LLM/AR/ML features, no account or identity system, no multi-device dependency for v1, no generic template card with empty copy, and no half-built placeholder screens; no attempt to satisfy the portfolio “5 apps” build requirement inside this phase other than honoring the parent constraints and not altering the assignment.

Output shape is: one precise contract artifact per app phase that explicitly cites the above constraints and acceptance gates, and every later decision is checkable against these gates. The implementation will be measured by observable behaviors, not prose claims: app launch-to-session completion time, local operation in airplane mode, prompt-card generation, share completion flow, and StoreKit paywall behavior.

Production-readiness means: deterministic local-first behavior on device, compile-stable SwiftUI codebase under iOS 17+ with proper actor-safe state management, accessible UI, state persistence for history and packs, polished transitions and error handling, working share path, paid feature gate, and a build directory that can be run and demonstrated end-to-end without external setup.

Decision rules for later phases are: do not add features that weaken frictionless one-device use; any change to scope must be explicitly logged as an assumption and approved only if it increases the chance the user plays and shares; if a proposed design or feature reduces social share likelihood or increases startup friction, it is rejected; if a requirement and implementation choice conflict, preserve the requirement and record the tradeoff in the phase log; and if any requirement is unmet (especially share loop, offline behavior, or buildability), the phase cannot move to “done” as production-ready. Biggest risk is that we build a delightful game but weak social output; the single non-negotiable fix is to force the recap card and sharing moment to be emotionally good, easy, and worthy of posting without asking. The final output must preserve this: Campfire’s value is not prompts alone, it is the ritual of “we played, we voted, now this card proves it and keeps the party loop alive.”

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Preserving the original prompt as the record of truth before I poke holes in it: the full text is captured verbatim in initial_prompt.md and reproduced in this phase's context above — the portfolio wrapper (5 separate mass-market apps, viral loop requirement, uniqueness rule against ~50 named prior apps, parallel build with per-app phases, "never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project exists") plus the Campfire-specific spec (pass-and-play icebreaker game, 3-4 modes, curated prompt packs, recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall via StoreKit, local-first with no accounts/networking for the core loop, invalidation criterion being "recap cards generated but never shared"). Nothing below overrides that text; it's the thing every later phase gets checked against.

Now treating it as a contract instead of inspiration, because the prompt is full of soft language that will get quietly reinterpreted under time pressure otherwise:

Hard requirements I'd hold everyone to: this is a single-app build (campfire only — the portfolio-of-5 framing is context, not a task for this workspace), local-first with zero account/network dependency for the entire core loop, 3-4 game modes each backed by real curated prompt text (not three placeholder strings and a TODO), a full pass-and-play flow (mode select → player setup → rounds → tally → recap card), a recap card that actually renders as an image/shareable artifact via ShareLink, and a StoreKit paywall gating pack unlocks that is wired to real StoreKit 2 entitlement checks, not a boolean toggle pretending to be one. Haptics tuned per mode and airplane-mode-proof operation are explicit acceptance criteria, not nice-to-haves — someone should be able to turn on airplane mode and run a full session with zero degradation.

The non-goal I want on record so nobody "improves" the scope mid-build: no multiplayer networking, no accounts, no server-hosted pack downloads, no LLM-generated prompts, no AR/ML. The prompt explicitly excludes LLM prompt generation as breaking the offline promise — that's a guardrail against a very tempting shortcut (it would be far easier to have an agent generate 200 prompts at build time than to hand-curate genuinely funny, appropriate, tested ones), so I want it named now: if a later phase writes prompts by having a model spit out a list without editorial pass for tone/awkwardness/repetition, that's a silent violation of "genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text," even though no line of code looks wrong.

Where I'll be the skeptic: the biggest single risk in this spec isn't technical, it's content. The prompt says outright "prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product" — and yet nothing in the phase playbook forces anyone to actually read the prompt packs for quality before calling the app done. A build can compile, ShareLink can fire, StoreKit sandbox can validate, and the product can still be dead on arrival because the jokes aren't funny or a "family-friendly" pack contains something that reads as inappropriate at a work happy hour. I want a decision rule that production-readiness explicitly includes a manual content-quality pass over every prompt pack (not just a count of prompts), because compiling and being good are unrelated claims here.

Second-order risk: pass-and-play with "oversized tap targets" and "confetti-style haptic-synced reveals" is a UI-heavy, state-machine-heavy app disguised as a simple game — round state, current-player tracking, tally logic per mode, and recap-card generation are all places where an off-by-one or a stale index shows up as "wrong person's prompt" or "recap card cites the wrong winner," which is exactly the kind of bug that looks fine in a screenshot and wrong in actual play. I want the later build/verify phases to actually play multiple full sessions across all modes, including edge cases: 2 players (minimum viable group), a double-digit player count, a player backing out mid-round, app backgrounded mid-pass (does state survive?), and a session with zero rounds completed (does recap generation degrade gracefully instead of crashing on an empty tally).

Third: the paywall is a place people love to fake. "Wired to StoreKit" must mean it's testable in a StoreKit configuration file / sandbox, with real free-vs-locked pack gating behavior, restore-purchases handled, and a defined behavior for what a free user sees when tapping a locked pack (paywall sheet, not a silent no-op or a crash). If StoreKit can't be fully exercised without a live App Store Connect product, that limitation must be stated explicitly in the final report, not hidden behind "IAP implemented."

Fourth, on the invalidation criterion itself: the prompt gives us a built-in kill signal — "if recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing." That's not something this MVP build can measure (no analytics, no accounts, local-first) — so I want it on record now that this criterion is aspirational/post-launch, not something this phase can validate, and nobody should later claim "the viral loop is working" as a completion criterion when there's no instrumentation to back that claim. Production-readiness for this phase means "the share flow works and produces a card compelling enough that a reasonable person would want to send it," evaluated by inspection, not by a metric we don't have.

Decision rule I want carried forward: if a later phase has to choose between shipping 3 well-made, fully-tested modes with genuinely good prompt packs versus 4 thinner modes to hit the "3-4" number, cut to 3 — the spec's own risk section says content quality is everything and a smaller finished app beats a larger half-built one per the global rules. And if any ambiguity comes up (e.g., exactly which 3-4 modes, exact pack names/count), the phase should name the assumption explicitly in its output rather than silently picking something, so it's auditable later — this is a QA discipline point, not a style preference: silent assumptions are exactly what causes "why does the recap card assume you own the spicy pack" bugs to surface at the end instead of the middle.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Reading through both takes, they're not actually in tension — Codex laid out the contract in broad strokes (local-first, zero-account, 3-4 modes with real prompt packs, full pass-and-play loop, recap card + ShareLink, StoreKit paywall, haptics/motion, accessibility and state coverage, buildable Xcode project) and I spent my round sharpening the same contract with the specific failure modes that tend to sneak past a "looks done" build: unedited/AI-dumped prompt packs pretending to be curated, state bugs in the pass-and-play round machine (wrong player, stale tally, recap citing the wrong winner), a paywall that's a boolean pretending to be StoreKit, and the fact that the prompt's own invalidation criterion (cards generated but never shared) isn't something this local-first MVP can instrument — so nobody gets to claim "the viral loop works" as a completion criterion later, only "the share flow works and the card is genuinely good."

Nobody pushed back on any of that, and both of us landed on the same non-goals (no networking, no accounts, no LLM/AR/ML, no multi-device dependency) and the same tie-breaker rule: if time forces a choice between 4 thin modes and 3 well-made ones, cut to 3. This reads as real convergence, not premature agreement — the two rounds are complementary, not duplicative.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Original prompt**: preserved verbatim in `initial_prompt.md` and reproduced in full above — the Campfire spec (pass-and-play icebreaker game, 3-4 modes, curated prompt packs, recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall via StoreKit, fully local/offline core loop) plus the parent portfolio wrapper (context only — this workspace builds the `campfire` app, the "5 apps" framing does not add tasks here).

**Hard requirements**:
- Single-app, local-first SwiftUI build: zero accounts, zero networking, zero other installs required for the core loop, fully airplane-mode-proof.
- 3-4 game modes, each backed by real, editorially-reviewed prompt content — not placeholder text and not an unedited raw list.
- Complete pass-and-play flow: mode select → player setup (count/names) → rounds with prompts → per-round vote/answer tally → end-of-session recap.
- Recap card that renders as an actual shareable artifact via `ShareLink`, good enough that a reasonable person would want to send it.
- Campfire+ paywall wired to real StoreKit 2 entitlement checks (testable via StoreKit configuration/sandbox), gating pack unlocks, custom prompts, and unlimited saved recaps — with restore-purchases handled and a defined (non-crashing, non-silent) behavior when a free user taps a locked pack.
- Haptics tuned per game mode; playful spring/bounce motion and a confetti-style reveal at round end.
- Full state coverage: empty, loading, success, and error states wherever reachable; accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, Dynamic Type, WCAG AA contrast); robust local persistence for packs and session history.
- A real, compiling Xcode project with full source in `app_build` — no app is "done" without this.

**Non-goals**: no core networking or multi-device sync in v1, no accounts/identity, no server-hosted pack downloads, no LLM-generated prompts (explicitly excluded — a model dumping prompts without an editorial pass for tone/repetition/appropriateness is a silent violation of this even if no code looks wrong), no AR/ML, no generic/empty-copy recap card, no half-built placeholder screens.

**Production-readiness definition**: compiles and runs deterministically on-device under iOS 17+ with actor-safe state management; a full session (all modes) can be played start to finish in airplane mode with no degradation; recap card generation and the share sheet actually work; StoreKit paywall behavior is real and testable in sandbox (any inability to exercise it against a live App Store Connect product must be stated explicitly, not hidden behind "IAP implemented"); prompt packs have had a manual content-quality pass, not just a line count; round-state logic has been exercised across edge cases (2-player minimum, large player count, mid-round backgrounding, zero-completed-round recap) without producing wrong-player or wrong-winner bugs.

**Decision rules for later phases**:
- Any scope change must be logged as an explicit named assumption, never silently substituted.
- If a choice reduces share likelihood or adds startup friction, reject it.
- If 4 modes can't all be done well in the time available, cut to 3 finished modes over 4 thin ones.
- The prompt's own invalidation criterion ("cards generated but never shared") is aspirational/post-launch and unmeasurable by this local-first MVP with no instrumentation — no later phase may claim "the viral loop is validated"; the bar this build can actually meet is "the share flow works and the card is worth sending."
- If a requirement and an implementation shortcut conflict, the requirement wins and the tradeoff gets recorded in the phase log.
- The app cannot be marked done as production-ready if the share loop, offline behavior, or buildability is unmet.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This output meets the bar. It preserves the original prompt (verbatim reference plus full reproduction), correctly scopes the "5 apps" portfolio framing as context-only rather than an in-scope task for this single-app child phase, and covers all five required elements (prompt reference, hard requirements, non-goals, production-readiness definition, decision rules) concretely enough that a later phase can self-check against it.

Strengths worth preserving into later phases:
- The non-goal on LLM-generated prompts is stated with its actual failure mode (a model dumping an unedited list still violates "genuinely well-written, tested" even if no code looks wrong) rather than as a bare restriction — this is exactly the kind of guardrail that prevents a plausible-looking shortcut.
- Production-readiness is behavioral (airplane-mode full session, StoreKit sandbox testability with explicit disclosure if it can't hit a live ASC product, edge-case round-state coverage) rather than a checklist of claims.
- The invalidation-criterion handling is the sharpest piece of this phase: it correctly identifies that "cards generated but never shared" is unmeasurable by an uninstrumented local-first MVP, and converts it into an achievable bar ("share flow works and card is worth sending") instead of letting a later phase falsely claim the viral loop is validated.
- The mode-count tie-breaker (3 finished over 4 thin) and the "log scope changes as named assumptions" rule give later phases an actual decision procedure, not just aspirational language.

No repair needed. Carry the StoreKit-sandbox-disclosure rule and the invalidation-criterion framing forward verbatim into the build/verify phases so they aren't softened under time pressure.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Reading through both takes, they're not actually in tension — Codex laid out the contract in broad strokes (local-first, zero-account, 3-4 modes with real prompt packs, full pass-and-play loop, recap card + ShareLink, StoreKit paywall, haptics/motion, accessibility and state coverage, buildable Xcode project) and I spent my round sharpening the same contract with the specific failure modes that tend to sneak past a "looks done" build: unedited/AI-dumped prompt packs pretending to be curated, state bugs in the pass-and-play round machine (wrong player, stale tally, recap citing the wrong winner), a paywall that's a boolean pretending to be StoreKit, and the fact that the prompt's own invalidation criterion (cards generated but never shared) isn't something this local-first MVP can instrument — so nobody gets to claim "the viral loop works" as a completion criterion later, only "the share flow works and the card is genuinely good."

Nobody pushed back on any of that, and both of us landed on the same non-goals (no networking, no accounts, no LLM/AR/ML, no multi-device dependency) and the same tie-breaker rule: if time forces a choice between 4 thin modes and 3 well-made ones, cut to 3. This reads as real convergence, not premature agreement — the two rounds are complementary, not duplicative.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Original prompt**: preserved verbatim in `initial_prompt.md` and reproduced in full above — the Campfire spec (pass-and-play icebreaker game, 3-4 modes, curated prompt packs, recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall via StoreKit, fully local/offline core loop) plus the parent portfolio wrapper (context only — this workspace builds the `campfire` app, the "5 apps" framing does not add tasks here).

**Hard requirements**:

- Complete pass-and-play flow: mode select → player setup (count/names) → rounds with prompts → per-round vote/answer tally → end-of-session recap.
- Recap card that renders as an actual shareable artifact via `ShareLink`, good enough that a reasonable person would want to send it.
- Campfire+ paywall wired to real StoreKit 2 entitlement checks (testable via StoreKit configuration/sandbox), gating pack unlocks, custom prompts, and unlimited saved recaps — with restore-purchases handled and a defined (non-crashing, non-silent) behavior when a free user taps a locked pack.

- Full state coverage: empty, loading, success, and error states wherever reachable; accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, Dynamic Type, WCAG AA contrast); robust local persistence for packs and session history.
- A real, compiling Xcode project with full source in `app_build` — no app is "done" without this.

**Non-goals**: no core networking or multi-device sync in v1, no accounts/identity, no server-hosted pack downloads, no LLM-generated prompts (explicitly excluded — a model dumping prompts without an editorial pass for tone/repetition/appropriateness is a silent violation of this even if no code looks wrong), no AR/ML, no generic/empty-copy recap card, no half-built placeholder screens.

**Production-readiness definition**: compiles and runs deterministically on-device under iOS 17+ with actor-safe state management; a full session (all modes) can be played start to finish in airplane mode with no degradation; recap card generation and the share sheet actually work; StoreKit paywall behavior is real and testable in sandbox (any inability to exercise it against a live App Store Connect product must be stated explicitly, not hidden behind "IAP implemented"); prompt packs have had a manual content-quality pass, not just a line count; round-state logic has been exercised across edge cases (2-player minimum, large player count, mid-round backgrounding, zero-completed-round recap) without producing wrong-player or wrong-winner bugs.

**Decision rules for later phases**:
- Any scope change must be logged as an explicit named assumption, never silently substituted.

title: Product Research
output:
Looking at this round, Codex and Claude are building the same picture from different angles rather than disagreeing — Codex framed the product as a "party object" (not a game menu), named the IA flow, and flagged prompt quality/turn ownership/generic recap card as the top risks; Claude filled in the specifics that make those risks actionable: the real competitor to study is Heads Up! (not Jackbox, which is multi-device), the cautionary comps are the glut of cheap "Most Likely To" list apps, and two concrete platform gotchas (`isIdleTimerDisabled` during rounds, verifying `ImageRenderer` output on a physical device, not just simulator) that would otherwise surface as embarrassing bugs late. Both land on the same tie-breaker already locked from the prior phase — three well-made mechanics beat four thin ones — and Claude went further to actually name the three: a voting/ranking mode, a reveal/confession mode, and a rapid binary-choice mode, explicitly distinguishing "modes" (mechanics) from "packs" (content flavors like spicy/family-friendly) so nobody conflates them mid-build. Nobody pushed back on that split.

The one open thread rather than a disagreement: Claude's specific three-mechanic proposal hasn't been explicitly co-signed by Codex yet, just implicitly consistent with Codex's own risk framing. That's a small confirmation gap, not a fight — I'll fold it into the output as the working decision since it satisfies both agents' stated constraints and nothing in the transcript contradicts it.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Audience and use context**: A roomful of people with one phone, not "app users" — holiday gatherings with mixed generations, first dates, new roommates, work happy hours, friend pregames. The defining trait is mixed comfort levels in one room, which is why the free tier must include at least one pack that's safe to pull out in front of anyone (grandma at Thanksgiving shouldn't get a spicy prompt from the free rotation).

**Comparable apps/patterns**: Heads Up! is the closest real comp — single device, pass-and-play, packs as the monetization unit, and the bar to study for tap-target sizing and cold-launch-to-play speed. Jackbox/Kahoot are useful for reveal-mechanic inspiration but are multi-device/networked, so their turn model doesn't transfer directly. The cautionary comp is the App Store glut of "Never Have I Ever"/"Most Likely To" list apps — mostly scrollable strings behind a paywall, low production value — which sets a low bar to clear but zero room to look like one of them. Paired is worth a glance for how a cheap-subscription prompt product frames its paywall moment.

**Platform-specific opportunities**: `isIdleTimerDisabled = true` during an active round (someone reading a prompt aloud for 30-45s will otherwise hit auto-lock — a first-session-breaking bug). Recap card rendering via `ImageRenderer` must be verified on a physical device, not just simulator, since gradient/SF-Symbol-heavy views have known blank/clipped-output edge cases. Contrast-safe design tokens are needed despite the saturated palette so accessibility isn't bolted on. Full airplane-mode determinism and instant cold-launch are treated as UX requirements, not just technical checkboxes.

**Major assumptions and risks** (named explicitly, to carry forward):
1. "Modes" are distinct game mechanics, not content-pack flavors — the three locked in for v1 are a voting/ranking mode, a reveal/confession mode, and a rapid-fire binary-choice mode, each of which can draw from any content pack. This is a named assumption, not yet re-confirmed word-for-word by both agents, but nothing contradicts it.
2. Turn-state correctness (current player, and for modes needing a distinct target player, non-repeating pairing logic) is a single-source-of-truth problem that must be tested at the 2-player minimum and at a double-digit player count — this is exactly the kind of bug that looks fine in a screenshot and wrong in actual play.
3. Recap card quality is the viral-loop lever; a generic card kills the whole "card as invite to next time" premise.
4. Monthly-first pricing framing ($2.99/mo default) may mismatch occasion-based usage patterns and invite fast-cancel behavior — worth anchoring toward annual or "unlock this pack" framing when the paywall UX gets built, though not a blocker for this phase.
5. No ads, ever, anywhere — implied by the offline-proof/no-download promise (an ad SDK would either need network or fail silently offline) and should be locked as an explicit non-goal now.

**Implications for the first build**: Information architecture should stay one level deep — mode browser → player setup (count + names) → active round cards → reveal/tally → recap — with no extra navigation stack. Invest in a strict, contrast-safe visual system before feature breadth. Build exactly the three named mechanics well rather than four thin ones, each backed by real curated content, with turn-state logic as a first-class engineering concern, not an afterthought.

# campfire — Product Research

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:14:44._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Before product debate begins, infer the likely market, audience, competitors/patterns, platform expectations, risks, and native iOS opportunities from the prompt. Label assumptions clearly and turn the research into implications for the first build.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

The first product decision is to treat this as a “party object” not a game menu: the app should feel like a single physical card deck you pass around, because the whole value proposition is reducing setup friction in group moments. Best direction is a frictionless loop: pick mode in <5 seconds, set names, then one giant prompt card per round with clear “next player” transitions and a strong wrap-up card that’s obviously worth sharing. That keeps the viral loop alive through social proof.

Audience is broadly “roomful of people with phones nearby” more than “app users”: families at holidays, dates, roommates meeting, office teams between activities, high-school or college friend groups, and people age ~13+ with iPhone access. This is not a niche personality quiz; it is a social ritual enhancer, so defaults must be warm, quick, and obvious even to someone who just opened the app for the first time ten seconds ago.

Primary competitors/patterns are Jackbox Party Pack (low-friction social reveal mechanics but usually multi-device), Jackbox/House party style prompt apps, Kahoot (timer, host/players split expectations but usually networked), Cards Against prompts, and generic icebreaker cards in chat apps. The gap to win is the one-device physical handoff model + shareable end card + no setup burden. If it feels like “another prompt app,” it loses; if it feels like a polished social prop, it wins.

Platform expectations on iPhone are strict here: iOS users in party contexts punish friction. So screen states must be legible under movement and dim light, button targets must be oversized, and mode transitions need immediate feedback. Haptics + subtle bounce is good, but avoid anything that feels like gamepad lag; motion should confirm action, not delay it. Accessibility has to be intentional, not bolted-on: large labels, high contrast despite saturated theme, Dynamic Type legibility for spoken prompts, and VoiceOver flows that still make sense when one user is holding the phone for another person. Because it’s offline-first, startup speed and deterministic behavior in airplane mode are part of the UX contract.

Big risks/unknowns that should be front-loaded as explicit assumptions:  
- Prompt quality is the core product moat, not the scoring logic. Without hand-curated, tone-appropriate packs, it becomes “just another icebreaker list” and retention dies after one awkward use.  
- One-screen-at-a-time pass-and-play UX can still fail if turn ownership is ambiguous; if players don’t know who is up now, social momentum breaks immediately.  
- “Viral loop” can underperform if recap card looks generic. A beautiful card with context (mode, winners, best answer, player count, time, custom pack tag) and instant sharing cues is the critical difference between a memory artifact and throwaway screenshot.

Assumption set that must be explicit: we should cap initial scope to three strong modes over four weak modes if quality suffers; only ship modes with stable answer/vote semantics; no account/network required even for history; local persistence can be simple and local from day one; StoreKit paywall must block real content paths, not just cosmetic UI. Also assume a single recap format for v1; do not overfit mode-specific card templates until flow proves.

For information architecture, one-screen depth is healthier than nested setup: top-level mode browser → player setup (count + names) → active round cards → reveal/tally → round-by-round summary chip → final recap with one tap to share and one tap to “save + play again” in same session. Any extra navigation stack is friction and should be avoided.

Design implication for first build: invest in a strict visual system before feature breadth. Use the campfire-to-pink gradient and rounded slab type, but enforce contrast-safe tokens and reusable large components; do not let motion/color become inaccessible noise. The first pass should feel like a party object: bright, tactile, predictable, and shareable.

Highest-stakes unknown for this phase is not technical debt, it is social behavior: does a casual user feel comfortable passing the phone and pressing a few large buttons for ten minutes with a group watching. If we don’t validate that flow mentally and in edge-case states (2 players, 10+ players, mid-session interruption, zero completed rounds), we risk shipping a pretty but socially broken app.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Audience is clearer than most of the packs in the spec suggest: this gets opened at the exact moment a room goes quiet — pregame before a party, dinner table lull, first date, new roommates, holiday gathering with mixed generations in the room, work happy hour. That "mixed generations/mixed comfort levels in one room" detail matters more than the spec treats it — it's why the family/spicy/work-safe/couples pack split exists at all, and it means the free tier needs at least one pack that's safe to pull out in front of anyone, or the zero-friction promise dies the first time someone opens it at Thanksgiving and gets a spicy prompt.

The closest real comp is Heads Up! — single device, pass-and-play, category packs as the monetization unit, dead-simple UI, huge in exactly this "party prop, not a utility" space. That's the bar to study for tap-target sizing and how fast you get from cold launch to "someone's holding the phone playing." The cautionary comps are the App Store's current glut of "Never Have I Ever" / "Most Likely To" list apps — most are a scrollable list of strings behind a paywall, often ad-supported, low production value. That's actually good news for differentiation (the bar is low) but bad news for anyone tempted to cut corners on content, because "we're better than a free list-app" is the entire pitch and there's zero room to ship something that reads the same as the bottom of that pile. Paired is worth a glance for how a cheap-subscription prompt-pack product frames its paywall moment, even though it's asynchronous, not pass-and-play.

Two platform-native things I want on record before build starts because they're easy to forget and both would break the demo: the app needs `isIdleTimerDisabled = true` while a round is active, since someone reading a prompt out loud for 30-45 seconds on a phone with a normal auto-lock will hit a black screen mid-round — that's a first-session-breaking bug, not a polish item. And the recap card has to be verified as an actual rendered image on a physical device, not just in Xcode preview — `ImageRenderer` on gradient-heavy, SF-Symbol-laden views has known edge cases (blank or clipped output) that don't always show up in simulator. Both of these are cheap to get right and expensive to discover after the app is "done."

The risk I most want named as an explicit assumption before anyone starts building: the spec says "3-4 game modes" and separately lists "family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples" as pack types, and it's easy to accidentally collapse those into the same thing. I'm reading modes as distinct game mechanics (e.g., a ranking/voting mode, a reveal/confession mode, a rapid-fire this-or-that mode) each of which can draw prompts from any of the content packs — not "spicy" itself being a mode. If that distinction gets blurred, you end up with either four content flavors wrapped around one thin mechanic, or four mechanics each starved for content, and either failure looks fine in a screenshot. This needs to be locked as a named assumption now, not discovered during build.

Second concrete risk: pass-and-play turn state is exactly the kind of thing that looks trivial and isn't. A mode like "who's most likely to..." needs a target player distinct from whoever's answering — with 2 players that's forced, with 5+ it needs deliberate non-repeating pairing logic, not "pick random and hope it's not the same person twice in a row." A single source of truth for current player/turn order, tested at the 2-player boundary and at a double-digit count, needs to be a build-time discipline, not a demo-day fix.

Third risk, and it's a product-economics one rather than a code one so it belongs in this phase's notes for whoever builds the paywall UX: this is occasion-based, not daily-habit, usage — someone downloads it the night of a party and might not open it again for three weeks. A monthly subscription default is a mismatch for that usage pattern and invites fast cancel/refund behavior; the paywall moment should probably anchor on annual or frame around "unlock this pack" rather than lead with $2.99/mo. Not something to fix in this phase, but if nobody writes it down now it'll get built exactly as literally specified and nobody will ask the pricing-anchor question until App Review.

One more assumption worth stating explicitly since it's implied but never said outright: no ads, anywhere, ever, in this app. It's implied by "no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around" positioning and by the airplane-mode-proof requirement — an ad SDK either needs network (breaking offline-proof) or fails/hangs silently offline (breaking the "just works" promise), and it'd violate the standing no-third-party-package-unless-it-reduces-risk rule. I'd rather this be a named non-goal now than have someone propose it later as a free-tier revenue lever.

For the first build, given the tie-breaker already locked in (3 finished over 4 thin), I'd commit now to picking exactly which three mechanics so nobody re-litigates it mid-build: one voting/ranking mode, one reveal/confession mode, one fast binary-choice mode. That combination covers meaningfully different pass-and-play rhythms (slow deliberation vs. quick reflex vs. group-judgment) rather than three variations on the same "read a card, move on" loop, which is what'll make the recap card interesting to look at across a full session instead of one mode's data dominating it.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at this round, Codex and Claude are building the same picture from different angles rather than disagreeing — Codex framed the product as a "party object" (not a game menu), named the IA flow, and flagged prompt quality/turn ownership/generic recap card as the top risks; Claude filled in the specifics that make those risks actionable: the real competitor to study is Heads Up! # campfire — Portfolio Selection

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:16:40._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

If the prompt requests multiple apps, force a real independent selection process: one app per requested category or requested count, with no collapsed wrapper project. Produce the required portfolio-json manifest for sibling child projects. If the prompt is a single app, explicitly record that no portfolio split is needed.

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Campfire turns one phone into a shared party host — pick a mode, pass it around, and everyone leaves with a recap card worth sending to the group chat.

**Primary user and scenario:** A mixed group of 2-15 people sharing one phone in a mildly awkward social moment — family holiday, first date, new roommates, work happy hour, a pregame — where whoever opened the app has to get everyone playing within about 10 seconds with zero explanation needed beyond what's on screen.

**Core loop:** Launch → mode picker (3 mechanics — voting/ranking, reveal/confession, rapid binary-choice — each with a one-line description and visible free/locked pack indicator) → player setup (count stepper + names, 2 minimum, soft cap ~12-15) → round sequence (one full-bleed prompt card per turn, unambiguous "who's up" indicator, input/vote/reveal per mode, haptic-confetti tally beat) → next round or end trigger → recap card (session stats, rendered shareable image) → replay or exit. - No accounts, no networking/sync in the core loop, no LLM-generated prompts, no AR/ML, no ads.

- Cold launch to an active round in under 10 seconds with no instructions read.
- A full 8-round session spanning all three modes completes with the app backgrounded at least once and zero state corruption.
