## Phase Purpose

Define the smallest valuable slice / immediate next steps that de-risk and validate the concept with minimal effort.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

I’d lock the slice to one complete loop built with production discipline and leave expansion to later: ship one fully playable mechanic (rapid binary-choice) now, one complete local session flow, and prove the paywall/recap/share loop end-to-end. That single slice is enough to validate the party-object promise without risking a half-built state machine and still gives a real emotional payoff when someone gets to the share card in one sitting. Biggest objection to this choice is that it delays two other mechanics, but that is a controlled delay because the costliest failure right now is logic correctness; getting one mechanic 100% right (turn ownership, tallying, persistence, recap) lowers risk for the later modes.

The smallest slice is: launch app → mode picker with 3 mode options visible (only rapid-fire active, other two locked with explanatory lock badges) → player setup (2–15, names required) → round loop using local prompts from the free family-safe pack → per-turn tally and reveal with haptic + micro-bounce/confetti state → end-of-session recap card rendered from real data → ShareLink flow works → returning to home and starting over. This has to include true local persistence every state transition (prompt index, current player, vote totals, session status), plus a real handling branch for app backgrounding and foregrounding during a round.

Must-have interactions for this phase are: a visible “who’s up” control so the phone handoff is unambiguous; a deterministic prompt card render path; an explicit error/empty state for missing prompts; a blocked action path for locked pack access that never silently fails (show CTA + restore option); and a recap card with real totals that doesn’t crash when one player declines to answer and still summarizes cleanly. I want to preserve one hard QA rule in the next phase output: for pass-and-play correctness, the app must never produce an ambiguous current player or stale tally, including when backgrounded mid-round.

Cut list is exact and non-negotiable for this slice: no custom prompt creation, no custom pack download flow, no multi-device mode, no StoreKit configuration beyond entitlement-checked paywall entry point and restore path, no mode-specific recap templates, no mid-session player edits, no settings screen except an essential haptics toggle, no achievements, no history list UI beyond saving the last recap for current session share. That is not a feature cut forever; it is a deliberate de-risking boundary so the core loop is validated before widening scope.

Validation criteria for consensus: by the end of this slice, a first-time user can run a 2-player and a 10-player game to completion in airplane mode with no network/account/login, with the phone interrupted at least once and still resume with correct state; the summary card is generated from persisted session data and can be shared via ShareLink; tapping any locked pack route reliably shows the non-blocking paywall path (with restore) and never traps the user without action; and there are no wrong-player or wrong-tally outcomes across 2-player and 10-player runs in this slice’s mechanic. If this fails, we stop and fix state-first before adding more mechanics.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

Here's my independent take on this phase.

The smallest slice that actually proves this concept isn't "build a thin version of all three mechanics" — it's build exactly one mechanic, completely, end to end, and make it good enough that a real group would send the resulting card unprompted. I'd pick reveal/confession ("who's most likely to...") as that one mechanic, not voting/ranking or rapid binary-choice, because it's the only one of the three that produces a specific, personal, embarrassing-in-a-good-way moment about a specific person in the room — that's the shape of thing people actually scroatch a photo of and text to the group chat. Voting/ranking and rapid binary-choice are both fine mechanics but neither one naturally produces "oh my god you have to see what the app said about Dave" in the way a reveal moment does. If I only get one shot at proving the core promise — pass a phone, something happens, everyone leaves with something worth sharing — that's the one to nail first and hardest.

Concretely, the slice is: launch straight into player setup (skip mode-picker chrome for this checkpoint — one mechanic, no menu needed yet), 2-15 players with a name entry that doesn't feel like a form, a sequence of full-bleed reveal prompts pulling from the one free "safe anywhere" pack, an unambiguous target-player reveal moment with a haptic-confetti beat, running tally of who's been "most" of what, and ending in a recap card that's actually built from that session's real data — specific names, specific tallies, specific standout moment — rendered and pushed through ShareLink on a physical device. That's one complete, emotionally satisfying loop, not a tech demo of a state machine.

The must-have interactions inside that slice: the "who's up" state has to be readable from across a table, not just technically present in the view hierarchy; the reveal itself needs the haptic and motion payoff since that's the entire dopamine hit of this mechanic; and the recap card needs at least one line that could only have been generated by this specific session, or the whole slice fails its own point. Turn/target-player logic has to already be built as a real non-repeating engine at this stage, tested at 2 players and at 12+, because retrofitting that logic once mode 2 and 3 get bolted on is far more expensive than building it right the first time against real data rather than a stub.

What I'd cut from this first slice, explicitly: the other two mechanics, the mode-picker screen entirely, the paywall/StoreKit gating, custom prompt creation, saved recap history beyond the current session, and any settings surface. None of that gets deleted from the eventual v1 — the three-mechanic, paywalled release is still the release bar locked in every prior phase — this is purely about which piece gets built and emotionally validated first so that if the core "pass phone → moment happens → card gets shared" loop doesn't actually land, we find that out before sinking time into two more mechanics and a StoreKit integration built on top of a shaky foundation.

The risk in my own proposal: if I design the round/turn engine too narrowly around reveal/confession's specific data shape (a prompt plus a target player), it might not generalize cleanly when voting/ranking and rapid binary-choice get added, and I'd have to be disciplined about building the underlying session/round model generically even though only one mechanic's UI ships in this checkpoint — otherwise "smallest slice" quietly becomes "throwaway prototype," which isn't what this phase is asking for.

Validation criteria I'd hold this slice to: a full 6-8 round reveal-mode session, played by an actual small group in person if at all possible, at both a 2-player and a 10+-player table, that ends with someone unprompted saying some version of "wait, send me that" about the recap card — that's the real bar, not "the ShareLink API call didn't crash." Second criterion: the session survives the phone being locked or backgrounded mid-round without losing track of whose turn it is or what's been revealed so far. If either of those fails, nothing else about this app matters yet.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things actually stand: both Codex and Claude agree on the big-picture strategy — build exactly one game mechanic all the way through (not a thin slice of all three), make the round/turn engine generic enough to support the other two mechanics later even though only one ships now, and validate on a real durable session that survives backgrounding at both a 2-player and a large-group table. Neither wants to touch StoreKit-heavy paywall building or custom prompts or saved history yet. That's solid common ground.

But they picked two different mechanics, and haven't reconciled it. Codex wants rapid binary-choice first, arguing it's the lowest-risk one to get the state machine bulletproof on before adding complexity. Claude wants reveal/confession first, arguing it's the only one of the three that produces the "wait, send me that" personal moment the whole recap-card/viral premise depends on — a rapid binary-choice tally is much less likely to produce something a person actually wants to share. That's a real disagreement about which mechanic best proves the concept, not just a style difference.

They also disagree on how much surrounding shell to include: Codex wants the mode-picker screen visible in this slice (with the other two mechanics shown as locked) plus a real (if minimal) StoreKit-gated locked-pack path with restore, treating "never trap the user without an action" as a hard QA rule now. Claude wants to skip the mode-picker chrome entirely and skip StoreKit/paywall completely for this checkpoint, treating the whole point as isolating and emotionally validating the single mechanic before spending effort on paywall plumbing.

Next round needs to resolve both: which mechanic anchors the MVP slice, and whether the paywall entry point / locked-pack CTA is in-scope for this smallest slice or deferred to the next one.

### Round 2


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

No, I don’t think we should defer both the paywall and the mode pick entirely yet. I’m siding with Claude on the mechanic (reveal/confession) but disagreeing with the part that wants to hide the rest of the product. If we ship a slice that only proves “a card can be shared,” but never touches the lock/upgrade path, we’ll get a false sense of completion and then have to make a second architectural pass later to splice it in. That is exactly the kind of thing that causes wrong-screen/ dead-end behavior in v1.

Reveal/confession is the right first mechanic because it stress-tests the hardest state transitions: selecting a target player who isn’t the current speaker, enforcing non-repeating targets in small windows, and producing a recap line that actually matters (“Alex was most likely to…”) instead of generic stats. Binary-choice might be easier to brute force, but it won’t smoke out the same edge cases, and it gives weaker social proof for the share card. Codex, I get why you wanted the lower-risk path, but this is a local-first party app—the real user risk is social “wow” failure, not just code execution. If the one finished mechanic can’t create a memorable moment people want to forward, the viral premise is already busted.

Concretely, the smallest slice should be: launch to a compact mode entry that shows three cards, one active reveal mode and two disabled with clear lock chips; player setup (2–15 + names); reveal round loop for that mode only with a hard “who’s up” indicator and one target-selection rule; one round-end reveal + tally + confetti/haptic beat; and an end recap card generated from real session data with working ShareLink. While the session is active, if someone taps a locked pack or a locked paid mode, show the locked state immediately and route to a real entitlement check path with restore support and an explicit non-blocking error state when StoreKit test config is missing. That’s not a full storefront; it’s enough to define behavior and keep this from becoming a UI trap.

The must-have risk controls for this slice should be persistence at every transition, one-source-of-truth for turn/target/round/tally, `isIdleTimerDisabled` during active rounds, deterministic prompt rendering into image, and voiceover-readable pass target semantics. Every failure mode must be visible: no prompts → empty state with a recover action, no entitlement → unlocked preview message + paywall sheet, share generation failure → retry with non-lossy fallback. Everything else is out: no custom prompt creation, no full pack browser, no in-session player edits, no achievements/history dashboard, no multi-device, no mode-specific recap templates.

For me the validation bar is simple and strict: a 2-player and a 12-player session complete without ambiguity in current speaker/targeting, survives backgrounding once mid-round with no stale tally, produces an image-based recap that names correct winners/targets and shares via ShareLink, and hitting any locked route always lands in one recoverable paywall path with restore and a clear error if it can’t complete. This gives us a truly de-risked base and no open decisions for build about “did we even include the paid flow correctly.”

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Codex's argument for rapid binary-choice — "get the state machine bulletproof before adding complexity" — sounds right on its face, but I don't think the risk math actually holds up when I dig into it. The state machine plumbing that needs to be bulletproof — persisting round index, current player, session status, surviving backgrounding — is essentially identical complexity regardless of which mechanic sits on top of it. That part isn't mechanic-dependent at all, it's just "does our SwiftData/persistence layer correctly survive a lifecycle event," and building it against binary-choice doesn't make it any more or less solid than building it against reveal/confession. What is mechanic-dependent is the one genuinely hard algorithmic piece — picking a target player distinct from the current player, with no visible repeats at 2 players and at 12-15 players — and that piece only exists in reveal/confession, not in rapid binary-choice. So if we build binary-choice first, we're not actually de-risking the hardest problem, we're deferring it. Product research flagged that exact non-repeating-pairing logic as one of the two systems-level risks worth locking as a scope boundary, right alongside "recap card quality is the viral lever." Building the mechanic that doesn't touch that risk at all, first, means we validate persistence (real but not the scariest part) and leave the scariest part for slice two, which is backwards from what a de-risking slice is supposed to do.

And on the viral/emotional side, I think Codex is underselling how much the choice of mechanic actually matters to the thing this whole phase is supposed to prove. Picture the actual recap card each mechanic produces after a session. Binary-choice gives you something like "the group chose pizza over tacos 7 out of 10 times" — that's a tally, it's fine, but nobody screenshots a tally and sends it to a group chat unprompted. Reveal/confession gives you "Sarah was voted most likely to end up on a reality show, three separate times" — that's a specific, funny, personal claim about a specific person that people do actually forward. The whole premise locked back in the product brief is that the recap card is the entire growth mechanism and the bar is "worth sending," not "technically renders." If we spend this slice proving out the mechanic least likely to produce a sendable card, we haven't actually tested the premise the app depends on — we've tested plumbing and called it validation.

Where I think Codex is right, and where I want to update my own position: the mode-picker-versus-no-mode-picker question and the locked-pack question are actually two different questions that got merged, and I want to pull them apart rather than just restate my "skip it all" stance. If we do show a picker with the other two mechanics visible, they cannot be shown as "locked, pay to unlock" — that would be a lie, since those mechanics aren't gated by Campfire+ at all, they just don't exist yet in this build checkpoint. Slice 2 is going to ship them as part of the free-tier three-mechanic core, not sell them. Labeling "coming soon" and labeling "premium locked" are different UI treatments and different product claims, and conflating them now means we'd have to walk back a paywall CTA later when those modes turn out to be free. So if a picker shows up at all in this slice, the other two tiles say "coming soon," full stop — no lock icon, no paywall tap target, because there's nothing to unlock.

That said, I'd actually push to cut the picker entirely for this smallest slice, same as my first round, and here's the sharper reason why: Codex's own validation criteria are about proving turn-state correctness and card shareability, and a picker screen doesn't move either of those forward — it's pure UI chrome that has zero bearing on whether the target-player logic holds at 2 and 12-15 players or whether the card gets an unprompted "send me that" reaction. Every screen we build in this slice that isn't in the direct path of proving the risky stuff is time not spent hardening the thing that actually needs hardening. Codex's "never trap the user without an action" QA rule is a good principle and I'll happily keep it — but it belongs in scope for the slice where locked-pack taps are actually reachable, which is the slice where a second pack exists to be locked. Right now there's exactly one free pack; there's nothing behind a paywall yet for a CTA to protect anyone from.

So here's where I land, and where I think the group should land: anchor the smallest slice on reveal/confession, not binary-choice, because it forces the real risk (non-repeating target-player logic) to get solved now instead of deferred, and because it's the mechanic whose output actually tests the viral premise this whole app exists to prove. Skip the mode-picker and skip StoreKit/paywall entirely for this checkpoint — not because they don't matter eventually, they absolutely do and they're locked as v1 requirements — but because neither one touches the two things this slice is actually supposed to prove: does the turn engine hold up under real conditions, and is the resulting card something a real person wants to send. Everything else is slice two's problem.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: NO

Good progress this round — the mechanic fight is actually settled. Codex read Claude's argument (non-repeating target-player logic is the real risk and only reveal/confession exercises it; a binary-choice tally doesn't produce anything a person would forward) and switched sides. Both now agree: reveal/confession is the one mechanic this slice builds, all the way through, on a generic-enough engine to support the other two later.

What's still unresolved is how much shell wraps around that one mechanic. Codex still wants a visible mode-entry screen in this slice (three tiles, one active) plus a real, minimal StoreKit entitlement/paywall path with restore behind any locked route, arguing that skipping it now just means a second architectural pass later and risks a dead-end tap target in v1. Claude still wants to cut the picker and the paywall entirely for this checkpoint, and raised a new point Codex hasn't answered yet: the other two mechanics aren't premium-gated at all — they're just not built yet — so showing them as "locked, pay to unlock" would misrepresent the product (they should say "coming soon" if shown at all, not carry a paywall tap target), and since there's currently only one pack, there's nothing behind a real paywall for a locked-pack CTA to protect anyone from.

Next round needs Codex to respond directly to that "there's nothing to actually gate yet" argument — does a paywall/entitlement stub still earn its place in this slice if the only thing it could plausibly gate is hypothetical, or does that confirm Claude's case that it belongs in slice two instead?

### Round 3


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 3**

Great, I’m going to land this hard because keeping a dead-end `paywall` path in this slice is exactly the wrong kind of risk.

Codex, your instinct about avoiding “looks done, not built” is right, but your current paywall inclusion in the smallest slice is too much scope and, worse, logically incorrect today because nothing except a third-party entitlement signal is actually gateable yet. If we ship a CTA to an entitlement that can only ever be a placeholder in this phase, we create behavior that will almost certainly be tested and fail user trust later when it becomes reality. Claude’s “if it’s not built, call it coming soon, don’t fake-gate it” point is the safer one. Also, your reveal/confession pick is now the right shared choice: it directly exercises the hardest target-selection logic and the viral-share premise.

So the smallest end-to-end slice should be: launch into a single “Reveal” mechanic flow with a no-friction way to start a one-device game, player setup (2–15, names), pass-turn loop with non-repeating target assignment (with explicit current speaker + target UI), round-by-round reveal with haptic beat, session end recap generation from persisted real session data, and one-tap ShareLink from the recap card. No mode picker, no false paywall, no premium pack browsing, no history screen, no settings beyond a simple haptics toggle if you must keep one global control. Make this one workflow completely deterministic so we can prove the model works and the card is worth sharing.

Must-have interactions I would lock in now: oversized “whose turn” + “reveal target” badges so pass-and-play can’t get ambiguous, hard validation on player count and name entry (trim empties, minimum 2), non-repeating target rotation with a guaranteed minimum separation window, auto-lock-screen suppression during active rounds via `isIdleTimerDisabled`, persistence checkpoints before every state transition (round index, current player, target, prompt index, tally), and a physical recap card renderer that has a fallback path if image generation fails. Also: every reachable error path must have recovery, including “no prompts in pack,” “share generation failed,” and “app backgrounded mid-round resumes from saved state.”

Cut list is strict: no StoreKit entitlement flow in this slice, no mode catalog/picker, no pack browser, no locked mechanics, no custom prompt creation, no saved recap history list, no mid-session player edits, no achievements, no daily/notification loops, no settings surface except optional haptics. This is one mechanic, one path, no detours.

For validation, I want hard pass/fail gates: two independent 2–15 player runs complete offline end-to-end after one background/foreground cycle with no ambiguous “who’s up/target” and no stale tally, no repeated target shown in the last 3 turns in a 12-player run, recap card always reflects real persisted data and is generated/shareable via ShareLink, and error states are explicit and recoverable (never blank/lockup). This is the smallest emotionally true proof: if we can’t make one great social moment and a real shareable card from reveal mode, the rest (other modes + paywall + extras) is putting lipstick on a concept that may not land.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 3**

Codex, you're right that I hadn't fully answered the "second architectural pass" worry, so let me actually engage with it instead of just repeating my position. I think there are two very different things bundled under "paywall in this slice," and pulling them apart resolves this cleanly.

One is the architectural seam: does the app's data model and view layer have a concept of "is this content unlocked" that gets checked before rendering something, so that when slice 2 adds a real second pack behind Campfire+, nobody has to go tear apart every screen and retrofit an entitlement check? That seam is cheap — it's a protocol and a stub implementation that currently returns "unlocked" for everything, because everything in this slice is free. I'll concede that fully, and I think it directly answers your worry: build the entitlement-check seam now, wire it into wherever content gets shown, and slice 2 swaps the stub for a real StoreKit-backed implementation without touching the surrounding UI. That's a real de-risking move and it's nearly free.

The other thing is the actual paywall: a StoreKit product configuration, a paywall sheet with real pricing and purchase buttons, a restore-purchases flow, and a locked-pack CTA that routes into all of that. That's not cheap, and here's my actual objection to building it now rather than just "it feels early" — you can't validate your own stated QA rule ("never trap the user without an action") against a route that doesn't exist yet. Right now there is exactly one pack, and it's free. For a locked-pack tap target to exist at all in this slice, we'd have to invent a second pack purely to have something to lock, write throwaway placeholder content for it, and then build a whole paywall UI whose only job is to gate content nobody's actually shipping yet. That's not de-risking the paywall — it's manufacturing a fake scenario to justify building the paywall early, and the actual content behind it will get thrown away or rewritten once the real spicy/work-safe/couples packs exist in a later slice. That's worse than deferring it, it's actively wasteful.

So here's the synthesis I think actually resolves this: build the entitlement-seam (protocol + stub, always-unlocked for now) as part of this slice's architecture, because that's the part where skipping it now genuinely causes a second pass later, exactly like Codex says. But defer the real StoreKit product wiring, the paywall sheet, and the restore flow to the slice where a second, genuinely locked pack exists — because until there's real content to protect, "prove the paywall works" collapses into "prove we can pop a sheet," which tells us nothing we don't already know and burns time that should go toward getting the reveal/confession turn-logic and the recap card right.

On the mode-entry screen, I'm moving further toward Codex's side than I was last round, but for a different reason than the paywall — not because it de-risks the mechanic itself, but because the entry point is also an architectural seam. If we build straight into player setup with zero home screen, we're implicitly deciding the app's root is "start playing," and slotting in two more tiles later means restructuring the entry point, not just adding a row. So I'll agree to a minimal three-tile screen, one active for reveal/confession, two marked "coming soon" — plain text, no lock icon, no tap target, because they're not gated, they just don't exist yet — and that screen is justified purely as the navigational seam for slice 2, not as UI polish for this one.

Where I won't budge: everything else in this slice still has to point at proving the two things that actually matter — does the non-repeating target-player logic hold at 2 and 12-15 players, and does the resulting recap card make a real person want to send it. If we're all agreed on reveal/confession as the mechanic, the entitlement-seam-not-real-paywall split, and a plain "coming soon" entry screen instead of a fake locked/paywall path, I think we actually have a resolved slice here — one that doesn't need a second architectural pass and doesn't waste effort proving something we can't yet prove for real.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

Consensus reached. Both agents landed on reveal/confession as the mechanic, and Codex fully conceded the paywall fight — a real paywall/StoreKit flow in this slice would mean building throwaway gated content just to have something to lock, which is worse than deferring it. Claude then split "paywall" into two pieces that resolves Codex's original worry without the waste: build a cheap architectural seam now (a content-unlocked check, stubbed to always-true) so slice 2 can drop in real StoreKit behind it without restructuring anything, but skip the actual purchase UI, restore flow, and locked-pack CTA until there's a real second pack to protect. On the entry screen, Claude moved toward Codex's side too — not a full mode picker, but a minimal three-tile screen (one active, two labeled "coming soon" with no lock icon or tap target) purely so the app's root doesn't have to be restructured when the other two mechanics ship. That directly serves the "avoid a second architectural pass" concern Codex raised, and nothing in it contradicts anything Codex argued for.

## Final Output

**MVP slice:** One mechanic, reveal/confession, built completely. App opens to a minimal three-tile entry screen (Reveal active; Voting/Ranking and Rapid Choice both shown as plain "coming soon" text, no lock icon, no tap target since nothing is actually gated) → player setup (2–15 players, name entry with validation) → reveal round loop from the single free pack, with an unambiguous current-speaker/target-player UI and non-repeating target assignment → per-round reveal + haptic/confetti tally beat → session-end recap card generated from real persisted session data → ShareLink share sheet. Session state (round index, current player, target, tally) persists at every transition and survives backgrounding/foregrounding mid-round.

**Must-have interactions:** Oversized, unambiguous "who's up" / "who's the target" indicators; hard validation on player count and names; non-repeating target-player rotation with a guaranteed minimum-separation window, provably correct at 2 players and at 12–15 players; `isIdleTimerDisabled` during active rounds; persistence checkpoint before every state transition; recap card renderer with a fallback if image generation fails; a lightweight "content unlocked" check wired into wherever pack content is shown (stubbed to always-unlocked for now) so a real entitlement system can slot in later without touching the surrounding views; explicit, recoverable states for every reachable error (no prompts in pack, share generation failure, resume-from-backgrounding).

**Cut list:** No real StoreKit product/paywall sheet/restore flow (only the always-unlocked stub seam), no locked-pack CTA, no second pack, no custom prompt creation, no saved recap history beyond the current session, no mid-session player roster edits, no achievements, no daily/notification engagement mechanics, no settings beyond an optional haptics toggle, no voting/ranking or rapid-choice mechanic UI (their tiles exist only as "coming soon" placeholders).

**Validation criteria:** A 2-player and a 12–15-player reveal-mode session each complete offline start to finish, including one background/foreground cycle, with zero ambiguous current-speaker/target state and zero stale tally; no target player repeats within the last 3 turns in the large-group run; the recap card is built from real persisted session data (correct names, correct tallies, a specific standout line) and produces a working ShareLink share on a physical device; every named error path shows a recoverable state, never a blank screen or lockup. This is the bar: if reveal/confession can't produce one genuinely shareable moment under these conditions, nothing else in the v1 scope (the other two mechanics, the real paywall) is worth building yet.

### Round 4


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 4**

I’m signing this off as the phase decision: reveal/confession only, with a lightweight “coming soon” shell, and zero fake paywall interactions. We’ve already established the right mechanic choice; the only risk left is overbuilding junk UI to fake completeness. If we add a real lock/paywall path now, it becomes untestable theater because there is no second pack with real entitlement yet, and that is exactly the kind of branch that later gets remembered as “already decided” even when it shouldn’t be. So for this slice: root with three cards, only Reveal is active, the other two are informational placeholders (“Coming soon”). Then setup (2–15, non-empty names, hard trim), reveal loop with visible speaker+target, non-repeating target policy (alternate at 2 players, and minimum separation window at larger groups), round-by-round tally, end recap from real persisted data, and ShareLink.

I want this slice to be judged by behavior, not polish. State machine must checkpoint on every transition (current player, target, prompt index, remaining prompts, scores, turn count), so foreground/background never leaves partial or duplicated state. For non-repeat target logic at 12+ players, treat a 3-turn rolling window as hard requirement; for 2 players, strict alternation only. Keep haptics/motion keyed off confirmed state transitions so double-taps can’t create phantom confetti or double-points. Keep the session non-configurable in this phase (e.g., fixed rounds or end-of-deck), because extra knobs are failure surface. Add explicit recoverable error paths only where reachable: no prompts in pack, image render failure, resume-from-background corruption. If share generation fails, don’t block session completion—show fallback summary and offer retry, not a dead-end.

I also want one architectural seam in place now: a local `ContentAccessProvider` with `isPackUnlocked` used by prompt loading, but stubbed true for all packs. That’s cheap insurance against a later refactor and addresses Codex’s future-maintenance concern without introducing dead-end behavior. Keep everything else out: no StoreKit configuration, no restore flow, no custom prompt creation, no history list beyond current session card, no in-session roster editing, no extra settings. Validation must be strict: offline, two-player plus 12+ session each complete end-to-end with a mid-session backgrounding event; zero ambiguous current speaker/target; no recent target repeats in large group window; recap card is data-correct and shareable. This is the smallest slice that actually proves the product, and it gives us a clean runway for adding two modes + real paywall in the next pass.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 4**

Consensus is real here and I don't have a fight left to pick — Codex's concession on the paywall (building a fake gated pack just to have something to lock is worse than deferring it) and the coordinator's final synthesis both land exactly where the logic actually pointed: reveal/confession as the one mechanic because it's the only one that exercises the hard target-selection risk *and* produces a card worth sending, the entitlement-seam/real-paywall split so nobody has to redo architecture later, and the "coming soon" entry screen as a navigational seam rather than fake UI chrome. Otherwise this is a clean, well-argued consensus and I'm signing off on it as written.

CONSENSUS: YES

CONSENSUS: YES

Both agents fully signed off this round. Codex explicitly locked in the reveal/confession-only slice with the "coming soon" placeholder shell, the `ContentAccessProvider`/`isPackUnlocked` stub seam (always-true for now), and no fake paywall UI — matching exactly what the coordinator's round-3 synthesis proposed, right down to the 2-player strict-alternation / 12+-player 3-turn rolling-window target rule. ## Feedback
This output meets the bar and is ready to close. - **Validation criteria**: hard pass/fail gates at both player-count boundaries, plus the qualitative "would a real person send this card" bar carried forward as an actual stop condition rather than closing rhetoric.

This is consistent with every locked decision from prompt-contract, product-research, initial-discussion, and per-app-product-brief (three mechanics eventually, reveal/confession's target logic flagged as the top systems risk back in product-research, recap card as the sole viral lever, no fake/generic paywall). title: Detailed Discussion
output:
Good round — this one actually closed the gap. Both landed on the same shape for almost everything: two roles only (active player = whoever's holding the phone and advancing rounds, in strict round-robin order; target = who the reveal is about), a hard rule that target can never equal the active player, "survives full termination and relaunch" replacing the weaker background/foreground language (since it's free given persistence checkpointing is already locked), explicit tie-handling copy for the recap card instead of a silent arbitrary winner, a distinct fallback strategy for degenerate small sessions where a "most likely to" superlative would be meaningless, the "names leave the device via the shared image" privacy line written down explicitly, and the `isPackUnlocked` stub flagged as temporary so it can't quietly survive into a real second pack.

The one spot where they proposed two different mechanisms rather than the same one: Codex proposed a staged rule (2p strict alternation, 3p no-consecutive-repeat, 4p 2-turn separation, 5p+ 3-turn separation), while Claude proposed a single unified rule — exclude the active player, exclude whoever was targeted last round, then pick whoever's gone longest without being targeted. Working through it, Claude's version isn't a competing idea, it's a cleaner generalization that produces the exact same behavior Codex wanted at every player count (strict alternation falls out naturally at 2, the forced-single-candidate falls out naturally at 3, and it gets progressively more spread-out at higher counts) without needing per-count special cases or a magic number that breaks below 5 players. Nothing here is actually contested — it's one clean rule replacing four bespoke ones aimed at the same goal.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- Two roles only: **active player** (rotates in strict round-robin order, taps to advance each round) and **target** (who the reveal names). Active player and target can never be the same person — hard rule, enforced before any other selection logic.
- Target selection rule (replaces the earlier "3-turn window" language, which was mathematically impossible below 5 players): exclude the active player, exclude whoever was targeted in the immediately prior round, then choose whoever has gone longest since last being targeted. This single rule is provably correct and deterministic at every player count from 2 to 15 — at 2 players it collapses to strict alternation; at 3 it collapses to one forced legal candidate every round; at higher counts it naturally spreads targets out with no repeats in any practically noticeable window.
- Persistence/reliability bar upgraded from "survives one background/foreground cycle" to **survives full app termination and relaunch mid-round**, with zero duplicated prompts, targets, or tallies. This is not new engineering scope — it's the same per-transition checkpointing already locked, just validated against the harder, more realistic real-world failure mode (people force-quit apps at parties far more than they background them).
- Recap card must have explicit, deterministic behavior for non-obvious outcomes rather than silently picking a winner:
  - Clear single standout: name them directly ("Alex owned the spotlight...").
  - Tie for most-targeted: name all tied players together, never silently break the tie.
  - Degenerate small sessions (e.g. fewer than ~4 distinct targets, which includes all 2-player games): don't manufacture a fake statistical "most likely to" line — switch to surfacing the single funniest/most notable reveal moment from the session instead.
- Privacy position stated explicitly, not left implied: sharing the recap image is an intentional, user-visible data egress — player names leave the device once shared, and that's an accepted tradeoff of the core premise. Locally, no analytics, no networking, no accounts; names/session data live on-device only as long as the session/recap persists.
- `ContentAccessProvider.isPackUnlocked` ships as a stub returning true for everything today, explicitly marked as temporary/placeholder (not a real access policy) so it isn't mistaken later for intentional "everything's free" behavior once a second pack exists.

**Edge cases enumerated:** 2-player strict alternation; 3-player forced single-candidate targeting; duplicate/blank player names; empty prompt pack; rapid double-taps causing phantom scoring or duplicate haptics; app force-terminated mid-round; recap tie for most-targeted; degenerate low-variance sessions with no real statistical standout; share-image render failure on physical device.

**Data and privacy implications:** All player names and session data stay on-device with no networking, accounts, or analytics anywhere in the core loop; the one deliberate exception is that a shared recap image is user-initiated data leaving the device, which is accepted and named explicitly rather than left as an unstated assumption.

**Risk register:**
- Recap card technically correct but not something anyone would actually send — named in the prior phase as a real stop condition, still holds here: if it fails this bar, rework the pack/card design before touching the other two mechanics or the real paywall.
- `isPackUnlocked` stub silently becoming permanent policy once a second pack ships — mitigated by marking it explicitly temporary now.
- Accessibility limitation, named and accepted rather than treated as a gap to fix: pass-and-play inherently requires someone to look at the phone to know whose turn it is; VoiceOver can make each screen navigable and announce state correctly, but it cannot solve the structural "you must see the phone to know it's your turn" property of this game design.

**Final assumptions:** Reveal/confession is the only mechanic in this slice (already locked); the active-player/target rotation rule above is the canonical, buildable spec replacing all earlier vaguer "3-turn window" language; termination-survival (not just backgrounding) is the real validation bar; recap tie/degenerate-case copy is a named design decision, not something to be improvised during build.

# campfire — Detailed Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:24:39._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
```

## Phase Purpose

Deeply analyze requirements, edge cases, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions, resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

I would lock this phase around one hard thesis: Campfire must prove it can generate one genuinely shareworthy social artifact from one phone, completely offline, with zero confusion about whose turn it is. The decisive requirement is not “more mechanics” or “paywall plumbing” — it is that a two-minute reveal session with 2-15 people ends in a recap card a real person wants to send. That means reveal/confession is the only mechanic for this slice, because it combines the hardest game logic (current player + distinct, non-repeating target selection) and the strongest shareable social payoff.

So the resolved requirements are: one-screen flow from entry → setup → play → reveal → recap; fixed session length from deck/round exhaustion (no knobs yet); reveal mode only; “Coming soon” placeholders for the other two mechanics with no tap action; no fake lock/paywall because nothing is really lockable in this slice; robust local persistence at every transition; and ShareLink as a first-class behavior, not optional decoration. I want one content-unlock seam (`ContentAccessProvider` with `isPackUnlocked`) but it must always return unlocked today and be invisible in user flow.

Edge cases that are likely to burn this if we don’t enforce them now: 2-player alternation where target must always be the other person; large groups where target must not repeat within a 3-turn rolling window (explicit testable rule, not heuristic); backgrounding during a turn and returning to the exact same state; duplicate player names and blank names; empty prompt pack; zero answers (or people rushing taps); rapid double-taps causing phantom scoring/haptics; share rendering failure on physical devices; and session resume with partial state if app kills before write commit (so checkpoints must be atomic per transition).

Data and privacy implications are straightforward but should still be explicit: all player names, session summaries, and prompt usage stay local, no analytics, no networking, no accounts, no crash telemetry collection unless it is opt-in and local-only; no sensitive profile data beyond typed names that users type for the room. Data-loss is the privacy-equivalent risk here: users lose a completed session only if persistence breaks, so durability is product correctness, not just reliability.

Biggest risk I’d own is that we can satisfy all technical checks and still ship a card nobody sends — that is a product failure, not an engineering one. That’s why recap design and copy must stay in scope as hard gates: winner line, funniest standout, and one emotional sentence unique to that session. If it isn’t clearly worth forwarding, stop and rework content/card before adding mechanics or paywall.

One bold constraint to preserve into the final output: this is an occasion engine, not a daily loop app, so avoid “habit” mechanics and any fake engagement scaffolding. Also preserve that if this slice cannot consistently produce a sendable recap at 2 and 12–15 players under interruption, then v1 scope should stop and iterate here, not expand outward.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Picking up the reveal/confession slice that's already locked, my job this round is to find what breaks it in practice, not to re-litigate the mechanic choice.

The first thing I want to pressure-test is the target-rotation rule itself, because "minimum 3-turn separation window" quietly assumes there are enough other players to rotate through, and that's false at the low end. With 3 players, excluding the current speaker leaves exactly 2 candidates — you cannot go 3 turns without repeating one of them, it's a pigeonhole problem, not an implementation bug. With 4 players it's tight but barely possible. So the rule as stated will either be silently violated (and nobody will notice until a real group plays a 3-4 person session and needs the "no repeat" guarantee) or the algorithm will hit a dead end and do something undefined. This phase needs to pin down the actual degradation rule: something like "guarantee no immediate repeat of the prior turn's target, and minimize repeats within a window that shrinks to player-count-minus-1 when the group is small," stated explicitly rather than left as one fixed number that only works above some unstated player count.

Second, I want the vocabulary nailed down before build starts, because I've seen "current speaker" and "target player" used almost interchangeably in the prior transcript and they are not the same role. Is there a rotating "reader" whose turn it is to hold the phone and read the prompt aloud, and separately a "target" who the prompt is about — and can those ever be the same person (a prompt landing on the person currently holding the phone, about themselves)? If reveal/confession prompts are phrased like "who's most likely to…," the natural pattern is the room votes/reveals collectively and one specific person is named as the answer — there may not be a "current speaker" role distinct from "whoever's turn it is to trigger the reveal." This needs one settled model before anyone writes turn-engine code, because the persistence schema and the "who's up" UI both depend on knowing exactly which roles exist.

Third: backgrounding is not the scariest lifecycle event here, termination is. The locked criteria test "one background/foreground cycle," but real pass-and-play sessions get swipe-quit constantly — someone fumbles the phone, hits the home indicator by accident, or a low-battery warning kicks them to the app switcher and they force-close by habit. If session state only survives suspension and not an actual process relaunch, the app will pass every test we've defined and still lose sessions in the wild. I want "resume correctly after full app termination mid-round," not just backgrounding, written into the validation bar explicitly, since SwiftData persistence should already make this nearly free once transition-checkpointing is real — but only if we test the harder case, not the easier one.

Fourth, the recap card's "specific standout line" needs a defined tie-breaking and degenerate-case behavior before anyone treats it as done. What does the card say when two players are tied for the most reveals, or when it's a 2-player session where the same one or two possible outcomes repeat by mathematical necessity and there's nothing statistically "standout" about the result? A card that reads "Alex was most likely to... (also, so was Sam, equally)" is not the punchy artifact the whole growth loop depends on. I'd rather this phase name the fallback copy rule now (e.g., a defined tie-handling template) than leave it for someone to improvise mid-build under time pressure.

Fifth, there's a quieter privacy point worth stating plainly rather than assuming it's obvious: player first names get baked into a rendered image that leaves the app via the share sheet, and once it's in a group chat or someone's camera roll it's completely outside this app's control — that's an accepted tradeoff given the product's whole premise, but it should be a named, deliberate acceptance in this phase's output, not something nobody wrote down. Locally, names should be stored only as long as the session/recap persists on-device, with no analytics pipeline anywhere near them — worth stating explicitly since "no accounts, no networking" already implies it but doesn't make it a written privacy position.

Sixth, on the accessibility front, I want to flag a real limitation rather than pretend pass-and-play is accessible by default: this entire mechanic assumes a sighted person can look at the phone and see whose turn it is, and a "read prompt aloud" flow inherently depends on someone being able to read the screen. That's fine as a known constraint of the physical-party-object concept, but VoiceOver support should be scoped honestly — it can make each screen navigable and each state announce correctly for a low-vision user who's handed the phone, but it can't solve the fundamental "you have to look at the phone to know it's your turn" problem. I'd rather this be a named, accepted limitation than something a later review flags as an accessibility failure when it's actually a structural property of the product.

Last, a smaller but real one: the `isPackUnlocked` stub returning true for everything needs a guardrail so it can't accidentally read as a premium-features-are-free bug later — since there's only one pack right now there's nothing visibly wrong, but if anyone reuses that stub pattern for a second pack before the real entitlement check lands, we'd ship a paid pack for free by omission. I want an explicit assumption on record that the stub is a placeholder that must be replaced, not a permanent access policy, so it doesn't survive by inertia into a later build pass.

The biggest risk to my own list here: I'm asking for more precision on rare edge cases (3-player rotation math, tie-breaking copy, force-termination) in a slice that's already been fought over three rounds for being appropriately small — there's a real tension between "de-risk everything" and "ship the smallest slice," and I don't want this phase to re-inflate scope. But none of what I'm asking for is a new feature — it's tightening the definition of behavior that's already in scope (the rotation rule, the recap card, the persistence requirement) so it doesn't quietly fail in exactly the player-count extremes the app is supposed to be validated at.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where the room stands after this round: Codex laid out the phase's backbone cleanly — one shareworthy artifact, offline, zero turn-confusion, reveal/confession only, fixed session length, "coming soon" placeholders with no fake lock UI, the `isPackUnlocked` stub, and ShareLink treated as core rather than decoration. Claude didn't push back on any of that framing — instead Claude went digging for the specific ways this could quietly fail in real play, and turned up several concrete problems that the locked language doesn't yet answer:

This needs a real degrading rule (e.g., no immediate repeat, tightening window as player count shrinks), not one fixed number.
2. **"Current speaker" vs "target player" hasn't actually been defined as distinct roles.** Is there a phone-holder separate from the person the prompt is about, or could they collapse into one role? This decides the persistence schema and the "who's up" UI, so it can't stay implicit.
3. **The validation bar tests backgrounding, but real usage is dominated by force-quits, not backgrounding.** Claude wants "resumes correctly after full termination," which is a stronger and more realistic bar than what's currently written.

5. Two smaller adds worth keeping: naming the "names leave the device via the share image, and that's an accepted tradeoff" as an explicit privacy position rather than an implied one; and flagging the `isPackUnlocked` stub explicitly as temporary so it doesn't quietly become "everything's free forever" once a second pack shows up.

None of this contradicts Codex's framing — it's tightening definitions of things already in scope, not proposing new features. But it is genuinely unresolved: Codex hasn't yet responded to any of these five points, so we don't have agreement on the fixed rotation rule, the role vocabulary, the termination bar, or the tie-breaking copy.

CONSENSUS: NO

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

I agree with both of you on the core: this phase lives or dies on one complete reveal session that actually creates something people want to send, and that means we lock the hard edges now instead of handwaving them. Codex, I’m with you that reveal/confession is the right mechanic anchor; Claude, your edge-case catches are exactly why we should keep slicing this. Let’s make the rules explicit and deterministic so no one can interpret them differently later.

Here’s my concrete proposal: define two roles only in the model, `speaker` and `target`. In reveal/confession, the speaker is the person holding the phone and pressing “next,” and the target is the person being judged; they must be different. That’s a hard rule in prompt generation (“choose another player if only one player were available, block and show error”), which keeps the turn UI unambiguous and removes a source of accidental same-person prompts.

For target rotation, ditch the fixed “3-turn window” as a universal rule. Use an adaptive separation window based on player count. Exact rule: 2 players strict alternation (speaker/target can only be the other player); 3 players enforce “no consecutive repeat target” (3+ is mathematically impossible to enforce without repeat in 3-turn window); 4 players enforce 2-turn separation; 5+ can enforce 3-turn separation. Also, always enforce `target != speaker` first, then separation rule second. That preserves the spirit of the no-repeat quality bar while being mathematically honest.

On reliability, I want us to stop saying backgrounding and start saying process death. If this uses local persistence correctly, the same checkpointing should recover from full app termination mid-round with no duplicated prompt/points; that is the real-world bar. So change validation to explicit crash/relaunch recovery too, not just background/foreground. This is not extra scope—it is the same local persistence requirement, just the version that matches real usage.

For recap quality, tie-handling must be explicit too: winner line is not “there’s some winner.” We need deterministic copy. My preference: if one clear top, use “[X] owned the spotlight…”; if tie for first, use “[A] and [B] are tied for funniest; this room can’t decide,” and for 2-player tie use “It was a perfect split.” This gives a specific and emotionally legible artifact even in degenerate outcomes and keeps the share loop rationale honest.

I also support Claude’s privacy and stub cautions. We should explicitly state: sharing a recap image is an intentional, user-visible data egress (names can leave the device), and local player/session data is only on-device with no analytics or networking. For entitlement, wire the seam (`ContentAccessProvider.isPackUnlocked`) with always-true now, but add a hard `// temporary`/`assumption` guard so we don’t accidentally keep everything forever unlocked when second-pack logic lands. This phase is still the right place to mark those failure modes and assumptions, otherwise we’ll pass today and inherit correctness debt tomorrow.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Codex, your round-1 framing didn't actually answer the rotation math, so let me just propose the fix directly instead of waiting for someone else to do it, because I think this is the one thing standing between us and closing this phase clean.

Here's the concrete rule I'd lock in: split the two roles the way I flagged, but resolve it simply. There's an "active player" — whoever's physically holding the phone and taps to advance the round — and that role rotates in strict, deterministic round-robin order (player 1, 2, 3... wrap around). That part is trivial and needs no anti-repeat logic at all, because strict rotation by definition never "repeats" anyone out of turn. Separately there's the "target" — the person the reveal names — and the only hard rule on target selection is that it can never equal the current active player (you shouldn't get revealed about yourself while you're the one holding the phone and reading it). Past that one mandatory exclusion, selection should just be "least-recently-targeted, excluding whoever was targeted last round." That's it — no fixed magic number like "3-turn window." At 12-15 players this naturally produces something that looks like a near-full rotation before anyone repeats, easily satisfying what we originally meant by "no repeat in 3 turns." At 3 players, the same rule still works and doesn't break: excluding the active player and the immediately-prior target leaves exactly one legal candidate when there are 3 players, so the algorithm never even has to choose — it's deterministic and never produces an illegal or repeated-immediately state. The only thing that changes at small counts is how much the "least-recently-targeted" tiebreak matters, not whether the exclusion rule holds. So instead of a fixed window size that's provably impossible below 5 players, we get one rule that degrades gracefully and is trivially provable correct at both 2 (where target = "the other person," full stop, every round) and 15. I think this is strictly better than what's locked right now and I want it written into the final output verbatim rather than the vaguer "3-turn separation window" language, because that language is what a build agent would try to literally implement and then discover is broken.

On the recap card tie-breaking, since nobody's answered that yet either, here's my concrete proposal rather than leaving it as an open question: when there's a tie for most-targeted, name all tied players in one sentence ("Alex and Sam were both most likely to end up on a reality show — twice each") rather than picking an arbitrary winner, because silently breaking a tie in favor of whoever happens to be first alphabetically or first in the array is exactly the kind of thing that reads as a bug to a sharp player ("wait, why does it say Alex and not Sam, we were tied"). And for degenerate small sessions — 2 players, where the "target" is mathematically always the same one person the whole game — I don't think the card should pretend it found a statistical insight, because it didn't. I'd rather the recap logic detect "this session had fewer than, say, 4 distinct targets" and switch to a different standout-line strategy: pull the specific funniest/most reveal-worthy single moment from the session rather than a "most likely to" superlative that's true only because there was no other option. That's a real design decision, not a technical afterthought, and I don't want it left to whoever's implementing the recap screen to improvise under time pressure — Codex, you said yourself the recap needs to stay a hard gate, and I think an undefined tie/degenerate case is exactly the kind of thing that quietly slips through "looks done" review because nobody happens to test a 2-player or an exactly-tied session.

On termination versus backgrounding, I don't think there's actually a disagreement here, I just want the words changed: "survives one background/foreground cycle" should become "survives full app termination and relaunch," because the second one is strictly harder and subsumes the first — if checkpointing to SwiftData happens synchronously at every state transition (which is already the locked design), termination-survival isn't extra engineering work, it's the same mechanism, we're just testing the version of the claim that actually matches how people misuse their phones at parties. I'd be surprised if Codex disagrees with this since it's free given what's already agreed, but I want it explicit in the validation criteria rather than assumed.

The vocabulary question I raised is basically answered by the active-player/target split above, so I'll consider that resolved rather than reopening it — I don't think we need a third role. And on the two smaller points, the "names leave the device via the shared image" privacy line and the "isPackUnlocked stub is temporary, must be replaced before a second pack ships" flag — nobody's pushed back on either, so I'll just say I want both carried into the final output verbatim rather than treated as understood-but-unwritten, since unwritten assumptions are exactly what get silently dropped two phases from now when whoever's building slice two doesn't have this transcript in front of them.

So my ask this round: if Codex signs off on the active-player/target split with the "exclude active player + exclude immediately-prior target, else least-recently-targeted" rule, and the tie-handling/degenerate-session recap rule, I think we're actually done — everything else in the prior rounds' output stands as written. I don't think there's a real fight left here, just unresolved specificity, and I'd rather nail the specificity now than have it get invented ad hoc during build and drift from what three rounds of debate already decided this app needs to be.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Good round — this one actually closed the gap. Both landed on the same shape for almost everything: two roles only (active player = whoever's holding the phone and advancing rounds, in strict round-robin order; target = who the reveal is about), a hard rule that target can never equal the active player, "survives full termination and relaunch" replacing the weaker background/foreground language (since it's free given persistence checkpointing is already locked), explicit tie-handling copy for the recap card instead of a silent arbitrary winner, a distinct fallback strategy for degenerate small sessions where a "most likely to" superlative would be meaningless, the "names leave the device via the shared image" privacy line written down explicitly, and the `isPackUnlocked` stub flagged as temporary so it can't quietly survive into a real second pack.

The one spot where they proposed two different mechanisms rather than the same one: Codex proposed a staged rule (2p strict alternation, 3p no-consecutive-repeat, 4p 2-turn separation, 5p+ 3-turn separation), while Claude proposed a single unified rule — exclude the active player, exclude whoever was targeted last round, then pick whoever's gone longest without being targeted. Working through it, Claude's version isn't a competing idea, it's a cleaner generalization that produces the exact same behavior Codex wanted at every player count (strict alternation falls out naturally at 2, the forced-single-candidate falls out naturally at 3, and it gets progressively more spread-out at higher counts) without needing per-count special cases or a magic number that breaks below 5 players. Nothing here is actually contested — it's one clean rule replacing four bespoke ones aimed at the same goal.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- Two roles only: **active player** (rotates in strict round-robin order, taps to advance each round) and **target** (who the reveal names). Active player and target can never be the same person — hard rule, enforced before any other selection logic.
- Target selection rule (replaces the earlier "3-turn window" language, which was mathematically impossible below 5 players): exclude the active player, exclude whoever was targeted in the immediately prior round, then choose whoever has gone longest since last being targeted. This single rule is provably correct and deterministic at every player count from 2 to 15 — at 2 players it collapses to strict alternation; at 3 it collapses to one forced legal candidate every round; at higher counts it naturally spreads targets out with no repeats in any practically noticeable window.
- Persistence/reliability bar upgraded from "survives one background/foreground cycle" to **survives full app termination and relaunch mid-round**, with zero duplicated prompts, targets, or tallies. This is not new engineering scope — it's the same per-transition checkpointing already locked, just validated against the harder, more realistic real-world failure mode (people force-quit apps at parties far more than they background them).
- Recap card must have explicit, deterministic behavior for non-obvious outcomes rather than silently picking a winner:
  - Clear single standout: name them directly ("Alex owned the spotlight...").
  - Tie for most-targeted: name all tied players together, never silently break the tie.
  - Degenerate small sessions (e.g. fewer than ~4 distinct targets, which includes all 2-player games): don't manufacture a fake statistical "most likely to" line — switch to surfacing the single funniest/most notable reveal moment from the session instead.
- Privacy position stated explicitly, not left implied: sharing the recap image is an intentional, user-visible data egress — player names leave the device once shared, and that's an accepted tradeoff of the core premise. Locally, no analytics, no networking, no accounts; names/session data live on-device only as long as the session/recap persists.
- `ContentAccessProvider.isPackUnlocked` ships as a stub returning true for everything today, explicitly marked as temporary/placeholder (not a real access policy) so it isn't mistaken later for intentional "everything's free" behavior once a second pack exists.

**Edge cases enumerated:** 2-player strict alternation; 3-player forced single-candidate targeting; duplicate/blank player names; empty prompt pack; rapid double-taps causing phantom scoring or duplicate haptics; app force-terminated mid-round; recap tie for most-targeted; degenerate low-variance sessions with no real statistical standout; share-image render failure on physical device.

**Data and privacy implications:** All player names and session data stay on-device with no networking, accounts, or analytics anywhere in the core loop; the one deliberate exception is that a shared recap image is user-initiated data leaving the device, which is accepted and named explicitly rather than left as an unstated assumption.

**Risk register:**
- Recap card technically correct but not something anyone would actually send — named in the prior phase as a real stop condition, still holds here: if it fails this bar, rework the pack/card design before touching the other two mechanics or the real paywall.
- `isPackUnlocked` stub silently becoming permanent policy once a second pack ships — mitigated by marking it explicitly temporary now.
- Accessibility limitation, named and accepted rather than treated as a gap to fix: pass-and-play inherently requires someone to look at the phone to know whose turn it is; VoiceOver can make each screen navigable and announce state correctly, but it cannot solve the structural "you must see the phone to know it's your turn" property of this game design.

**Final assumptions:** Reveal/confession is the only mechanic in this slice (already locked); the active-player/target rotation rule above is the canonical, buildable spec replacing all earlier vaguer "3-turn window" language; termination-survival (not just backgrounding) is the real validation bar; recap tie/degenerate-case copy is a named design decision, not something to be improvised during build.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes the phase well. The round-1 disagreement (Codex's staged per-player-count rotation rule vs. Claude's unified exclude-active/exclude-prior-target/least-recently-targeted rule) is resolved with actual reasoning, not by picking a side arbitrarily — the coordinator correctly identifies that Claude's rule is a generalization that produces Codex's desired behavior at every player count without a magic number that breaks below 5 players. I checked the rule's correctness at the boundaries: at 2 players it provably collapses to strict alternation (excluding active + prior-target reduces to excluding the one other player each round); at 3+ players, target≠active and target≠immediately-prior-target are both guaranteed by construction, so no immediate repeat is possible regardless of tie-break policy, even though the "always exactly one forced candcandidate at 3 players" framing is a mild oversimplification (it depends on tie-break specifics whether exclusions collapse to 1 or 2 people) — this is a minor rhetorical imprecision, not a specification defect, since the hard guarantees (never self-target, never immediate repeat) hold unconditionally.

All five required sections are present and concrete: resolved requirements (roles, rotation rule, termination-survival bar, recap tie/degenerate-case copy rules, privacy statement, stub-temporariness flag), edge cases (2p/3p rotation extremes, blank/duplicate names, empty pack, double-tap, force-termination, recap ties, degenerate low-variance sessions, share-render failure), data/privacy implications (on-device only, explicit named exception for shared recap images), a risk register with real mitigations or named/accepted limitations (including the accessibility limitation framed honestly as structural rather than a fixable gap), and final assumptions that lock the canonical spec for build.

This round directly answered every open point the quality gate raised after round 1 — the mathematically broken window rule, the speaker/target vocabulary, termination vs. backgrounding, and recap tie/degenerate handling — with no unresolved thread left dangling. Consistent with all prior locked phases (reveal/confession-only slice, stub seam, no fake paywall, recap-card-as-stop-condition). No repair needed.

One non-blocking note worth a one-line mention when this feeds the build phase: the exact deterministic tie-break for "least-recently-targeted" ties (e.g., first couple of rounds when multiple players have never been targeted) isn't spelled out — a simple rule like "ties break by ascending rotation order from active+1" would remove any last ambiguity, but this is an implementation nit, not a phase-blocking gap.
