# campfire — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:31:33._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

I would keep this phase locked to a single, non-negotiable proof path: launch → reveal setup → pass-and-play reveal loop → recap card → ShareLink. Adding anything else now (paywall, second mode, saved archive, mid-session roster editing, settings surface) makes it easier to demo and harder to prove reliability, which is the opposite of this phase’s goal.

The screen set should stay tiny and stable: entry (with one active Reveal tile and two non-tappable “Coming soon” placeholders), player setup, reveal round, reveal/result, recap card, and a small error state set for no prompts and image render failure. That gives enough structure to ship a real social artifact while leaving a clean seam for future mechanics and paywall work.

Biggest risk is not UI polish; it is state correctness under real party conditions. The app must be correct with 2 players and big groups, survive force-quit and relaunch mid-round, and never drift duplicate state (prompt index, tally, speaker, target). If this fails once, it becomes trust-damaging in front of a group and impossible to recover later.

The rotation rule should be simple and universal: active player is strict round-robin; target is chosen with speaker/target exclusion of self and prior target, then least-recently-targeted tie-break. This is robust from 2 to 15 players and avoids special-case hacks. Recap quality must be part of the product core, not an afterthought: define tie and low-variance fallback copy now, otherwise a “statistically” generated card can look fake and kill the viral loop.

The phase must preserve the “no fake gating” rule: `ContentAccessProvider.isPackUnlocked` can exist as a temporary always-true seam, but there is no real lock sheet or paywall route in this slice because there is only one actual free pack. Preserve this explicitly so architecture is ready for StoreKit in the next pass without confusing users now.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Thinking about this as someone standing in a living room at 9pm on a Friday, phone in hand, three friends staring at them waiting to see what this thing even is — that's the user I'm designing for, and every screen decision below is in service of that person not fumbling for more than a couple of seconds at any point.

Screen inventory, in the order this person actually encounters them: a cold-launch entry screen with three tiles (Reveal lit up in full color, Voting/Ranking and Rapid Choice rendered as flat, desaturated "Coming soon" text with no border, no chevron, nothing that reads as tappable); a player setup screen that's really one screen with two states — a name-entry list with a persistent "add player" affordance and a big disabled-until-valid continue button showing why it's disabled; a pass-the-phone interstitial that appears between every single round, not just the first one — this is the one screen I want to insist on that isn't explicitly nailed down yet, a full-bleed "PASS TO [NAME]" moment with a single giant tap target, because the entry-screen/round-loop language so far implies the round header just updates in place, and I think that's a mistake for a physical pass-and-play object. If the active-player name only changes at the top of a card, the outgoing holder has no clear beat telling them "your job here is done, hand it off now" — they'll either sit there staring at the next prompt that isn't theirs, or two people will both go for the phone. A dedicated hand-off screen, even a two-second one with a spring-bounce transition, is cheap and removes a real point of confusion at exactly the moment 12+ player groups get chaotic. After hand-off: the round screen itself, which has its own two-beat internal state — a "ready" state showing the big two-column header (who's holding, who's tonight's spotlight is on — though I'd word it so the target's identity is NOT shown until the reveal beat, otherwise there's no reveal, just an announcement) and a tap-to-reveal action, then a revealed state with the actual prompt text, the haptic/confetti moment, and the advance control. Then, after the deck or round count is exhausted, an end-of-session recap screen with the card preview and the ShareLink button, and from there either "play again" (new setup, not same players auto-loaded — that's out of scope per the locked cut list) or exit to the entry screen.

On the target-reveal timing question above — I want to flag this as a real design decision, not a detail: does the room see the prompt category before knowing who it's about, and is there a genuine "who could it be" beat before the name shows? I think yes, that's basically the entire fun of the mechanic, and if the target's name renders simultaneously with the prompt text, we've built a slot machine with the tension already spent before anyone pulls the lever.

State-wise, every screen needs its full set considered, not assumed: setup has an error state for blank/whitespace names shown inline per-row (not a toast, not a summary banner — the invalid row itself should show the problem) and a distinct disabled-continue state under 2 or over 15 with the reason spelled out in text, not just a grayed button. The round screen has an error state for an empty/exhausted prompt pack that has to be reachable and has to offer a real recovery path — end session early and go to recap with whatever data exists, not a dead screen. The recap screen has a loading state while the image renders (should be near-instant but still needs a spinner state defined rather than a blank flash) and an explicit failure state — fallback text summary plus a retry button, never a silent dead end. The entry screen technically has no error state since there's nothing to fail, but it does have the accessibility-only "not available" state on the two placeholder tiles.

Visual direction: I'd commit hard to the campfire-orange-to-hot-pink gradient as a background wash that shifts subtly per screen (warmer/more orange during setup, hotter pink during the reveal beat) rather than a static gradient slapped behind everything — that's the difference between "branded app" and "this feels like it's reacting to what's happening." Bold rounded slab type for anything that's a name or a big state word (PASS TO SAM, YOUR TURN), but the actual prompt text itself should be a slightly calmer, more readable weight — if everything is shouting, nothing is. Oversized tap targets aren't just a nice-to-have here, they're load-bearing: someone's handing this phone to a slightly tipsy relative, the tap target for "advance" and "pass" should be close to full-width, full-height-band, not a pill button.

Accessibility notes: contrast-safe tokens have to be defined and tested against the actual gradient stops before anyone starts implementing views, because saturated orange-to-pink backgrounds are exactly where white text quietly fails WCAG AA at the pink end even when it passes at the orange end. Dynamic Type needs headroom in the two-column header specifically — "PASS TO ALEXANDRIA" at accessibility text sizes will not fit a two-column layout, so that header needs a tested wrapping/truncation rule now, not a shrug later. I'll also restate plainly, because it needs to survive into this phase's output even though it's not new: this is a sighted, pass-and-play experience by structural design, and no amount of VoiceOver polish changes that — what VoiceOver can and must do is correctly announce whichever state is on screen and never announce the two placeholder tiles as interactive.

The single biggest risk to my own position here is the pass-the-phone interstitial — it's an extra screen and extra tap in a product where every prior phase has been ruthless about cutting anything that isn't load-bearing, and someone could reasonably argue it slows down a 15-player, many-round session with an extra tap per round. I think it's worth it because ambiguous turn-taking was already flagged twice in earlier phases as the top systems risk, and a screen is a much cheaper fix than a state bug — but if the group decides it's one tap too many, the fallback is at minimum a strong transition animation and haptic buzz at the moment the active player changes, so the hand-off is still unmistakable even without a dedicated screen.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things stand after round 1. Codex mostly reaffirmed what's already locked from prior phases — same six-screen proof path (entry → setup → round → reveal → recap → share), state correctness as the top risk, the universal rotation rule, tie/low-variance recap copy, and the always-true `isPackUnlocked` seam with no real paywall. Nothing new there, and nothing contested.

Claude took that skeleton and did the actual design-phase work: a concrete screen inventory in user-facing order, plus two genuinely new proposals that Codex hasn't weighed in on yet —

1. **A dedicated "pass the phone" hand-off screen** between every round, not just an in-place header update — a full-bleed "PASS TO [NAME]" beat with one giant tap target, argued as cheap insurance against the exact turn-ambiguity risk flagged twice in earlier phases as the top systems risk. Claude flagged the real tradeoff itself: it's an extra tap per round in a product that's been ruthless about cutting non-load-bearing screens, and offered a fallback (strong transition + haptic only, no dedicated screen) if the group decides it's one tap too many.
2. **Delayed target reveal** — the two-column header shows "who's holding" immediately but withholds "who's the spotlight's on" until a deliberate tap-to-reveal beat, so there's an actual "who could it be" moment instead of announcing everything at once.

Claude also raised concrete, testable requirements that need sign-off: contrast-safe gradient tokens checked against the actual orange→pink stops (not just at one end), and a defined wrapping/truncation rule for the two-column header at accessibility Dynamic Type sizes (e.g. "PASS TO ALEXANDRIA" has to fit somehow).

None of this contradicts Codex — it's just that Codex's round hadn't addressed any of it yet. Next round needs Codex to actually respond to the hand-off-screen tradeoff and the delayed-reveal-timing call, since those are real product decisions (not details) that change the screen inventory and state model.

## Final Output

(Not final yet — round 1 only. Proceeding to round 2 for Codex to respond to the hand-off screen, reveal-timing, and contrast/Dynamic-Type asks before this phase can close.)

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

I’m siding with a tighter version than Claude’s “full pass-it-screen every round.” I agree the intent is great, but for this slice that extra route is exactly the kind of friction that makes a room tune it out. We’re explicitly optimizing for the shortest path to the social moment and the most failure-prone state correctness at 2–15 players. Adding an extra full screen just to communicate “pass to X” makes every round feel heavier, and in a 12-person chain game that becomes 10–15 extra taps before anyone even hears a prompt. So I think we should not add a separate hand-off screen.

Here’s the compromise that keeps Claude’s clarity gains without the UI tax: keep one round screen only, but make pass ownership explicit in that state. Right after each tap on “Next,” show a hard visual transition into a dominant two-column header with one oversized left badge “YOU HOLD [NAME]” and a right badge that reads “TONIGHT’S SPOTLIGHT: ?” until the reveal action. Then one big full-width button—single tap only—unveils the target and prompt together, and that’s where the haptic/confetti is triggered once. In other words, we preserve the suspense and the “who now?” ambiguity-free handoff cue, but we don’t add extra navigation or a second round-specific screen.

I’m also going to lock the reveal-timing choice: no pre-show of target identity in that initial state, target should be hidden until user-triggered reveal. If we show both at once the first thing people discuss is “it was Sam” and we lose the social beat that makes this mechanic feel like a party prop instead of a list reader. So the delay is not cosmetic; it’s core game tension.

On Codex’s earlier point about getting this done, this is still the minimum-behavior path: entry (with two non-interactive coming-soon tiles), setup, round/reveal, result, recap, share. If we don’t keep that line short, we never get the hardest part—rotation + persistence + real recap—to rock-solid, and that’s the only thing that can prove the idea right. To satisfy the earlier accessibility concern too, this still means: big labels, VoiceOver order that reads “You hold … Target locked …,” and the coming-soon tiles explicitly announced as not available. That gets us to production-quality proof with less dead-lean structure and more social value.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Codex, your round 1 is basically the coordinator's synthesis restated as a pragmatist's memo, and I don't have a fight with any of the substance — the six-screen proof path, the strict-round-robin-plus-least-recently-targeted rule, the "no fake gating" line — that's all already locked and I'm not touching it. But you didn't actually engage with the two things I put on the table, and I want to push on both instead of letting them get quietly absorbed into "yeah, sounds fine."

On the pass-the-phone moment: I think I was overclaiming when I called it a "screen," and on reflection that's actually the wrong frame and probably why it reads as scope creep against your "keep the screen set tiny" instinct. It shouldn't be a seventh destination in the screen inventory — it should be a state of the reveal-round screen itself, the same way "ready" and "revealed" are states of that screen, not separate screens. So the round screen's state machine becomes: handoff → ready → revealed → (loop or done). That's zero new navigation, zero new routes, and it keeps your six-screen structure completely intact. Where I'll go further than my round 1 pitch: make the handoff state auto-advance after something like a second and a half with a haptic buzz, with a tap-anywhere to skip early for anyone who's already looking at the phone and ready to go. That kills your legitimate worry about an extra mandatory tap slowing down round 11 of a 15-player game, while still giving the outgoing player an unambiguous "your job is done, physically hand this to Sam now" beat instead of relying on them to notice a header changed. I'd rather ship that than nothing, because the whole reason target-rotation correctness got promoted to a scripted-verification must-have two phases ago is that turn ambiguity is the failure mode that looks fine in a demo and blows up at a real party — and a header-only transition is exactly the kind of thing that looks fine in a demo.

On delayed target reveal, I want to make the case harder than I did last round, because I think this is actually load-bearing for the thing your own round 1 message cares about most — recap card quality. You wrote that a card built from bad data "can look fake and kill the viral loop," and I agree, but the emotional intensity of the reveal moment itself is what makes the eventual recap line land at all. If "TONIGHT'S SPOTLIGHT" just prints the name the instant the round starts, there's no beat, no group lean-in, no reaction to capture in memory — it's a status readout, not a reveal. If instead the header shows something like a silhouette or "???" and the actual name animates in with the confetti-haptic beat after a tap, you get an actual moment, and moments are what get talked about afterward and make someone want the recap card as a souvenir of it. This costs us nothing new architecturally — it's the same tap-to-reveal control already in the round screen's state model, just changing what's hidden versus shown between the "ready" and "revealed" states.

One more thing I want to nail down before this phase closes, because it's the kind of detail that's easy to hand-wave past: the recap card's visual composition needs an actual described layout, not just "renders session data." I'd propose it reads top-to-bottom as a keepsake, not a stat sheet — big campfire-gradient background matching the app's palette, session title/date-ish framing at top, the standout line (or tie line, or funniest-moment line per the locked copy rules) as the dominant, largest text on the card since that's the one line someone screenshots for, then a small players-list footer in smaller type. That ordering matters because if the standout line is buried under a list of names and round counts, nobody's thumb stops scrolling long enough to read it, and the entire "worth sending" bar this project keeps holding itself to depends on that one line hitting immediately. I also want to flag, since the pitfalls reference already warned about it and it's exactly the kind of thing that quietly fails only on a real device: whatever gradient and text layering we use for that card has to actually get rendered and screenshotted on a physical phone before this is called done, not just eyeballed in Xcode previews.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

This round actually closed the gap. Codex came back with a real compromise instead of just restating "keep it small" — no separate hand-off screen, but pass-ownership gets folded into the existing round screen as a state (hidden target badge until a single reveal tap fires the confetti/haptic beat). Claude met that exactly where it needed to: reframing her own "hand-off screen" pitch as a state, not a new destination — handoff → ready → revealed, with the handoff state auto-advancing after ~1.5s with a haptic buzz, tap-anywhere to skip early. That resolves both people's actual concerns: Codex's fear of extra mandatory taps piling up over a 12-15 player game, and Claude's fear of an in-place header update being too easy to miss at a real party. Zero new screens, zero new navigation — the six-screen structure stays exactly as locked.

Delayed target reveal is also fully agreed now — target identity stays hidden ("TONIGHT'S SPOTLIGHT: ?") until the same tap that triggers the reveal animation. Both agents independently landed on this being load-bearing for the reveal actually feeling like a moment instead of a status readout, which matters because that reaction is what makes the eventual recap line worth screenshotting.

The one thing added this round that hasn't been explicitly contested or explicitly confirmed by name is Claude's recap card layout description (gradient background → title/date framing → standout/tie/funniest-moment line as the dominant, largest text → small players-list footer) and the requirement that it get rendered and screenshotted on a physical device before being called done. Nothing in Codex's message argues against it, and it's a direct, concrete answer to something already locked two phases ago (recap quality is the whole viral loop) — so I'm folding it in as settled rather than leaving it dangling.

## Final Output

**Screen inventory** (six screens, unchanged from prior phases — no new navigation added):
1. Entry — three tiles: Reveal (active, full color, tappable), Voting/Ranking and Rapid Choice (flat, desaturated "Coming soon" text, no tap target, no lock icon, `accessibilityHint("Not available yet")`).
2. Player setup — name list + add-player affordance, disabled-until-valid continue button showing why it's disabled.
3. Reveal round — a single screen with an internal state machine (see below), not split into multiple screens.
4. Recap card — card preview + ShareLink button.
5. Error/recovery states — reachable from setup (invalid name count/blank names) and from the round screen (empty/exhausted prompt pack → end session early into recap with whatever data exists) and recap (render failure → fallback text summary + retry).
6. (Exit/replay routes back to Entry — "play again" always re-enters setup fresh, no auto-loaded roster, per the already-locked cut list.)

**Primary user flow:** Entry → tap Reveal → player setup (2–15 names, validated) → round screen cycling through handoff → ready → revealed states once per turn → recap card → ShareLink → play again (back to setup) or exit (back to entry).

**State model per screen:**
- *Entry:* no error state possible; only variation is the two placeholder tiles rendering as visually and semantically non-interactive.
- *Setup:* default (empty/partial roster) → inline per-row error for blank/whitespace names (shown on the offending row, not a toast/banner) → disabled-continue state with a stated reason when under 2 or over 15 players → valid/enabled continue.
- *Round screen (the core state machine):* **handoff** (dominant "PASS TO [NAME]" moment, auto-advances after ~1.5s with a haptic buzz, tap-anywhere skips early) → **ready** (two-column header: "YOU HOLD [NAME]" fully shown, "TONIGHT'S SPOTLIGHT: ?" hidden, single full-width reveal button) → **revealed** (target name animates in simultaneously with the haptic/confetti beat and the prompt text) → advance loops back to handoff for the next round, or exits to recap once the round count/deck is exhausted. Empty-pack error state is reachable from here with a real recovery path (end session early → recap with existing data).
- *Recap:* loading (brief render state, spinner not a blank flash) → success (card preview + working ShareLink) → failure (fallback text summary + retry, never a dead end).

**Recap card visual composition (locked this round):** reads top-to-bottom as a keepsake, not a stat sheet — campfire-gradient background matching the app palette, light title/date framing at top, the standout/tie/low-variance line (per the copy rules locked in the prior phase) as the single largest, most dominant text on the card, small players-list footer beneath it in smaller type. Must be rendered and screenshotted on a physical device before being considered done — Xcode previews alone don't count, consistent with the known gradient/text-layering render-failure risk already flagged in earlier phases.

**Visual direction:** campfire-orange-to-hot-pink gradient wash that shifts subtly by screen context (warmer/orange during setup, hotter/pink during the reveal beat) rather than a static background; bold rounded slab type reserved for names and big state words (PASS TO SAM, YOUR TURN); prompt body text in a calmer, more readable weight so the shouty type stays reserved for state, not content; oversized, near-full-width/full-height-band tap targets for advance/reveal/pass actions.

**Accessibility notes:** contrast-safe design tokens must be verified against the actual gradient stops end-to-end (not just checked at the orange end — saturated pink is where white text is most likely to quietly fail WCAG AA); the two-column header needs a tested wrapping/truncation rule for accessibility-size Dynamic Type (a long name like "ALEXANDRIA" must have a defined behavior, not an assumed fit); VoiceOver reads state in order ("You hold... Target locked...", revealing the name only once actually revealed); the two coming-soon tiles are explicitly announced as unavailable, never as live controls. As already accepted in an earlier phase: this is a structurally sighted, pass-and-play experience, and VoiceOver's job is to correctly announce whichever state is on screen, not to solve the "you must look at the phone to know it's your turn" property of the design.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes cleanly. All five required outputs are present and concrete: screen inventory (six screens/states, explicitly noting no new navigation was added beyond the locked prior-phase set), primary user flow (single linear path, Entry → Reveal → Setup → Round state machine → Recap → Share/replay), state model per screen (each screen's states enumerated, including the reveal-round internal state machine handoff→ready→revealed with explicit trigger/timing behavior), visual direction (gradient-shift-by-context, type hierarchy rules distinguishing state words from prompt body), and accessibility notes (contrast checked against actual gradient stops, not just one end; Dynamic Type wrapping rule for long names; VoiceOver read-order; explicit non-interactive announcement for placeholder tiles).

The round's real disagreement — a dedicated hand-off screen vs. folding pass-ownership into existing round-screen state — was resolved with genuine synthesis, not a coin flip: Claude reframed her own proposal as a state (handoff/ready/revealed) rather than a new destination, which satisfies Codex's "don't add navigation weight for a 12-15 player game" concern while preserving the "unambiguous whose-turn-is-it" cue that's been flagged as the top systems risk since product-research and detailed-discussion. Delayed target reveal is explicitly locked with reasoning tied back to the recap-card-is-the-viral-loop thesis. The recap card layout and physical-device-render requirement is reasonably folded in as settled since nothing contradicts it and it directly answers a standing risk from two phases back.

Consistent with everything locked upstream (six-screen/no-new-navigation constraint, active-player/target rotation rule, `isPackUnlocked` stub, no fake paywall, tie/degenerate recap copy rules, termination-survival persistence bar). A SwiftUI developer has enough here to build the view hierarchy and state machine directly. No repair needed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

title: Design Handoff
output:
Round 2 actually closed the loop. Codex came back and gave a direct, unambiguous answer to what I flagged as open: no external Claude Design handoff before build, build straight into SwiftUI from this spec — matching Claude's position exactly rather than hedging. Claude then sharpened that into something more useful than a flat "no": keep Codex's Claude Design prompt in the final output, but relabel it honestly as an optional post-build visual-QA tool (a second opinion on palette/type pairing after the app exists) rather than a pre-build gate — since the actual risk in this app (handoff timing, reveal-haptic sync, contrast through animation, ImageRenderer on-device behavior) can only be verified by running real SwiftUI, not by reviewing a static comp. Nobody's contesting that framing.

Two smaller things surfaced this round that are genuine improvements, not new fights: Claude caught a real gap in Codex's recap spec — a "zero prompts played" case (deck empties on round one) is different from the already-locked "low-variance/2-player" degenerate case, since there's no moment to surface at all, and needs its own honest copy rather than being forced into the funniest-moment template. And there's one small unresolved implementation detail — Codex wants a visible "duplicate input blocked" toast on the double-tap guard, Claude argues that's wrong (a working guard should be silent; telling the user about a tap they didn't perceive as a problem just surfaces the implementation detail as confusion) — Codex hasn't responded to that specific point yet, but it's a minor UI-polish call, not a structural disagreement, and Claude's reasoning is the stronger one given the guard's whole point is to make the second tap a no-op.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**External design decision:** No external Claude Design handoff before build. This app's real risk lives in state-machine and motion-timing behavior (handoff auto-advance feel, reveal/haptic sync, contrast through animation, `ImageRenderer` output on a physical device) that a static mockup cannot validate — only running SwiftUI can. Build directly from this document. Codex's Claude Design prompt (reproduced below) is preserved, but explicitly as an *optional post-build visual-QA tool* — a second opinion on palette/type pairing after the app is running — not a step the build waits on. The physical-device screenshot checkpoint happens early, after the entry and setup screens are wired, not after the whole app is done, so a bad palette call is cheap to catch.

**Screens (five, one linear stack — no side navigation, no interactive back-swipe anywhere in the session flow):**

1. **Entry** — one full-color tappable "Reveal" tile; "Voting/Ranking" and "Rapid Choice" render as flat, desaturated, non-interactive "Coming soon" labels (no border, no chevron, no press state). States: normal, brief skeleton-shimmer loading if content hydration is delayed, empty/error overlay with Retry if the pack fails to load. Reveal tile gets a small spring press-scale on tap.
2. **Player setup** — "Set up your circle," numbered name rows, Add Player control (visible until 15), Continue anchored at bottom. Duplicate names allowed; blank/whitespace names rejected with an inline error on the offending row only (no toast/banner). Continue is disabled with a stated reason under 2 or over 15 players. No auto-fix suggestions — block and explain.
3. **Round** — one screen, one internal state machine, no new navigation: **handoff** (full-bleed "PASS TO [NAME]," auto-advances ~1.5s with a haptic buzz, tap-anywhere skips early) → **ready** (two-column header: "YOU HOLD [NAME]" fully shown, "TONIGHT'S SPOTLIGHT: ?" hidden, one large reveal control) → **revealed** (target name and prompt animate in together with the haptic/confetti beat, holds briefly before advance is available). Target is never shown before the reveal tap — that delay is the mechanic, not decoration. Empty/exhausted pack mid-session exits early into recap with whatever data exists, never a dead screen.
4. **Recap** — card preview first, then Share/Retry/Play again/Exit. Card composition: gradient background matching app palette → small title/date framing → the standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data line as the single largest, most dominant text → small players-list footer. Four defined copy cases, not three: clear single standout (named directly), tie (all tied players named together, never silently broken), low-variance/degenerate session (2-player or under ~4 distinct targets — switches to the funniest specific moment instead of a fake stat), and now **zero-data** (pack emptied before any round completed — honest copy acknowledging nothing happened, e.g. "the fire didn't quite catch this time," not a forced fit into the funniest-moment template). Loading state is a real spinner, not a blank flash; failure state is a readable fallback text summary plus Retry, with share of the text fallback still available — never a dead end. A subtle pre-share note states that names are user-entered and will leave the device via sharing, since that's an intentional, accepted tradeoff.
5. **Error/recovery states** — attached to setup (validation), round (empty pack), and recap (render failure) as described above; exit/play-again always returns to a fresh setup screen, never an auto-loaded roster.

**Navigation and state architecture:** a single root route enum (`entry`, `setup`, `session`, `recap`) drives the view — not a `NavigationStack` with pushed routes, since the default edge-swipe-to-go-back has to be explicitly killed everywhere in the session flow (a mid-round back-swipe is a state nobody designed for). The round screen's `handoff`/`ready`/`revealed` phase lives as persisted model state (SwiftData), not transient `@State` — that's what makes true termination-and-relaunch recovery real rather than assumed. Every transition (handoff shown, reveal tapped, round advanced) triggers an explicit synchronous save immediately after the state mutation. On launch, an explicit "resume in-progress session" path routes straight back into the exact persisted phase — treated as its own tested code path, since it's the one the happy-path demo never exercises.

**Target rotation (already locked, restated for the builder):** active player rotates in strict round-robin order; target excludes the active player and whoever was targeted the immediately prior round, then picks whoever's gone longest since last being targeted. Collapses to strict alternation at 2 players, one forced legal candidate at 3, and spreads out cleanly above that — no magic window number.

**Double-tap / phantom-transition guard:** silent by design — the reveal/advance control disables itself the instant it's tapped, so there is nothing left to tap a second time; no "duplicate input blocked" toast or in-screen message, since a working guard means the user never perceives a second tap happened in the first place. Same guard scope covers the confetti/haptic trigger.

**`isIdleTimerDisabled`:** scoped via `.onAppear`/`.onDisappear` on the round-container view only, explicitly reset to `false` on every exit path including the early-exit-on-empty-pack route.

**Recap card rendering:** `RecapCardView` rendered at a fixed, screen-independent frame via `ImageRenderer` at 3x scale, snapshotted only after a confirmed layout pass (not off a currently-on-screen view) — must be rendered and screenshotted on a physical device before being called done; Xcode previews don't count.

**Design tokens:**
- Gradient stops: Ember Orange `#FF8E3C` → Coral `#FF5A78` → Hot Pink `#D94AF0`, with interpolation weight shifting by context — setup skews warmer/orange, the reveal beat skews hotter/pink.
- A first-class `scrim` token (a fixed-opacity dark overlay, tuned against the actual pink stop) sits behind every white-text zone structurally, so WCAG AA contrast is guaranteed by the system rather than checked screen-by-screen.
- Text: `#FFFDF8` on gradient, `#F4F3F8` on dark backgrounds, muted `#FFD5C8`; semantic `success`/`warning`/`danger` tokens defined.
- Typography: SF Rounded, heavy/black weight reserved for state words and names (PASS TO SAM, YOU HOLD); calmer regular-weight rounded body for prompt text; two-column header uses `minimumScaleFactor` plus a defined two-line max with truncation, tested against a long all-caps name at the largest accessibility Dynamic Type size.
- Spacing/shape: radius family 18/24/30; vertical rhythm 14/18/24/28; tap targets minimum 44pt everywhere, 48×56 (or near-full-width bands) for core round-screen actions.
- Motion: ~260ms spring for handoff→ready, ~200ms tighter spring for the reveal-name beat, confetti capped at 500–700ms and decorative/non-blocking, haptic fired on the exact frame the reveal animation starts (not before, not after).

**Component inventory:** `CampfireGradientSurface(context:)`, `ModeTile` (active/non-interactive variants), `NameRow` with inline validation, `AddPlayerButton`, `RoundStateHeader`/`TwoColumnStateHeader` (owns target-visibility policy and truncation), `HandoffBanner` (cancellable `.task`-scoped auto-advance timer), `RevealActionButton`/`RevealContentCard` (owns the silent double-tap guard), `ConfettiOverlay` (hand-rolled via `Canvas`/`TimelineView`, no third-party dependency), `RecapCardView` + `RecapCardRenderer`, `ShareRow`/`ShareLinkButton`, and one reusable `ErrorPanel`/`ErrorStateView(message:, retryAction:)` used everywhere an error state is reachable instead of bespoke implementations.

**Accessibility:** every interactive control ≥44pt; VoiceOver reads role-then-state in order ("You hold [name]... target hidden..." then, only after reveal, the target name); the two coming-soon tiles are always announced as unavailable via `accessibilityHint("Not available yet")`, never as live controls; long names get a tested wrap/truncation rule at accessibility Dynamic Type sizes rather than an assumed fit; every error state includes accessible recovery-action text. This remains a structurally sighted, pass-and-play experience by design — VoiceOver's job is to correctly announce whichever state is on screen, not to solve the "you must look at the phone to know it's your turn" property of the product.

**Claude Design prompt (kept, relabeled as optional post-build visual-QA only):**
"Design a premium SwiftUI visual direction for a party pass-and-play game called 'Campfire.' Direction: bold, energetic, warm-to-hot gradient, rounded playful geometry, oversized tap targets, physical-phone handoff cues. Reference flow: Entry, Player Setup, Round (handoff → ready → revealed states), Recap, exit/replay. Focus: one working mode (Reveal/confession), two non-interactive coming-soon placeholders, deterministic turn-role clarity, and a shareable recap card as the emotional payoff. Large, legible text at accessibility sizes, rounded slab-like type, strict contrast against gradient stops. Confetti-style reveal moment synced to haptics; clear loading/error/success handling on setup and recap. Include reusable component names, color tokens, typography scale, spacing scale, motion timings, and accessibility states (voice order, 44pt targets, VoiceOver naming)."
