# campfire — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:34:47._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Here is a direct, implementation-ready Campfire design handoff for this phase.

Campfire is a pass-and-play party object, not a menu app, so every state must make the room understand “who holds the phone now” in one glance and keep momentum under real social conditions (group of 2–15, ambient noise, one hand, one eye on the glass).

The entire flow stays linear in one stack: entry → setup → round loop → recap → share/exit. No side drawers, no mid-session edits, no secondary navigation.

Core screen list and flow  
- Entry screen: 3 tiles for “Reveal,” “Voting/Ranking (Coming soon),” and “Rapid Choice (Coming soon).” Reveal is the only tappable mode.  
- Player setup screen: player count + player name entry, minimum 2 / maximum 15, duplicate names allowed, whitespace-only names blocked in place.  
- Round screen (one screen, three internal states): handoff → ready → revealed.  
- Recap screen: rendered recap card preview + ShareLink + actions.  
- Empty-state / error overlays are attached to setup, round, and recap (no dead ends).  
- Exit/play-again returns to setup (fresh), never resume old roster automatically in this slice.

Screen-by-screen specification

Entry screen  
Intent: start in <1s and communicate exactly what works.  
Layout: full-screen gradient background with a short value statement at top, then one row of three mode tiles stacked if needed by width.  
Components:  
- App title + one-line promise chip  
- Primary mode tile: Reveal (full color, high-emphasis container)  
- Two secondary tiles: plain “Coming soon” labels only, no chevrons, no press state, no lock icon  
- Subtle copyright/privacy line at very bottom (“No accounts, no network needed.”)
States:  
- Normal: Reveal enabled, placeholders shown as non-interactive  
- Loading: only if content hydration is delayed (brief skeleton shimmer over tiles)  
- Empty: no prompt pack loaded → show overlay with reason + Retry  
- Error: repository read/write failure → show retry + close app/exit button  
Accessibility:  
- Reveal tile announced as “Reveal: available”  
- Placeholder tiles announced with `accessibilityHint("Not available yet")` and disabled status  
- All targets >= 44pt and high contrast text against gradient stop checks  
Motion:  
- Tile press scale 0.98 with spring release on Reveal  
- No extra transitions beyond needed; this screen should feel immediate

Player setup screen  
Intent: minimize friction and prevent invalid sessions from becoming edge-case crashes later.  
Layout:  
- Header: “Set up your circle” + small helper (“Add 2–15 names”)  
- Dynamic list of name fields with numbered chips  
- Add player button visible until max reached  
- Inline validation row under each invalid entry  
- Primary Continue button anchored bottom  
Components:  
- NameFieldRow (label + text field + inline error)  
- Add Player control  
- Player count chip / constraint line  
- Continue CTA
States:  
- Default: 2 empty required rows plus “Add player”  
- Validation: any blank/whitespace name shows inline error on that row; continue disabled with reason text  
- Over-limit (16th+) row disables Add player and disables Continue  
- Saved-progress? In this slice, no autosave of names across app relaunch beyond current session object  
- Error: keyboard focus / system focus failure -> fallback message + “Try again”  
Accessibility:  
- Each row has “Player N name, required” label and inline error announcement  
- Dynamic Type: allow line wrap in helper text, fixed minimum hit area for buttons  
Motion:  
- Continue enables with a small “ready” pulse and light haptic  
- Failed validation shakes only the invalid field row, not entire form

Round screen (single screen, internal state machine)  
Intent: make turn ownership unmistakable and keep reveal emotional.  
State machine:  
- Handoff: dominant full-screen moment “PASS TO [Name]” + auto-advance after ~1.5s with short medium haptic; any tap skips early  
- Ready: two-column header across top  
  - Left: “YOU HOLD” + active player name  
  - Right: “TONIGHT’S SPOTLIGHT: ?” (name hidden)  
  - One full-width reveal button (or large tap region)  
- Revealed: target name animates in, prompt body fades/scales in, confetti burst + haptic, then lock reveal to this state for a beat  
- Advance: pressing Continue moves to next handoff or recap if done  
Components:  
- Large state header (dominant typography)  
- Prompt card surface with high-contrast body text  
- Primary big button (full-width, near full-height band on narrow phones)  
- Subtle round progress indicator (round X of Y) if known  
- Debug overlay in dev builds (optional) for active/target/tally counters  
States:  
- Normal three states above  
- Empty pack during session: immediate switch to end-of-session path with “Ran out of prompts” banner and recap fallback  
- Render/de-sync guard: if same-round duplicate input detected, block transition and show toast-like in-screen message  
- Recovery: one clear forward path always leads to recap  
Accessibility:  
- Header order: active holder first, then target state, then prompt action  
- On active turn, VoiceOver should announce both role + action in one read  
- Target name is not spoken in ready state, only after reveal  
Motion:  
- Handoff and reveal transitions use spring easing and light bounce; no endless choreography  
- Confetti-like effect should be decorative and bounded (short duration, no lag)  
Persistence rule reflected in UI: state advances only at explicit tap/timeout checkpoints, never continuously on drag

Recap screen  
Intent: produce one social object worth sending, not a scoreboard dump.  
Layout:  
- Rendered card preview top, share row bottom  
- Action row: Share, Retry render, Play again, Exit  
- Recap card composition locked into hierarchy  
  - Top banner with session title + timestamp  
  - Dominant headline line = standout/tie/funniest-moment sentence  
  - Secondary mini list of players + total targets (small)  
Components:  
- RecapCardView (rendered image target)  
- ShareLink button  
- Fallback summary text card  
States:  
- Loading: short spinner over muted background while rendering card image  
- Success: shareable image preview available  
- Failure: render error message + fallback text card + Retry  
- Empty: if zero prompts consumed, show “No prompts played yet” and still allow share as “no-show” summary  
Accessibility:  
- Summary headline announced first when screen opens  
- Share button labeled with current party title (“Share Campfire recap for …”)  
- Ensure fallback text is readable and in semantic order  
Motion:  
- Gentle appear-in of card, no complex transitions

Design tokens (implement in a shared theme file)

Color  
- `campfire.gradient.start`: `#FF8E3C`  
- `campfire.gradient.mid`: `#FF5A78`  
- `campfire.gradient.end`: `#D94AF0`  
- `campfire.background.secondary`: `#1A0F19` for dark mode wash  
- `text.primary.onGradient`: `#FFFDF8`  
- `text.primary.onDark`: `#F4F3F8`  
- `text.muted`: `#FFD5C8`  
- `success`: `#3AE29A`  
- `warning`: `#FFC96F`  
- `danger`: `#FF6A6A`  
- Maintain minimum contrast at all gradient points; avoid pure white at the brightest pink stop unless in shadowed bubble.

Typography  
- Primary/identity: rounded, bold slab-feel type via `.system(.title, design: .rounded, weight: .heavy)` and `.system(.largeTitle, design: .rounded, weight: .bold)`  
- Body: `.system(.body, design: .rounded, weight: .medium)`  
- Prompt copy: `.system(.title3, weight: .regular, design: .rounded)`  
- Use uppercase only for command words (PASS TO, YOUR TURN), not for all body text

Spacing and shape  
- Radius family: 18, 24, 30  
- Primary vertical rhythm: 14/18/24/28  
- Tap target: minimum 48x56 for core actions, >=44 in all states

Animation and feedback  
- 260ms spring for screen transitions  
- 80ms quick press affordance  
- Confetti burst at reveal: 500–700ms max; deterministic on first reveal press only  
- Hard rule: reveal transition should fire once per round even on aggressive tapping; add transition lock + de-bounce window

Component inventory  
- `CampfireGradientSurface`  
- `ModeTile` (reveal/coming-soon variants)  
- `PlayerNameRow` with inline validation  
- `RoundStateHeader` with active/target columns and overflow strategy  
- `RoundActionButton` (large primary action)  
- `PromptBodyCard`  
- `RecapCardRenderer` + `RecapCardFallback`  
- `ShareRow` with ShareLink and secondary actions  
- `StateBanner` reusable empty/loading/error banner

Interaction and reliability notes that must survive  
- Active player and target must be visibly separated at all times; never show only small text for state  
- Target is never equal to active, by rule; previous target excluded before least-recently-targeted selection  
- Reveal must not double-trigger on rapid taps  
- Round progress is stateful and checkpointed every transition so termination/relaunch can recover  
- Coming-soon content never reads like an affordance or paywall today

Accessibility constraints that are mandatory, not optional  
- All interactive controls >=44pt  
- Dynamic Type tested in large accessibility presets, with truncation policy for long names (left column may wrap to 2 lines, right column clamps to 2 lines with ellipsis)  
- Explicit labels for round states and button purpose  
- Distinct non-interactive status on disabled CTAs  
- Error states must be reachable and include recovery action text

Privacy and data handling that impact UI wording  
- On recap card screen, show a subtle pre-share note that shared images are intentional and include names, so users understand that share leaves device  
- No profile/profile-like telemetry surfaced; share is user-initiated only

Biggest risk I see in this direction  
The same one that kills party apps: ambiguous turn ownership. If “who holds” and “who’s target” are not visually and temporally distinct, the social loop fails in real rooms before any logic bug appears. Preserve the two-column dominant header and the hidden-target reveal beat exactly as specified.

If this is handed to external Claude Design, upload/use this concise prompt  
Design a premium SwiftUI visual direction for a party pass-and-play game called “Campfire.” Direction: bold, energetic, warm-to-hot gradient, rounded playful geometry, oversized tap targets, physical-phone handoff cues. Build a 6-screen flow: Entry, Player Setup, Round (handoff → ready → revealed states), Recap, and exit/replay. Focus: one working mode (Reveal/confession), two non-interactive coming-soon placeholders, deterministic turn-role clarity, and shareable recap card as the emotional payoff. Keep text large and legible at accessibility sizes, use rounded slab-like type, and ensure strict contrast against gradient stops. Add confetti-style reveal moment synced to haptics and a clear loading/loading-error-success handling on setup and recap. Include an implementation-oriented style brief with reusable component names, color tokens, typography scale, spacing scale, motion timings (spring/alpha/scale), and accessibility states (voice order, 44pt targets, VoiceOver naming).

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Coming into this phase with the frontend-engineer hat on, my job is to take everything the design_discussion phase already locked — six screens/states, the handoff→ready→revealed round machine, delayed target reveal, the recap card layout — and turn it into something a SwiftUI builder can actually implement without inventing anything mid-build. Let me lay out where I'd land.

On navigation architecture: I don't think this should be a `NavigationStack` with pushed routes. A pushed stack gives you an interactive back-swipe gesture for free, and that's actively dangerous here — someone swiping back mid-round would put the app in a state nobody designed for (round in progress, but nav says you're on the previous screen). I'd model this as a single root enum (`AppRoute: .entry, .setup, .session, .recap`) driving a `switch` in the root view, with the round screen itself owning its own internal `RoundPhase` enum (`.handoff, .ready, .revealed`) as `@State`/persisted model state, not as separate navigation destinations. That was already the design decision from the last phase; I'm just flagging the implementation consequence — whoever builds this needs to explicitly disable interactive pop everywhere in the session flow, not just "not add" back buttons, because SwiftUI's edge-swipe-to-go-back is enabled by default even with no visible back button.

On persistence and the state machine, this is where I want to be the most concrete, because "checkpoint at every transition" is easy to say and easy to half-implement. I'd model this with SwiftData: a `Session` model holding an ordered `players: [Player]`, `currentRoundIndex`, `activePlayerIndex`, `roundPhase` (the handoff/ready/revealed enum, persisted, not just in-memory `@State` — this is the detail that actually makes termination-survival real, because if `roundPhase` lives only in a SwiftUI `@State` var, relaunching after a force-quit will always drop you back to some default phase instead of the exact one you were in), a `targetHistory` array (needed for the least-recently-targeted rule to survive relaunch), and a `tally`. Every transition — handoff shown, reveal tapped, round advanced — needs an explicit synchronous `try? modelContext.save()` right after the state mutation, not a reliance on SwiftData's autosave, because autosave timing is not guaranteed to run before a kill signal. I'd also flag: on launch, the app needs a real "is there an in-progress session" check that routes straight into the session at the exact persisted phase, and that path needs its own test — it's a distinct code path from "starting fresh," and it's exactly the path that's easiest to leave unbuilt because the happy-path demo never exercises it.

The double-tap guard needs to be a first-class piece of state, not an afterthought — I'd add an `isTransitioning: Bool` (or make the phase transition itself atomic — set phase, save, then re-enable input) and have the reveal/advance button's action check-and-set that flag before doing anything, with the button itself also disabled while true so there's a visual affordance, not just a silent no-op tap. Same guard needs to cover the confetti/haptic trigger specifically, since that's the thing that's most visible if it fires twice.

For `isIdleTimerDisabled`, I'd scope it with `.onAppear`/`.onDisappear` on the round-container view rather than scattering toggles across child views, and explicitly reset it to `false` on every exit path from that container — including the early-exit-on-empty-pack path, which is easy to miss since it's not the "normal" exit.

On the recap card render, since this has already been flagged twice as a known failure mode: I'd render the card via a dedicated `RecapCardView` sized to a fixed frame (not dependent on the visible screen's layout pass) and snapshot it with `ImageRenderer(content:)` at 3x scale, invoked only after confirming the view has actually laid out — a common cause of blank/clipped `ImageRenderer` output is snapshotting before the gradient/text views have gone through a layout pass, so I'd render it into an offscreen container with an explicit size rather than relying on a currently-on-screen view. This has to be verified on-device with an actual saved screenshot before it's called done, not just "the code compiles."

On the design tokens, since the phase rules want values not adjectives: I'd lock the gradient as three stops — Ember Orange, Coral, Hot Pink — with the interpolation weight shifting per context (setup screens weighted toward Ember/Coral, the revealed-state confetti beat weighted toward Coral/Hot Pink), and I want a `scrim` token as a first-class part of the system, not a per-screen judgment call: a fixed-opacity black overlay (I'd propose something in the 25-35% range, tuned once against the actual pink stop and then reused everywhere) sitting behind any white text zone, so contrast is guaranteed structurally instead of being something each screen has to individually pass. That's the practical fix for the WCAG-AA-at-the-pink-end risk that's been flagged twice now — bake it into the token system so it can't be forgotten screen by screen. Typography: SF Rounded (`.fontDesign(.rounded)`), heavy/black weight reserved for state words and names (PASS TO SAM, YOU HOLD), a calmer regular-weight system font for prompt body text, and an explicit truncation/line-limit rule for the two-column header — I'd say `minimumScaleFactor` plus a defined two-line max with tail truncation for the header specifically, tested against a long all-caps name at the largest accessibility Dynamic Type size, since "ALEXANDRIA" at AX5 in a two-column layout will not fit at any font size that's still legible without a defined fallback.

Component inventory I'd want locked: `GradientBackground(context:)` (drives the contextual warm/hot shift), `TwoColumnStateHeader` (handles the hide/reveal of the target name and the truncation rule above), `HandoffBanner` (auto-advance timer + tap-anywhere-to-skip, needs a `Task`-based timer that's cancelled if the user taps early — another place a stray uncancelled `Task` could leak state if not handled via `.task` scoped to the phase), `RevealButton` (owns the double-tap guard), `ConfettiOverlay` (I'd build this with `Canvas` + `TimelineView(.animation)` rather than pulling in a third-party confetti package, consistent with the "no third-party dependency unless it materially reduces risk" rule — a burst of 30-40 particles with random velocity/rotation is genuinely simple to hand-roll and keeps this fully first-party), `RecapCardView`, `ShareLinkButton`, and a single reusable `ErrorStateView(message:, retryAction:)` used for the empty-pack and render-failure cases so error handling doesn't get bespoke-implemented three different times.

Motion: spring-based throughout, not linear easing — something like `.spring(response: 0.4, dampingFraction: 0.75)` for the handoff-to-ready transition and a slightly snappier `.spring(response: 0.3, dampingFraction: 0.65)` for the reveal-name-appears beat, with the haptic (`UIImpactFeedbackGenerator(style: .heavy)` or `.notification(.success)`, whichever tests better on-device) fired at the exact frame the reveal animation starts, not before and not after — a haptic that fires before the name is visible reads as broken, not exciting.

On the external-design question: my read is we should not hand this off to an external design tool. This app's entire risk surface is state-machine and motion-timing behavior — the handoff auto-advance, the delayed reveal, the contrast-safe scrim, the confetti-haptic sync — none of which a static Claude Design mockup can actually represent or validate. A picture of the reveal screen tells you nothing about whether the double-tap guard works or whether the gradient scrim holds contrast through the animation. I'd rather this handoff (with the concrete tokens and component list above) go straight to SwiftUI implementation, verified on a physical device, than go through an external design pass that produces static comps needing re-translation back into this same state machine anyway. The risk I'd flag on my own recommendation: skipping external design means nobody with a dedicated visual-design eye pressure-tests the palette/type pairing before code exists, so if we go this route, whoever builds it should still produce and screenshot the actual running screens early, specifically to catch a palette or type-scale mistake before it's threaded through the whole state machine.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at round 1, Codex and Claude both did real, substantial work rather than just restating prior phases — and they're not fighting so much as operating at different altitudes that mostly stack cleanly on top of each other.

Codex delivered the screen-by-screen spec the phase rubric actually asks for: layout intent, components, state variants, accessibility, and motion notes for all five screens, plus a full token set (named hex values for the gradient, type scale, spacing/radius scale, animation timings) and a component inventory. Claude took that same locked screen/state model and pushed it down to the implementation layer — how state should actually be modeled (a root route enum instead of `NavigationStack` to kill the dangerous default back-swipe mid-round), how persistence has to work concretely (`roundPhase` and `targetHistory` living in SwiftData, not `@State`, with synchronous saves on every transition so termination-survival is real rather than assumed), how the double-tap guard and idle-timer scoping should be structured, and a specific fix for the contrast risk that's been flagged twice now (a first-class `scrim` token baked into the token system, not a per-screen judgment call). None of that contradicts Codex's spec — it's the engineering translation of it, and if anything it tightens two things Codex left softer (the gradient tokens, the long-name truncation rule).

The one place they genuinely diverge and haven't reconciled yet: Codex closed with a ready-to-use external Claude Design prompt, framed as "if this is handed to external design, use this." Claude closed by explicitly arguing *against* going external at all — her point is that this app's real risk (handoff timing, delayed reveal, contrast-through-animation, double-tap suppression) is state-machine and motion behavior that a static mockup literally can't represent, so she wants this to go straight to SwiftUI with an early on-device screenshot pass instead. Those aren't necessarily incompatible (Codex's prompt is conditional, not a recommendation), but nobody has actually said out loud "here's the phase's decision: build direct, no external design pass" versus "here's the prompt, use it." That's the one thing this phase needs to close explicitly, since the phase's own acceptance checklist requires either a design prompt *or* an explicit no-external-design call — not both left dangling.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex to respond directly to Claude's case against external design and the two of them to land on one explicit answer: build straight into SwiftUI with an early physical-device screenshot checkpoint (Claude's position), or actually route this through external Claude Design first. Everything else — screen specs, state model, tokens, component inventory, accessibility, motion — is already in strong enough shape to lock as-is.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Codex and Claude are actually very close here, and I’m going to keep that line: what we have is right for this slice, but we should stop dithering on the design handoff and lock one implementable path now. I disagree with any external design handoff for Campfire in this phase. Claude is right: a static design pass can’t prove the handoff timing, the double-tap lock, reveal suppression, or relaunch-resume behavior, and those are the actual risk drivers in a room. Codex’s concern about speed and single-surface proof is also valid, and this exact point pushes us toward implementation-first with a strict spec, not a mockup-first detour.

So: no external design handoff in this phase. Build direct in SwiftUI from this screen contract.

Entry screen is the first ten-second promise: one full-width “Reveal” tile plus two desaturated plain “Voting/Ranking (Coming soon)” and “Rapid Choice (Coming soon)” labels that look visually like labels, not controls. Reveal is the only tappable action; everything else is announced as unavailable via `accessibilityHint("Not available yet")` and reduced opacity. This matters more than beauty: if the group opens app in a lull, they should not spend time trying to tap fake controls. I keep Codex’s cold-start claim and add one rule from Claude’s UX point: the app should animate into the active tile state immediately with a tiny spring press, then sit in high-contrast reading mode. Empty/loading/errors belong here too because content hydration can fail even at launch: skeleton tile shimmer while packs hydrate, empty prompt-pack warning with Retry/Back out action.

Setup screen is where we prevent avoidable breakage. Keep it brutally simple: two-column-ish vertical stack with a “Set up your circle” header, player count helper (“2–15”), numbered name rows, and one clear Add Player control until max. Duplicate names are allowed; blank/whitespace names are not. The inline error is where it belongs: each bad row shows a short inline message so hosts can fix quickly. Continue button is disabled with an explicit reason if fewer than 2 or more than 15. I disagree with any auto-fix suggestions here; just block and explain, because this is party context and ambiguity creates argument. The next state is deterministic: once valid, move straight into session flow and lock this exact roster for the run.

Round screen stays one route, three internal states, not new navigation. I agree with the compromise from round 2: state machine `handoff -> ready -> revealed` inside the same screen gives us the “who holds it now” clarity without adding clicks every turn. Handoff state is dominant and very clear: full-bleed “PASS TO [NAME]” and a subtle timer to auto-advance after ~1.5s with a short haptic, with tap-anywhere-to-skip so impatient rooms don’t feel stalled. Ready state shows the two-column header where both roles are unmistakable (“YOU HOLD [NAME]” left, “TONIGHT’S SPOTLIGHT: ?” right) and one large reveal control. Here is non-negotiable: target remains hidden until the reveal action; the reveal beat is the social mechanism, not the ready UI. Revealed state animates in with bounce+scale and confetti beat synchronized to haptics, then holds visibly for a beat so people see the result before continue. No double scoring: transition guard must be active (button lock/debounce window) from tap start to completion. Round completion increments exactly once, checkpoints to persistence, then goes handoff of next active player or recap when exhausted. This is where the old rotation math lands: active rotates in strict order; target selection excludes active and previous target and picks least-recently-targeted with stable tie-break. This is a player experience rule, not just math.

Recap screen is the viral object, so treat it like the product itself, not a stats sheet. Show card preview first, with a short render loading state and always a recovery route: if image rendering fails, present readable fallback summary with retry and still allow copy/share of text as a fallback artifact. I’m pushing hard on copy hierarchy: headline is the moment line (single standout / tie / low-variance funny moment), then a tiny players footer. Session context should include date/title at top in low-contrast tone. Share is a first-class action and should feel easy: one tap to open share sheet. If people ask “share image?” the app should already have made the value obvious with no dead end. Add the pre-share subtle note: names are user-entered and will leave device via sharing; that’s intentional.

Error and recovery architecture is part of the UX, not backend trivia. Empty pack should never crash. If deck empties mid-session, transition to a recap-with-partial-data state, not “stuck.” Render failure has Retry and fallback summary. Resume-from-kill should be an explicit behavior: if app is force-quit and reopened mid-round, restore exact phase, active player, target, current prompt index, and tallies before user resumes.

Navigation is root-state driven, not `NavigationStack` with back-swipes. That’s one of those “looks fine in local test, fails at parties” points. A single root enum (`entry`, `setup`, `session`, `recap`) with explicit disable-back transitions plus full control over lifecycle reduces accidental exits and makes resume logic consistent. This is the builder-safe architecture.

Design system I’m locking now is purpose-built, not decorative. Base gradient is warm-to-hot but never flat: setup uses stronger orange, active reveal leans toward pink for emotional beat. Keep a shared scrim token to guarantee contrast at high pink points. Rounded slab-like identity for command/state words (`PASS TO`, `YOU HOLD`, `TONIGHT’S SPOTLIGHT`), and calmer rounded body for prompts. Minimum tap targets for core controls are near-full-width bands on narrow phones; every control >=44pt minimum, and at least 48x56 in round/reveal actions. For overflow at accessibility sizes, left column may wrap to two lines; right column is max two lines with truncation so it never hides active role. Dynamic Type is tested not just conceptually but as a behavior rule.

Interaction and motion: springy and short, not theatrical. Handoff → ready uses a 260ms spring, reveal beat uses tighter spring around 200ms with a capped confetti burst (500–700ms), and everything is deterministic-once-per-round. No persistent loops. Haptic runs on confirmed reveal transition only. Confetti is decorative and bounded, never blocking the screen.

Component inventory for implementation: `CampfireGradientSurface(context:)`, `ModeTile` with active/noninteractive variants, `NameRow` with inline validation, `AddPlayerButton`, `RoundStateHeader` with target-visibility policy and truncation logic, `HandoffBanner`, `RevealActionButton`, `RevealContentCard`, `StateBadge` and reusable `ErrorPanel`, `RecapCardView`, `RecapCardRenderer`, `ShareRow`, and optional `DebugStatePanel` behind flag.

Accessibility constraints are mandatory and tied to specific behavior: VoiceOver should announce “you hold [name], target hidden” in handoff/ready and reveal target only after transition. The two coming-soon tiles must be read as unavailable. Empty/error states always have recovery action text and accessible labels. No fake “you can still tap this later” messaging.

For this phase’s required output decision: no external Claude Design pass. We already have a concrete implementation-first artifact, and the real risks are in turn logic, transition debouncing, persistence boundaries, and reveal timing. This is the kind of thing only executable SwiftUI behavior can validate.

If we build this exactly as written, the team gets a party-ready MVP with the full social moment and no hidden ambiguity.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Codex's spec is genuinely good and I want to say that plainly before I push on the one open thread — the recap screen's "Empty: if zero prompts consumed, show 'No prompts played yet' and still allow share as a 'no-show' summary" is a state I hadn't covered and it's a real gap in what I wrote: someone could theoretically hit the empty-pack error on the very first round and bail straight to recap with literally nothing played, and that's different from the "low-variance 2-player" degenerate case we locked earlier — this is zero data, not thin data. That needs its own copy, not a fallback to the funniest-moment template, since there's no moment to surface. I'd fold that in as a fourth recap case: clear standout / tie / low-variance-funniest-moment / zero-data-no-show, and the zero-data copy should probably just be honest about it — something like "the fire didn't quite catch this time" rather than pretending there's content. Small thing, but it's exactly the kind of edge case that gets improvised badly under time pressure if it's not named now.

I also want to flag one soft disagreement with Codex's round screen spec: the "Render/de-sync guard: if same-round duplicate input detected, block transition and show toast-like in-screen message" — I'd cut the toast. A visible "duplicate input blocked" message is telling the room about an implementation detail they'll never understand or care about ("what does that mean, I just tapped it"). The double-tap guard I described last round should be silent — the button disables itself the instant it's tapped, so there's physically nothing to tap again, and the guard exists so a stray second event can't fire logic, not so the user gets told off for tapping twice. If the guard is doing its job, the user never even perceives that a second tap happened. Surfacing a message here would actually make the bug more visible than the bug itself.

Now, the actual thing the coordinator flagged, and I want to be direct about it: I don't think Codex's prompt and my "skip external design" position are as reconcilable as "his is just conditional" makes it sound, because the phase rubric forces a real choice — a build agent reading this handoff needs to know whether it's building from this document or waiting on design assets to come back from somewhere else. Leaving it as "here's a prompt, use it if you want to" is itself a decision by default, and it's the wrong one for this specific app.

Here's my actual case, sharpened from last round: everything that makes Campfire good or bad lives in timing and state, not in a single frame you could screenshot and hand to a designer. The delayed-reveal beat only works if the haptic lands on the exact frame the name becomes visible — that's not describable in a mockup, it's a number in a `.spring()` modifier and a call site for `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator`. The handoff auto-advance only works if 1.5 seconds actually feels right in someone's hand at a real party, not in a still image. The contrast-safe scrim I proposed only proves itself by rendering the actual gradient through the actual animation and checking it doesn't wash out mid-transition. A Claude Design output, even a good one, would give us a beautiful static picture of the "revealed" state and tell us nothing about whether the three-state machine holds together, whether the double-tap guard is silent the way it needs to be, or whether the recap card's `ImageRenderer` snapshot actually renders on a physical device instead of blank — which, again, has been flagged twice in this project's own history as a real, not hypothetical, failure mode. Every one of those things can only be verified by writing the SwiftUI and running it. So sending this handoff externally doesn't save build risk, it just delays discovering the risk that actually matters here.

Where I'll actually move, though: I don't want us to throw away Codex's prompt, because there's a real, narrower use for it that isn't "block on it before building" — if, after we build this, someone wants a second opinion on the visual system specifically (palette, type pairing, whether the gradient feels premium versus garish), that prompt is a genuinely well-written brief to hand over at that point, as a post-build sanity check rather than a pre-build gate. So my proposal to close this out: explicit decision is no external design pass before build — we build directly from this handoff in SwiftUI, with the physical-device screenshot checkpoint I called for last round happening early (after screen 1-2 are wired, not after the whole app is done, so a bad palette call gets caught while it's still cheap to change). Codex's Claude Design prompt gets kept in the final output, but relabeled honestly as an optional later-stage visual-QA tool, not a step this build is waiting on. That's not really a compromise that waters down either position — it's just being honest that the prompt has real value at a different point in the process than "before any code exists," and I think that's a better answer than pretending the two of us actually agree on when external design should happen when we don't yet.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Round 2 actually closed the loop. Codex came back and gave a direct, unambiguous answer to what I flagged as open: no external Claude Design handoff before build, build straight into SwiftUI from this spec — matching Claude's position exactly rather than hedging. Claude then sharpened that into something more useful than a flat "no": keep Codex's Claude Design prompt in the final output, but relabel it honestly as an optional post-build visual-QA tool (a second opinion on palette/type pairing after the app exists) rather than a pre-build gate — since the actual risk in this app (handoff timing, reveal-haptic sync, contrast through animation, ImageRenderer on-device behavior) can only be verified by running real SwiftUI, not by reviewing a static comp. Nobody's contesting that framing.

Two smaller things surfaced this round that are genuine improvements, not new fights: Claude caught a real gap in Codex's recap spec — a "zero prompts played" case (deck empties on round one) is different from the already-locked "low-variance/2-player" degenerate case, since there's no moment to surface at all, and needs its own honest copy rather than being forced into the funniest-moment template. And there's one small unresolved implementation detail — Codex wants a visible "duplicate input blocked" toast on the double-tap guard, Claude argues that's wrong (a working guard should be silent; telling the user about a tap they didn't perceive as a problem just surfaces the implementation detail as confusion) — Codex hasn't responded to that specific point yet, but it's a minor UI-polish call, not a structural disagreement, and Claude's reasoning is the stronger one given the guard's whole point is to make the second tap a no-op.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**External design decision:** No external Claude Design handoff before build. This app's real risk lives in state-machine and motion-timing behavior (handoff auto-advance feel, reveal/haptic sync, contrast through animation, `ImageRenderer` output on a physical device) that a static mockup cannot validate — only running SwiftUI can. Build directly from this document. Codex's Claude Design prompt (reproduced below) is preserved, but explicitly as an *optional post-build visual-QA tool* — a second opinion on palette/type pairing after the app is running — not a step the build waits on. The physical-device screenshot checkpoint happens early, after the entry and setup screens are wired, not after the whole app is done, so a bad palette call is cheap to catch.

**Screens (five, one linear stack — no side navigation, no interactive back-swipe anywhere in the session flow):**

1. **Entry** — one full-color tappable "Reveal" tile; "Voting/Ranking" and "Rapid Choice" render as flat, desaturated, non-interactive "Coming soon" labels (no border, no chevron, no press state). States: normal, brief skeleton-shimmer loading if content hydration is delayed, empty/error overlay with Retry if the pack fails to load. Reveal tile gets a small spring press-scale on tap.
2. **Player setup** — "Set up your circle," numbered name rows, Add Player control (visible until 15), Continue anchored at bottom. Duplicate names allowed; blank/whitespace names rejected with an inline error on the offending row only (no toast/banner). Continue is disabled with a stated reason under 2 or over 15 players. No auto-fix suggestions — block and explain.
3. **Round** — one screen, one internal state machine, no new navigation: **handoff** (full-bleed "PASS TO [NAME]," auto-advances ~1.5s with a haptic buzz, tap-anywhere skips early) → **ready** (two-column header: "YOU HOLD [NAME]" fully shown, "TONIGHT'S SPOTLIGHT: ?" hidden, one large reveal control) → **revealed** (target name and prompt animate in together with the haptic/confetti beat, holds briefly before advance is available). Target is never shown before the reveal tap — that delay is the mechanic, not decoration. Empty/exhausted pack mid-session exits early into recap with whatever data exists, never a dead screen.
4. **Recap** — card preview first, then Share/Retry/Play again/Exit. Card composition: gradient background matching app palette → small title/date framing → the standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data line as the single largest, most dominant text → small players-list footer. Four defined copy cases, not three: clear single standout (named directly), tie (all tied players named together, never silently broken), low-variance/degenerate session (2-player or under ~4 distinct targets — switches to the funniest specific moment instead of a fake stat), and now **zero-data** (pack emptied before any round completed — honest copy acknowledging nothing happened, e.g. "the fire didn't quite catch this time," not a forced fit into the funniest-moment template). Loading state is a real spinner, not a blank flash; failure state is a readable fallback text summary plus Retry, with share of the text fallback still available — never a dead end. A subtle pre-share note states that names are user-entered and will leave the device via sharing, since that's an intentional, accepted tradeoff.
5. **Error/recovery states** — attached to setup (validation), round (empty pack), and recap (render failure) as described above; exit/play-again always returns to a fresh setup screen, never an auto-loaded roster.

**Navigation and state architecture:** a single root route enum (`entry`, `setup`, `session`, `recap`) drives the view — not a `NavigationStack` with pushed routes, since the default edge-swipe-to-go-back has to be explicitly killed everywhere in the session flow (a mid-round back-swipe is a state nobody designed for). The round screen's `handoff`/`ready`/`revealed` phase lives as persisted model state (SwiftData), not transient `@State` — that's what makes true termination-and-relaunch recovery real rather than assumed. Every transition (handoff shown, reveal tapped, round advanced) triggers an explicit synchronous save immediately after the state mutation. On launch, an explicit "resume in-progress session" path routes straight back into the exact persisted phase — treated as its own tested code path, since it's the one the happy-path demo never exercises.

**Target rotation (already locked, restated for the builder):** active player rotates in strict round-robin order; target excludes the active player and whoever was targeted the immediately prior round, then picks whoever's gone longest since last being targeted. Collapses to strict alternation at 2 players, one forced legal candidate at 3, and spreads out cleanly above that — no magic window number.

**Double-tap / phantom-transition guard:** silent by design — the reveal/advance control disables itself the instant it's tapped, so there is nothing left to tap a second time; no "duplicate input blocked" toast or in-screen message, since a working guard means the user never perceives a second tap happened in the first place. Same guard scope covers the confetti/haptic trigger.

**`isIdleTimerDisabled`:** scoped via `.onAppear`/`.onDisappear` on the round-container view only, explicitly reset to `false` on every exit path including the early-exit-on-empty-pack route.

**Recap card rendering:** `RecapCardView` rendered at a fixed, screen-independent frame via `ImageRenderer` at 3x scale, snapshotted only after a confirmed layout pass (not off a currently-on-screen view) — must be rendered and screenshotted on a physical device before being called done; Xcode previews don't count.

**Design tokens:**
- Gradient stops: Ember Orange `#FF8E3C` → Coral `#FF5A78` → Hot Pink `#D94AF0`, with interpolation weight shifting by context — setup skews warmer/orange, the reveal beat skews hotter/pink.
- A first-class `scrim` token (a fixed-opacity dark overlay, tuned against the actual pink stop) sits behind every white-text zone structurally, so WCAG AA contrast is guaranteed by the system rather than checked screen-by-screen.
- Text: `#FFFDF8` on gradient, `#F4F3F8` on dark backgrounds, muted `#FFD5C8`; semantic `success`/`warning`/`danger` tokens defined.
- Typography: SF Rounded, heavy/black weight reserved for state words and names (PASS TO SAM, YOU HOLD); calmer regular-weight rounded body for prompt text; two-column header uses `minimumScaleFactor` plus a defined two-line max with truncation, tested against a long all-caps name at the largest accessibility Dynamic Type size.
- Spacing/shape: radius family 18/24/30; vertical rhythm 14/18/24/28; tap targets minimum 44pt everywhere, 48×56 (or near-full-width bands) for core round-screen actions.
- Motion: ~260ms spring for handoff→ready, ~200ms tighter spring for the reveal-name beat, confetti capped at 500–700ms and decorative/non-blocking, haptic fired on the exact frame the reveal animation starts (not before, not after).

**Component inventory:** `CampfireGradientSurface(context:)`, `ModeTile` (active/non-interactive variants), `NameRow` with inline validation, `AddPlayerButton`, `RoundStateHeader`/`TwoColumnStateHeader` (owns target-visibility policy and truncation), `HandoffBanner` (cancellable `.task`-scoped auto-advance timer), `RevealActionButton`/`RevealContentCard` (owns the silent double-tap guard), `ConfettiOverlay` (hand-rolled via `Canvas`/`TimelineView`, no third-party dependency), `RecapCardView` + `RecapCardRenderer`, `ShareRow`/`ShareLinkButton`, and one reusable `ErrorPanel`/`ErrorStateView(message:, retryAction:)` used everywhere an error state is reachable instead of bespoke implementations.

**Accessibility:** every interactive control ≥44pt; VoiceOver reads role-then-state in order ("You hold [name]... target hidden..." then, only after reveal, the target name); the two coming-soon tiles are always announced as unavailable via `accessibilityHint("Not available yet")`, never as live controls; long names get a tested wrap/truncation rule at accessibility Dynamic Type sizes rather than an assumed fit; every error state includes accessible recovery-action text. This remains a structurally sighted, pass-and-play experience by design — VoiceOver's job is to correctly announce whichever state is on screen, not to solve the "you must look at the phone to know it's your turn" property of the product.

**Claude Design prompt (kept, relabeled as optional post-build visual-QA only):**
"Design a premium SwiftUI visual direction for a party pass-and-play game called 'Campfire.' Direction: bold, energetic, warm-to-hot gradient, rounded playful geometry, oversized tap targets, physical-phone handoff cues. Reference flow: Entry, Player Setup, Round (handoff → ready → revealed states), Recap, exit/replay. Focus: one working mode (Reveal/confession), two non-interactive coming-soon placeholders, deterministic turn-role clarity, and a shareable recap card as the emotional payoff. Large, legible text at accessibility sizes, rounded slab-like type, strict contrast against gradient stops. Confetti-style reveal moment synced to haptics; clear loading/error/success handling on setup and recap. Include reusable component names, color tokens, typography scale, spacing scale, motion timings, and accessibility states (voice order, 44pt targets, VoiceOver naming)."

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This output fully satisfies the phase rubric. All six required elements are present and concrete: screen-by-screen spec (5 screens, each with explicit states — normal/loading/error where reachable), a genuinely builder-usable design token system (named hex gradient stops, a first-class contrast-guaranteeing scrim token, typography scale with a tested truncation rule for the two-column header, spacing/radius scale, motion timings down to spring response/damping values and haptic-firing frame), a component inventory (12 named reusable views with clear ownership boundaries, e.g. `RevealActionButton` owning the double-tap guard), state/interaction notes (the handoff→ready→revealed machine, target-rotation rule restated for the builder, silent double-tap guard, idle-timer scoping), accessibility requirements (44pt minimums, VoiceOver read-order, Dynamic Type wrap rule, explicit non-interactive announcement for placeholder tiles), and an explicit, reasoned external-design decision (no external pass before build, with Codex's prompt preserved and honestly relabeled as a post-build visual-QA tool rather than left as a dangling conditional).

The phase's central open thread from round 1 — whether to route through external Claude Design or build direct — is resolved with real reasoning tied to this app's specific risk profile (state-machine/motion-timing behavior that a static mockup can't validate), not a coin flip, and both agents converged on it explicitly in round 2. The one remaining nit (toast vs. silent double-tap guard) is correctly scoped by the coordinator as a minor UI-polish call, not a structural disagreement, and the coordinator states which way to build it rather than leaving it ambiguous — so nothing is actually left undecided for the builder. The zero-data recap case Claude caught is folded into the four-case copy model with concrete fallback copy, closing a real gap rather than leaving it implicit.

Everything here is consistent with every prior locked phase (six-screen/no-new-navigation constraint from design_discussion, the active-player/target rotation rule, `isPackUnlocked` stub, termination-survival persistence bar, tie/degenerate recap copy rules). A SwiftUI builder has enough here — specific values, not adjectives — to implement directly. No repair needed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Round 2 actually closed the loop. Codex came back and gave a direct, unambiguous answer to what I flagged as open: no external Claude Design handoff before build, build straight into SwiftUI from this spec — matching Claude's position exactly rather than hedging. Claude then sharpened that into something more useful than a flat "no": keep Codex's Claude Design prompt in the final output, but relabel it honestly as an optional post-build visual-QA tool (a second opinion on palette/type pairing after the app exists) rather than a pre-build gate — since the actual risk in this app (handoff timing, reveal-haptic sync, contrast through animation, ImageRenderer on-device behavior) can only be verified by running real SwiftUI, not by reviewing a static comp. Nobody's contesting that framing.

Two smaller things surfaced this round that are genuine improvements, not new fights: Claude caught a real gap in Codex's recap spec — a "zero prompts played" case (deck empties on round one) is different from the already-locked "low-variance/2-player" degenerate case, since there's no moment to surface at all, and needs its own honest copy rather than being forced into the funniest-moment template. And there's one small unresolved implementation detail — Codex wants a visible "duplicate input blocked" toast on the double-tap guard, Claude argues that's wrong (a working guard should be silent; telling the user about a tap they didn't perceive as a problem just surfaces the implementation detail as confusion) — Codex hasn't responded to that specific point yet, but it's a minor UI-polish call, not a structural disagreement, and Claude's reasoning is the stronger one given the guard's whole point is to make the second tap a no-op.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**External design decision:** No external Claude Design handoff before build. This app's real risk lives in state-machine and motion-timing behavior (handoff auto-advance feel, reveal/haptic sync, contrast through animation, `ImageRenderer` output on a physical device) that a static mockup cannot validate — only running SwiftUI can. Build directly from this document. Codex's Claude Design prompt (reproduced below) is preserved, but explicitly as an *optional post-build visual-QA tool* — a second opinion on palette/type pairing after the app is running — not a step the build waits on. The physical-device screenshot checkpoint happens early, after the entry and setup screens are wired, not after the whole app is done, so a bad palette call is cheap to catch.

**Screens (five, one linear stack — no side navigation, no interactive back-swipe anywhere in the session flow):**

1. **Entry** — one full-color tappable "Reveal" tile; "Voting/Ranking" and "Rapid Choice" render as flat, desaturated, non-interactive "Coming soon" labels (no border, no chevron, no press state). States: normal, brief skeleton-shimmer loading if content hydration is delayed, empty/error overlay with Retry if the pack fails to load. Reveal tile gets a small spring press-scale on tap.
2. **Player setup** — "Set up your circle," numbered name rows, Add Player control (visible until 15), Continue anchored at bottom. Duplicate names allowed; blank/whitespace names rejected with an inline error on the offending row only (no toast/banner). Continue is disabled with a stated reason under 2 or over 15 players. No auto-fix suggestions — block and explain.
3. **Round** — one screen, one internal state machine, no new navigation: **handoff** (full-bleed "PASS TO [NAME]," auto-advances ~1.5s with a haptic buzz, tap-anywhere skips early) → **ready** (two-column header: "YOU HOLD [NAME]" fully shown, "TONIGHT'S SPOTLIGHT: ?" hidden, one large reveal control) → **revealed** (target name and prompt animate in together with the haptic/confetti beat, holds briefly before advance is available). Target is never shown before the reveal tap — that delay is the mechanic, not decoration. Empty/exhausted pack mid-session exits early into recap with whatever data exists, never a dead screen.
4. **Recap** — card preview first, then Share/Retry/Play again/Exit. Card composition: gradient background matching app palette → small title/date framing → the standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data line as the single largest, most dominant text → small players-list footer. Four defined copy cases, not three: clear single standout (named directly), tie (all tied players named together, never silently broken), low-variance/degenerate session (2-player or under ~4 distinct targets — switches to the funniest specific moment instead of a fake stat), and now **zero-data** (pack emptied before any round completed — honest copy acknowledging nothing happened, e.g. "the fire didn't quite catch this time," not a forced fit into the funniest-moment template). Loading state is a real spinner, not a blank flash; failure state is a readable fallback text summary plus Retry, with share of the text fallback still available — never a dead end. A subtle pre-share note states that names are user-entered and will leave the device via sharing, since that's an intentional, accepted tradeoff.
5. **Error/recovery states** — attached to setup (validation), round (empty pack), and recap (render failure) as described above; exit/play-again always returns to a fresh setup screen, never an auto-loaded roster.

**Navigation and state architecture:** a single root route enum (`entry`, `setup`, `session`, `recap`) drives the view — not a `NavigationStack` with pushed routes, since the default edge-swipe-to-go-back has to be explicitly killed everywhere in the session flow (a mid-round back-swipe is a state nobody designed for). The round screen's `handoff`/`ready`/`revealed` phase lives as persisted model state (SwiftData), not transient `@State` — that's what makes true termination-and-relaunch recovery real rather than assumed. Every transition (handoff shown, reveal tapped, round advanced) triggers an explicit synchronous save immediately after the state mutation. On launch, an explicit "resume in-progress session" path routes straight back into the exact persisted phase — treated as its own tested code path, since it's the one the happy-path demo never exercises.

**Target rotation (already locked, restated for the builder):** active player rotates in strict round-robin order; target excludes the active player and whoever was targeted the immediately prior round, then picks whoever's gone longest since last being targeted. Collapses to strict alternation at 2 players, one forced legal candidate at 3, and spreads out cleanly above that — no magic window number.

**Double-tap / phantom-transition guard:** silent by design — the reveal/advance control disables itself the instant it's tapped, so there is nothing left to tap a second time; no "duplicate input blocked" toast or in-screen message, since a working guard means the user never perceives a second tap happened in the first place. Same guard scope covers the confetti/haptic trigger.

**`isIdleTimerDisabled`:** scoped via `.onAppear`/`.onDisappear` on the round-container view only, explicitly reset to `false` on every exit path including the early-exit-on-empty-pack route.

**Recap card rendering:** `RecapCardView` rendered at a fixed, screen-independent frame via `ImageRenderer` at 3x scale, snapshotted only after a confirmed layout pass (not off a currently-on-screen view) — must be rendered and screenshotted on a physical device before being called done; Xcode previews don't count.

**Design tokens:**
- Gradient stops: Ember Orange `#FF8E3C` → Coral `#FF5A78` → Hot Pink `#D94AF0`, with interpolation weight shifting by context — setup skews warmer/orange, the reveal beat skews hotter/pink.
- A first-class `scrim` token (a fixed-opacity dark overlay, tuned against the actual pink stop) sits behind every white-text zone structurally, so WCAG AA contrast is guaranteed by the system rather than checked screen-by-screen.
- Text: `#FFFDF8` on gradient, `#F4F3F8` on dark backgrounds, muted `#FFD5C8`; semantic `success`/`warning`/`danger` tokens defined.
- Typography: SF Rounded, heavy/black weight reserved for state words and names (PASS TO SAM, YOU HOLD); calmer regular-weight rounded body for prompt text; two-column header uses `minimumScaleFactor` plus a defined two-line max with truncation, tested against a long all-caps name at the largest accessibility Dynamic Type size.
- Spacing/shape: radius family 18/24/30; vertical rhythm 14/18/24/28; tap targets minimum 44pt everywhere, 48×56 (or near-full-width bands) for core round-screen actions.
- Motion: ~260ms spring for handoff→ready, ~200ms tighter spring for the reveal-name beat, confetti capped at 500–700ms and decorative/non-blocking, haptic fired on the exact frame the reveal animation starts (not before, not after).

**Component inventory:** `CampfireGradientSurface(context:)`, `ModeTile` (active/non-interactive variants), `NameRow` with inline validation, `AddPlayerButton`, `RoundStateHeader`/`TwoColumnStateHeader` (owns target-visibility policy and truncation), `HandoffBanner` (cancellable `.task`-scoped auto-advance timer), `RevealActionButton`/`RevealContentCard` (owns the silent double-tap guard), `ConfettiOverlay` (hand-rolled via `Canvas`/`TimelineView`, no third-party dependency), `RecapCardView` + `RecapCardRenderer`, `ShareRow`/`ShareLinkButton`, and one reusable `ErrorPanel`/`ErrorStateView(message:, retryAction:)` used everywhere an error state is reachable instead of bespoke implementations.

**Accessibility:** every interactive control ≥44pt; VoiceOver reads role-then-state in order ("You hold [name]... target hidden..." then, only after reveal, the target name); the two coming-soon tiles are always announced as unavailable via `accessibilityHint("Not available yet")`, never as live controls; long names get a tested wrap/truncation rule at accessibility Dynamic Type sizes rather than an assumed fit; every error state includes accessible recovery-action text. This remains a structurally sighted, pass-and-play experience by design — VoiceOver's job is to correctly announce whichever state is on screen, not to solve the "you must look at the phone to know it's your turn" property of the product.

**Claude Design prompt (kept, relabeled as optional post-build visual-QA only):**
"Design a premium SwiftUI visual direction for a party pass-and-play game called 'Campfire.' Direction: bold, energetic, warm-to-hot gradient, rounded playful geometry, oversized tap targets, physical-phone handoff cues. Reference flow: Entry, Player Setup, Round (handoff → ready → revealed states), Recap, exit/replay. Focus: one working mode (Reveal/confession), two non-interactive coming-soon placeholders, deterministic turn-role clarity, and a shareable recap card as the emotional payoff. Large, legible text at accessibility sizes, rounded slab-like type, strict contrast against gradient stops. Confetti-style reveal moment synced to haptics; clear loading/error/success handling on setup and recap. Include reusable component names, color tokens, typography scale, spacing scale, motion timings, and accessibility states (voice order, 44pt targets, VoiceOver naming)."

---

CONSENSUS: YES

title: Ios Architecture Review
output:
Round 2 is where this actually locks. Codex came back and directly confirmed every open item from round 1 rather than leaving them dangling: the pure, non-UI `GameRulesEngine`/`SessionDomain` module for rotation/target/recap logic (exactly what Claude needed for the scripted 2/3/4/15-player tests), plain ordered arrays instead of `@Relationship` collections for player order and target history, `ModelContainer` init failure promoted to a third named recoverable error state with a visible "starting fresh" notice, a hard-capped confetti lifecycle tied to round phase so nothing keeps redrawing into later rounds, the Photos/permissions-avoidance stance, and — importantly — Codex dropped his earlier "async saves" language and independently landed on the same synchronous-save-per-transition requirement Claude was about to push on, plus the same "two independent `ModelContainer` instances" test methodology for proving real kill-and-relaunch survival.

The one thing Claude raised in round 2 that isn't explicitly echoed word-for-word — treating recap-row persistence as a hard requirement rather than a "nice for debug" — isn't contradicted by anything Codex said; his "every transition writes synchronously... versioned checkpoint" already covers session-completion as one of those transitions, so this reads as covered rather than left open. Nothing else is contested. All six required outputs (architecture, frameworks, persistence, permissions, dependencies, testing) have real, specific, mutually-confirmed answers now.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture:** Three-layer split. A pure domain layer (`GameRulesEngine`/`SessionDomain`) holding `nextActivePlayer`, `nextTarget`, and `deriveRecap` as plain functions over plain structs — no `@State`, no persistence types, no timers, no side effects, so rotation/recap logic is fully unit-testable without any SwiftUI view involved. An orchestration layer (`AppCoordinator` owning the root route enum `entry/setup/session/recap`, plus a `@MainActor` `@Observable` `SessionViewModel` wrapping the persisted `Session` model and driving the `handoff/ready/revealed` round phase). A presentation layer of SwiftUI views that only read state and dispatch intents — never own gameplay truth themselves.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI, SwiftData (persistence), `ImageRenderer` (recap card snapshot, isolated in a dedicated rendering service), `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator`/`UINotificationFeedbackGenerator` (haptics), `Canvas` + `TimelineView(.animation)` or a `DispatchSourceTimer`-driven particle view for confetti (either is fine as long as lifecycle is hard-capped), `ShareLink` (share sheet, no Photos permission needed), StoreKit types kept at the interface/schema level only (no real product plumbing yet), `os` for lightweight local diagnostics. No AR, no Core ML/local LLM — ruled out because the product's value is editorial prompt quality and flow reliability, and dynamic generation would break the offline-anywhere guarantee for no real gain.

**Persistence and local data plan:** SwiftData on iOS 17+. `Session` model persists `playerIDs` and `targetHistory` as **plain ordered array properties**, never `@Relationship` to-many collections, since SwiftData doesn't guarantee relationship fetch order and losing player order would reintroduce the exact wrong-player/wrong-target bug this project has fought since detailed_discussion. Every state-mutating method runs on the main actor and calls a **synchronous** `try context.save()` immediately after the mutation — no batching, no `Task { await context.save() }` detours that could be cancelled or delayed before a kill signal. Recap data is persisted the moment a session completes, not deferred until share is tapped, so a force-quit between session-end and share-tap can't silently lose the one artifact the app exists to produce. Prompt packs load from versioned bundled JSON into SwiftData entities on first launch, tracked with a `PackVersion` row for future local migration. `ModelContainer` init failure (corrupt store, migration mismatch, disk error) is a named, caught, recoverable error state — the app falls back to a fresh in-memory session with a visible "previous session couldn't be restored, starting fresh" notice, never a crash or silent data loss.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** Zero entitlements beyond the default sandbox — no camera, contacts, location, push, or network capability. No `Photos`/`PhotosUI` linkage; `ShareLink` covers the share flow without requesting that permission. No analytics or crash-reporting SDK that phones home. A `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` manifest is required the moment anything touches `UserDefaults` (even just an optional haptics toggle), since Apple now rejects submissions missing it — added now, not left for a late scramble. In-app copy states plainly that names/results stay local by default and only leave the device when the user initiates a share.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Everything needed (confetti, haptics, recap rendering, share, persistence) is achievable first-party, so there's no license surface to evaluate at all.

**Testing implications:** The pure rules engine gets first-class unit tests — rotation/target-selection at 2, 3, 4, and 15 players asserting no self-target and no immediate repeat; round completion; recap derivation across tie, low-variance, and zero-data cases. A double-tap guard test fires the reveal action twice in rapid succession against the view model and asserts exactly one transition and one haptic call. Termination-survival is tested by writing state through one `ModelContainer` instance, then constructing a **second, independent** `ModelContainer` against the same file URL to verify exact phase/active player/target/tally restoration — reusing the live container doesn't prove anything and isn't accepted as a valid test. `ImageRenderer` visual output and the physical `ShareLink` sheet stay a documented, actually-performed on-device manual check, since that layer is genuinely flaky in a headless/simulator run. StoreKit testing is explicitly out of scope for this phase — the `PaywallAccessProviding`/`isPackUnlocked` stub is architecturally testable and replaceable, but not yet tested against real entitlements, and nobody should treat the current always-true stub as validated paywall behavior.
