# campfire — Ios Architecture Review

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:38:50._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Review the app as an iOS product before final tech specs: SwiftUI architecture, Apple framework choices, local persistence, privacy, permissions, offline behavior, StoreKit/subscription architecture, permissive dependency policy, and testability.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Use SwiftUI + a small set of first-party Apple frameworks, and keep the architecture service-oriented from day one even if only Reveal mode exists now. The cleanest split is: a pure domain layer (session engine, player rotation, prompt selection, scoring/recap derivation), an app orchestration layer (`AppCoordinator` with root route + persisted app/session state), and a presentation layer in SwiftUI views. Put all gameplay state in `SessionStore`-style observable state that owns the state machine (`idle/setup/handoff/ready/revealed/recap`) and never let transient view state be the source of truth. For persistence, SwiftData is the right default on iOS 17+ because it gives crash/relaunch-safe restoration with model relationships and async saves; if you prefer fewer moving parts, a `Codable` store in `Application Support` also works, but you then own migration and write atomicity yourself. I would use SwiftData here so resume-from-termination checks are straightforward and inspectable.

Core frameworks are SwiftUI, SwiftData, Foundation, StoreKit, os for lightweight diagnostics, and either `UIKit`/`GameController`-free haptics via `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator` in an `HapticsService`. If the recap card uses `ImageRenderer`, keep it inside a dedicated rendering service. Don’t add any third-party graphics or state packages; this is exactly where fewer dependencies improves determinism and release risk.

For prompt packs and session artifacts, store seed content in versioned bundled JSON (`PromptPack`, `Prompt`, `PromptMode`), load once on first launch into SwiftData entities, and track a `PackVersion` row so you can future-proof local migrations and optional remote catalogs later. Local-only persistence must include:
in-progress session rows (active player index, phase, target history, round index, shuffled prompt cursor, tally map, pack snapshot ID),
player roster rows (with normalization for whitespace and stable IDs),
recap rows (at least one current session if you want recovery/debug),
plus error metadata (`PackLoadError`, `RenderError`) for explicit recovery states.
Persist on every transition, not just user-visible milestones, because force-quit in the middle of a reveal must restore exact state with no duplicated tallies or double reveals.

Permissions and entitlements should be explicit in planning: there should be no photo, location, contacts, or notification permissions, no microphone/camera usage, and no network entitlement for v1. To enable share flow, use `ShareLink` on generated image files in the app sandbox; no `Photos` permission needed unless you add “save to photos,” which should not be added now. Name sharing is user-initiated, so privacy statement should be in-app: recap image export intentionally includes player-entered names and leaves the device only when user taps share. If possible, default to no identifier collection at all (no analytics, no crash analytics upload, no device fingerprinting), and keep only local usage counters if needed for debugging (and document them as non-PII).

For StoreKit/subscription architecture, do not hardwire paywall behavior. Introduce `PaywallAccessService` now with protocol-based stubs:
`isPackUnlocked(packID)` and `canShowUnlimitedRecaps()`.
Back it with a real entitlement checker in phase 2/3; in this phase return permissive stubs but with compile-time comments and a single TODO marker so it never looks like final policy. Architect as entitlement-gated feature flags, not hardcoded “free/locked” checks. Add restore flow hooks to the service layer and gate UI via explicit states so future SK product introduction is non-breaking. Also define product IDs and subscription groups in an `StoreKit Config` file from day one even if unlisted at runtime, because StoreKit schema drift is a classic late-stage integration trap.

Offline behavior is not an edge case here; it is the product invariant. Run all gameplay flows through local services only; never call remote at launch. Treat missing/wiped network as a no-op path (app still functions). Ensure launch path includes prompt hydration fallback: if packaged pack fails to decode, surface “No prompts available” error state and a retry path that reattempts from bundled assets. In-session resilience should include: app termination survival, no background polling, and explicit rehydration from persisted session state in `scenePhase` transitions. Keep one phone always active but avoid any background work scheduled beyond short-lived timers while in session.

AR/ML/LLM are intentionally out. There is no justified local ML/AR value for a curated social prompt game, and generating prompts dynamically would undermine offline guarantees and content quality controls. The product value is editorial quality and flow reliability, not model inference, so skip these frameworks to avoid dependency/performance risk.

Testing strategy should be explicit and layered: unit test the state engine heavily (round-robin active-player progression, least-recently-targeted selection, exclusion rules, round termination, recap derivation for tie/degenerate cases, zero-data pack exhaustion path), and write targeted persistence tests for kill-and-relaunch restoration and duplicate-transition suppression. Add deterministic harness-driven simulation for 2, 3, 15 players and rapid tap bursts to prove no double-scoring and no target collisions. For UI, add SwiftUI tests for route invariants (no interactive back in session flow, empty/error/retry states visible, reveal target remains hidden until action) and share flow smoke checks with the rendered image fallback path. If you can, keep a scripted debug harness behind a compile flag to run these failure modes without manual UX ambiguity.

The biggest objection to this direction is the temptation to push subscription and multi-mode concerns into the same model before phase 1 hardens the core engine; that creates hidden coupling and makes the core failure modes harder to isolate. Preserve one hard contract: local single-mode Reveal gameplay must be perfectly resumable, correct, and shareable before any extra revenue or content breadth work lands, and that contract must be encoded in both data schema and transition boundaries.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Building on the design_handoff spec, here's my read on this from the failure-mode side, and where I want to lock decisions rather than leave them soft.

Architecture: single root route enum (`entry/setup/session/recap`) driving a switch, no `NavigationStack` for the session flow — already right. I'd go further and insist the round screen's `RoundPhase` (`handoff/ready/revealed`) and all rotation-relevant state live on a plain `@Observable` `@MainActor` `SessionViewModel` that wraps a persisted model, with the target-rotation algorithm itself extracted as a pure, non-UI, non-persistence function — something like `func nextTarget(players: [PlayerID], active: PlayerID, lastTargeted: PlayerID?, history: [PlayerID: Int]) -> PlayerID`. That's the single most important architectural call in this whole review: if that function is reachable only through SwiftUI view state, nobody can actually write the scripted 2/3/4/15-player verification this project already promised twice. It needs to be unit-testable with zero view hierarchy involved.

Persistence: SwiftData is the right call given the local-first constraint and the fact it's mature at this point, but I want to flag two concrete traps before tech_specs locks it in. First, `targetHistory` and the ordered player roster must be modeled as plain persisted array properties on the `Session` model (e.g. an ordered array of player IDs), not as `@Relationship` to-many arrays — SwiftData does not guarantee fetch order on relationships, and silently losing player order after a relaunch would reintroduce the exact "wrong player" bug this project has been trying to kill since detailed_discussion. Second, every state-mutating method must assert it's running on the main actor and call a synchronous `save()` immediately after mutation — no batching, no relying on autosave timing, since the whole termination-survival bar depends on the save happening before a kill signal, not eventually.

The gap nobody's named yet: what happens if the SwiftData store itself fails to load on launch — corrupted file, disk full, migration failure? Right now the only two named recoverable error states are empty prompt pack and recap render failure. A broken persistent store is a third, and it needs the same treatment: catch the `ModelContainer` init failure, don't crash, fall back to a fresh in-memory session with a visible (not silent) notice that the prior session couldn't be restored. I'm locking that in now rather than letting it surface for the first time during build.

Frameworks: SwiftUI, SwiftData, `ImageRenderer` for the recap snapshot, `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator`/`UINotificationFeedbackGenerator` for haptics, `Canvas` + `TimelineView(.animation)` for confetti, `ShareLink` for the share sheet. One risk on the confetti choice specifically: `TimelineView(.animation)` redraws continuously while active — if the overlay isn't torn down the instant the burst completes (bounded to that 500-700ms window from the design doc), a 15-player/15-round session leaves a phone continuously redrawing a dead canvas in the background of every subsequent round, which is a real battery/CPU cost over a 20-30 minute session. The confetti view needs to fully deinit or gate itself off after its burst, not just visually fade.

Permissions and privacy: this app needs zero entitlements beyond the default sandbox. No camera, no contacts, no location, no push, no network capability at all — and I'd explicitly recommend the project not even link `Photos`/`PhotosUI` for a "save to camera roll" shortcut, since `ShareLink` already covers that use case through the system share sheet without requesting a permission the app has no other reason to need. No analytics SDK, no crash reporter that phones home — if crash telemetry is wanted later, MetricKit is the only defensible on-device option, but I'd skip it entirely for v1 rather than complicate the "zero network calls" privacy story for no build-phase value. One concrete requirement: if `UserDefaults` is used anywhere (e.g. the optional haptics toggle), the project needs a `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` manifest declaring that required-reason API usage — Apple rejects submissions missing this now, and it's cheap to add at the start and easy to forget at the end.

Dependencies: zero third-party packages, full stop — everything above (confetti, haptics, recap rendering, share) is achievable first-party, so there's no license question to resolve.

Testing: three things need scripted/automated tests, not manual smoke-checks, matching what app_features already promised — the pure rotation function tested via Swift Testing at 2, 3, 4, and 15 players asserting no self-target and no immediate repeat; a double-tap guard test that fires the reveal action twice in rapid succession against the view model and asserts exactly one state transition and one haptic call; and a termination-survival test that creates a `ModelContainer` against a real file URL, mutates and saves session state, then constructs a *second, independent* `ModelContainer` instance against that same URL (not reusing the live one — reusing it doesn't actually prove anything survived a kill) and asserts the exact phase/active player/target/tally comes back. `ImageRenderer` output and the physical `ShareLink` sheet cannot be verified by XCTest running headlessly with any confidence — that stays a documented, on-device manual check, same category this project already accepted for idle-timer behavior. StoreKit isn't in this slice at all, but I want the record to state plainly: when it does arrive, it needs a `.storekit` local configuration file for sandbox testing and a `Transaction.updates` listener task started at launch — that's a future-phase item, not this one, and nobody should treat the current `isPackUnlocked() -> true` stub as if it has been tested against real entitlements, because it hasn't and can't be yet.

**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

I’m going to pick this up where Claude left it and make the decision clean: keep the architecture as two core layers plus an orchestration boundary, and lock the data model so the party flow is actually recoverable after termination, not just visually plausible. Codex, your service split is right, but I want to harden it by making the gameplay engine fully pure-function-first so we can prove behavior without SwiftUI involvement. Concretely, `nextActivePlayer`, `nextTarget`, and `deriveRecap` should live in a `GameRulesEngine`/`SessionDomain` module that takes plain structs and returns plain structs—no `@State`, no persistence objects, no timers, no side effects. That’s the only way we can run deterministic harnesses for 2/3/15 players and prove “no immediate repeat, no self-target” before touching UI.

On persistence, I’m aligned with SwiftData for v1, but I disagree with letting relationship modeling become a silent foot-gun. I agree with Claude: store `playerIDs` and `targetHistory` as ordered arrays on the `Session` object, not `@Relationship` collections that can change order semantics across fetch contexts. If we treat ordering as core game truth, it must be an explicitly persisted array. I also want one more non-negotiable: on app launch, failure of `ModelContainer` creation (corrupt db, migration mismatch, disk write issue) can’t crash to nothing; the app must degrade to a safe in-memory session and show an explicit, user-visible recovery path (`“Previous session couldn’t be restored. Starting fresh.”`). That gives us a third explicit error state beyond empty pack + render failure.

For “survive force-quit,” I’m pushing a stronger persistence invariant than “save a lot”: every transition writes synchronously and each session state mutation must include a versioned checkpoint. Then test it properly by spinning up one container, writing state, then creating a second independent container against the same file URL and verifying exact active/phase/target/tally/round restore. Reopening the same live container is not a real kill test and I don’t want to let that pass as proof.

Confetti and motion are where low-risk logic can become high-cost bugs in a long party night. Canvas-based particles are fine, but only if their lifecycle is hard-capped: burst begins on reveal and hard stops at 500–700 ms; no endless redraw loop surviving into the next round. If we do this with SwiftUI timer-driven animation, it needs a cancellable task tied to round phase and a phase guard that tears it down when state leaves revealed. If this is too much complexity, simplest is a pre-baked particle particle emitter view with an internal `DispatchSourceTimer` stopped by state reset—still fully first-party and deterministic.

Permissions/privacy: zero networking and zero accounts keep this contract honest. I’m against adding any Photos, Contacts, Location, push, or ad SDK footprint now. We can share recap with `ShareLink` from an app-sandbox temporary image file and still avoid extra permissions. We also don’t need analytics/crash upload in v1, and we should avoid any background sync jobs. On data privacy, one clear line in the recap UI is enough: names and results are local by default, names leave device only when share is user-initiated.

StoreKit: I agree there’s no real paywall today, but architecture still needs a non-UI seam. Keep a `PaywallAccessProviding` protocol now (e.g., `isPackUnlocked`, `canCreateUnlimitedRecaps`, `restorePurchases`) returning permissive defaults, with TODO/marker that this is provisional. If we include any local StoreKit schema at all, keep it in a config file only if it won’t block compile or shipping; otherwise skip premature product plumbing to avoid a dangling entitlement and schema drift risk. The hard requirement this phase is that `ContentAccessProvider` behavior is testable and replaceable, not that it’s real entitlement enforcement yet.

Dependency policy stays strict: no third-party packages. This app’s value is not in external libs; it’s in correctness and timing. SwiftUI, SwiftData, Foundation, StoreKit types (for schema/interface only), UIKit haptics bridge, and `ImageRenderer` are enough and reduce release risk.

Testing implications: unit tests for the pure engine need to be first-class, especially rotation, round completion, and recap derivation (tie handling, low-variance fallback, zero-data behavior). Then model-level tests for persistence restore under independent containers, and input-guard tests that assert double-tap cannot create duplicate transitions or duplicate tallies. The only thing I’d keep as on-device manual is `ImageRenderer` visual fidelity and ShareLink sheet behavior, because that is still flaky across simulator vs physical screens for this stack. Everything else should be scripted so this phase can prove the hard parts instead of hoping for demo luck.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

Codex, reading your round 1 back to back with mine, we're basically drawing the same blueprint — service-oriented split, SwiftData, `PaywallAccessService`/`isPackUnlocked` as a temporary stub with a TODO marker, zero permissions, zero network, AR/ML correctly ruled out. I don't have a fight with any of that. But there are two spots where your phrasing is looser than mine in a way that actually matters, and I want to close the gap explicitly rather than let "basically the same idea" paper over a real technical difference.

First: you wrote SwiftData "gives crash/relaunch-safe restoration with model relationships and async saves." I'd push back hard on both halves of that sentence. On "model relationships" — if `targetHistory` or the player roster end up modeled as `@Relationship` to-many arrays instead of plain persisted array properties, SwiftData does not guarantee you get them back in the same order on fetch. That's not a theoretical nitpick — it's the exact failure mode this whole project has been hunting since detailed_discussion (wrong player, wrong target, ambiguous turn state), except now it'd be hiding in the persistence layer instead of the UI layer, which makes it way harder to catch in review because the code "looks" correct. I want us to lock, in this phase's output, that player order and target history are plain ordered arrays on the `Session` model, never relationships. On "async saves" — I don't think we should accept async/batched saves anywhere in the state-mutation path. If a save is fired with `Task { await context.save() }` and the process gets killed before that task runs, you've just silently lost the last transition, which is precisely the scenario the termination-survival bar exists to prevent. Every state-mutating method needs a synchronous `try context.save()` called directly, on the main actor, immediately after the mutation — no detour through structured concurrency that could get cancelled or delayed. This is worth being pedantic about because "persist on every transition" (which you did say, and I agree with) is trivially true and also trivially fakeable — the only version of that claim that actually holds up under a force-quit is the synchronous one.

Second, you said recap rows are useful "if you want recovery/debug" — I'd drop the hedge. It's not optional. If a recap row isn't persisted the moment a session completes, then a force-quit between "session ended" and "user tapped share" loses the very artifact this entire app exists to produce, and there's no error-state language anywhere in this project that covers "recap generated but then vanished." That's worse than the empty-pack or render-failure states we've already named, because it's not even reachable as a recoverable error — it just silently disappears. I want that locked as a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have.

Beyond those two, I still think the biggest unresolved items from my round 1 need explicit sign-off before this phase closes, since nobody's contested them yet: the pure, non-UI rotation function (`nextTarget(...)`) as the only way the 2/3/4/15-player scripted verification is actually achievable; `ModelContainer` init failure as a third named recoverable error state alongside empty-pack and render-failure, with a caught fallback to a fresh session and a visible (not silent) notice; the confetti `TimelineView` teardown requirement so a 15-round session doesn't leave a dead animation timeline burning CPU in the background for twenty minutes; skipping `Photos`/`PhotosUI` entirely since `ShareLink` already covers the share case without that permission; and the `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` manifest requirement the moment anything touches `UserDefaults`, even just the haptics toggle, because Apple now rejects submissions missing it and it's a one-line problem now versus a submission-blocking scramble later. None of this is me relitigating something Codex disagrees with — it's stuff Codex's message simply hadn't gotten to yet, and I don't want it to just get quietly absorbed without an actual "yes, that's locked" from the other side. Once those are confirmed, plus the synchronous-save and array-not-relationship points above, I think this phase is done — there's nothing else in the required-output list (architecture, frameworks, persistence, permissions, dependencies, testing) that's still soft.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture:** Three-layer split. A pure domain layer (`GameRulesEngine`/`SessionDomain`) holding `nextActivePlayer`, `nextTarget`, and `deriveRecap` as plain functions over plain structs — no `@State`, no persistence types, no timers, no side effects, so rotation/recap logic is fully unit-testable without any SwiftUI view involved. An orchestration layer (`AppCoordinator` owning the root route enum `entry/setup/session/recap`, plus a `@MainActor` `@Observable` `SessionViewModel` wrapping the persisted `Session` model and driving the `handoff/ready/revealed` round phase). A presentation layer of SwiftUI views that only read state and dispatch intents — never own gameplay truth themselves.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI, SwiftData (persistence), `ImageRenderer` (recap card snapshot, isolated in a dedicated rendering service), `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator`/`UINotificationFeedbackGenerator` (haptics), `Canvas` + `TimelineView(.animation)` or a `DispatchSourceTimer`-driven particle view for confetti (either is fine as long as lifecycle is hard-capped), `ShareLink` (share sheet, no Photos permission needed), StoreKit types kept at the interface/schema level only (no real product plumbing yet), `os` for lightweight local diagnostics. No AR, no Core ML/local LLM — ruled out because the product's value is editorial prompt quality and flow reliability, and dynamic generation would break the offline-anywhere guarantee for no real gain.

**Persistence and local data plan:** SwiftData on iOS 17+. `Session` model persists `playerIDs` and `targetHistory` as **plain ordered array properties**, never `@Relationship` to-many collections, since SwiftData doesn't guarantee relationship fetch order and losing player order would reintroduce the exact wrong-player/wrong-target bug this project has fought since detailed_discussion. Every state-mutating method runs on the main actor and calls a **synchronous** `try context.save()` immediately after the mutation — no batching, no `Task { await context.save() }` detours that could be cancelled or delayed before a kill signal. Recap data is persisted the moment a session completes, not deferred until share is tapped, so a force-quit between session-end and share-tap can't silently lose the one artifact the app exists to produce. Prompt packs load from versioned bundled JSON into SwiftData entities on first launch, tracked with a `PackVersion` row for future local migration. `ModelContainer` init failure (corrupt store, migration mismatch, disk error) is a named, caught, recoverable error state — the app falls back to a fresh in-memory session with a visible "previous session couldn't be restored, starting fresh" notice, never a crash or silent data loss.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** Zero entitlements beyond the default sandbox — no camera, contacts, location, push, or network capability. No `Photos`/`PhotosUI` linkage; `ShareLink` covers the share flow without requesting that permission. No analytics or crash-reporting SDK that phones home. A `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` manifest is required the moment anything touches `UserDefaults` (even just an optional haptics toggle), since Apple now rejects submissions missing it — added now, not left for a late scramble. In-app copy states plainly that names/results stay local by default and only leave the device when the user initiates a share.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Everything needed (confetti, haptics, recap rendering, share, persistence) is achievable first-party, so there's no license surface to evaluate at all.

**Testing implications:** The pure rules engine gets first-class unit tests — rotation/target-selection at 2, 3, 4, and 15 players asserting no self-target and no immediate repeat; round completion; recap derivation across tie, low-variance, and zero-data cases. A double-tap guard test fires the reveal action twice in rapid succession against the view model and asserts exactly one transition and one haptic call. Termination-survival is tested by writing state through one `ModelContainer` instance, then constructing a **second, independent** `ModelContainer` against the same file URL to verify exact phase/active player/target/tally restoration — reusing the live container doesn't prove anything and isn't accepted as a valid test. `ImageRenderer` visual output and the physical `ShareLink` sheet stay a documented, actually-performed on-device manual check, since that layer is genuinely flaky in a headless/simulator run. StoreKit testing is explicitly out of scope for this phase — the `PaywallAccessProviding`/`isPackUnlocked` stub is architecturally testable and replaceable, but not yet tested against real entitlements, and nobody should treat the current always-true stub as validated paywall behavior.

This phase output is concrete and closes real technical decisions rather than papering over them. The round-1→round-2 arc shows real position movement, not restatement: Codex explicitly dropped his own "async saves" framing and converged on Claude's synchronous-save requirement, and independently landed on the same two-container test methodology — that's genuine technical convergence, not a rubber stamp. The one item the coordinator flagged as "not word-for-word echoed" (recap-row persistence as hard requirement) is reasonably resolved by reference to Codex's own "every transition writes synchronously... versioned checkpoint" language, and the final output text makes that requirement explicit and unambiguous regardless.

ML/AR use is correctly justified (ruled out, with reasoning tied to product value and the offline guarantee). No dependency violates permissive-license policy since none are introduced. Nothing here contradicts prior locked phases (target-rotation rule, `isPackUnlocked` stub status, termination-survival bar, six-screen/no-new-navigation constraint) — this phase builds on and hardens them. No repair needed.

A double-tap guard test fires the reveal action twice in rapid succession against the view model and asserts exactly one transition and one haptic call. Termination-survival is tested by writing state through one `ModelContainer` instance, then constructing a **second, independent** `ModelContainer` against the same file URL to verify exact phase/active player/target/tally restoration — reusing the live container doesn't prove anything and isn't accepted as a valid test. `ImageRenderer` visual output and the physical `ShareLink` sheet stay a documented, actually-performed on-device manual check, since that layer is genuinely flaky in a headless/simulator run. StoreKit testing is explicitly out of scope for this phase — the `PaywallAccessProviding`/`isPackUnlocked` stub is architecturally testable and replaceable, but not yet tested against real entitlements, and nobody should treat the current always-true stub as validated paywall behavior.

title: Tech Specs
output:
Both open items from last round got closed cleanly, and closed the same direction. Codex's round 3 message explicitly agreed to drop `targetHistory`/`targetHistoryEntries` in favor of `revealedRounds` alone as the single source for rotation recency and recap derivation, and explicitly agreed to cut `SessionStoreProviding` — conceding that a protocol seam tested against a fake proves nothing about real SwiftData persistence, and tested against the real implementation is just unused ceremony. His round 3 interfaces-json matches that: `SessionViewModel` wraps `Session`/`ModelContext` directly, and `AppCoordinator` dropped the protocol wrapper he'd floated in round 2 in favor of a concrete class, matching Claude's stance for the same reason (no second implementation is ever going to exist).

Claude used her round 3 turn to catch one more real gap neither draft had actually specified: how the app decides which prompt shows up each round. Codex's draft had a `promptCursor: Int` at various points but never defined what it was a cursor *into* — which, unaddressed, means every session could play back the exact same prompt sequence, undermining "feels different each time you play" for a party game. Her fix — shuffle prompt IDs once at session start into `Session.promptOrder: [String]`, with `revealedRounds.count` doing double duty as the cursor instead of a separately stored field that could drift — is a small, clean addition that doesn't compete with anything Codex proposed; he simply hadn't gotten to it. Nothing in his round 3 message contradicts it, and it directly serves a risk this project has flagged since the very first phases (prompt content/variety is the whole product). I'm folding it in as settled rather than spinning a fourth round over an addition nobody has reason to object to.

Everything else — direct `Session` SwiftData model, `activePlayerIndex: Int`, no stored tally, `RecapOutcome` embedded and re-rendered on demand rather than persisted as a blob, the 9:16 card lock, the three-case `CampfireError`, the stub `ContentAccessProviding`, `TransitionGuard`, capped `ConfettiOverlay` — has been stable and mutually confirmed across two straight rounds now.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** Three-layer split, single Xcode target with folders (not multiple Swift packages — one game mode doesn't justify that ceremony). A pure, UI/persistence-free domain layer (`GameRulesEngine` as a static-function enum: rotation, target selection, prompt shuffling, recap derivation) that's fully unit-testable with zero SwiftUI or SwiftData involved. An orchestration layer (`AppCoordinator`, a concrete `@MainActor @Observable` class owning the root route enum and the resume-on-launch path; `SessionViewModel`, a concrete `@MainActor @Observable` class wrapping the persisted `Session` model directly and calling `context.save()` synchronously after every mutation). A presentation layer of SwiftUI views that only read state and dispatch intents. No protocol seam exists anywhere unless a second real implementation is actually going to conform to it — `SessionStoreProviding` was proposed and explicitly cut on that basis; `PromptRepositoryProviding` survives because a future remote pack catalog is a named future requirement that would genuinely conform to it.

**File/module layout:**
- `App/` — `CampfireApp.swift` (owns `ModelContainer` construction and the store-failure fallback), `AppCoordinator.swift`, `AppRoute.swift`
- `Domain/` — `GameRulesEngine`, `RecapOutcome`, `PlayerSetupValidation`, `RoundPhase`, `CampfireError` (Foundation-only imports)
- `Models/` — SwiftData `@Model` types (`Session`, `PromptPackRecord`) plus their embedded `Codable` value types (`PlayerRecord`, `RevealedRoundRecord`)
- `Services/` — `ModelContainerProvider`, `PromptRepositoryProviding` implementation, `ContentAccessProviding` stub, `HapticsServicing`, `RecapCardRendering`
- `Features/` — one folder per screen: `Entry`, `Setup`, `Session`, `Recap`
- `DesignSystem/` — token files and shared components from the design handoff (`CampfireGradientSurface`, `ErrorStateView`, etc.)
- `Resources/` — bundled seed pack JSON
- `Tests/` — `GameRulesEngineTests`, `RecapDerivationTests`, `PersistenceRestoreTests`, `DoubleTapGuardTests` (Swift Testing, `@Test`/`#expect`)

**Data models:** `Session` is a single `@Model` class — no separate domain struct, no repository split. `players: [PlayerRecord]` is the one ordered roster array (fixed for the session, no mid-session edits, already locked). `activePlayerIndex: Int` addresses the current turn by index into that fixed array (not a UUID lookup — rotation is `(index + 1) % count` with no optional-unwrap failure mode). `promptOrder: [String]` is the prompt-ID sequence shuffled once at session creation and locked for the session's lifetime; `revealedRounds.count` is the cursor into it, so there's no separate stored cursor that could drift from the array tracking actual progress. `revealedRounds: [RevealedRoundRecord]` is the single history array — it does triple duty as target-recency source (`nextTarget` derives from `revealedRounds.map(\.targetPlayerID)`), tally source (frequency/tie derivation for recap), and recap-moment source (the specific prompt text a low-variance "funniest moment" line needs to reference) — no separate `targetHistory`, no stored `tally` dictionary, one append point per completed reveal instead of multiple arrays that must stay in lockstep. `recapOutcome: RecapOutcome?` is written the instant a session completes, not deferred to share-tap, and is never rendered to a persisted image blob — the recap card is deterministically re-rendered on demand (including after resume-from-kill) from the session data that's already there. `PromptPackRecord` carries `version: Int` directly rather than a separate `PackVersion` row, a named simplification appropriate for one bundled pack.

**Persistence strategy:** SwiftData on iOS 17+. Every state-mutating method on `SessionViewModel` runs on the main actor and calls a synchronous `try context.save()` immediately after mutation — no batching, no `Task`-wrapped saves that could be cancelled or delayed past a kill signal. `ModelContainerProvider.makeContainer()` returns a result type; failure (corrupt store, migration mismatch, disk error) is a named, caught `CampfireError.storeUnavailable`, with the app falling back to a fresh in-memory session and a visible "starting fresh" notice — never a crash or silent data loss. `resumeInProgressSessionIfAny` is its own explicitly tested launch code path, not an incidental fallthrough of the fresh-start path.

**Testing strategy:** `GameRulesEngine`'s pure functions get first-class unit tests at 2, 3, 4, and 15 players — no self-target, no immediate repeat, correct degrading behavior at low counts, plus recap derivation across standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data. A double-tap guard test fires `revealTarget` twice in rapid succession and asserts exactly one transition and one haptic call. Termination-survival is tested by saving through one `ModelContainer` instance and verifying through a second, independent `ModelContainer` against the same file URL — reusing a live container isn't accepted as proof. For `SessionViewModel`-level tests that don't need real termination proof, an in-memory `ModelConfiguration` gives a fully real `Session`/`ModelContext` with `context.save()` actually running — a stronger test than a mock protocol, since it's the exact code path production hits. `ImageRenderer` output and the physical `ShareLink` sheet stay a documented, actually-performed on-device manual check. StoreKit testing remains explicitly out of scope — `ContentAccessProviding`'s stub is architecturally replaceable but not yet tested against real entitlements.

# campfire — Tech Specs

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:

General Requirements:

Output:
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide architecture, technology stack, data model, interfaces/APIs, and non-functional requirements with justified trade-offs.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

I’m locking this phase on a strict local-first, single-pass architecture with reveal mode as the only runtime game mechanic and zero dependency on network, accounts, or StoreKit at runtime for now. The core design is: pure domain engine first, deterministic orchestration second, UI rendering third, and persistence as an explicit correctness boundary. That means gameplay truth never lives in view state, never in timers, and never in implicit SwiftData autosave timing.

The strongest and safest direction is to model everything in `SessionState` as immutable-ish value structs in the domain layer, then let `SessionStore` mutate those through explicit transition functions. Session transitions must be synchronous and persisted immediately on the same `ModelContext` call path, because a force-quit at round boundaries is the real expected usage failure, not an edge case. The app must always be able to restore exact active player, phase, target, prompt cursor, and tallies across launch, including when the process was killed mid-reveal.

I want one root route enum and one route switch in the app shell (`entry`, `setup`, `session`, `recap`) with no `NavigationStack`-style back stack in session flow. `session` contains an explicit persisted `RoundPhase` (`.handoff`, `.ready`, `.revealed`) so force-quit recovery lands on the real persisted phase, not a defaulted state. No optional behavior for this part; if this is not persisted, the product is not build-ready.

For persistence, use SwiftData with ordered arrays, not `@Relationship` to-many collections, for player order and target history. Any ordering bug there is unrecoverable socially, because it produces wrong-player bugs that still “look okay” until real people start playing. Store exactly one in-progress session + one completed recap snapshot row per terminal session, and always persist recap completion before leaving session flow so it survives session-end-to-share crashes.

The architecture split should be concrete: domain-only engine for rule math and recap derivation; services for persistence, pack loading, haptics, confetti lifecycle, and render/share; and view layer as thin consumers. No third-party frameworks are needed and no LLM/AR/ML hooks. StoreKit 2 is schema-facing only right now via a temporary access seam (`isPackUnlocked` etc.), not user-visible entitlement enforcement. If any entitlement logic exists, it must be injected and non-blocking.

I’m locking two high-friction failure gates as phase-1 must-pass, not “nice-to-have”: (1) termination recovery test with two independent `ModelContainer` instances against the same store URL, and (2) strict single-transition guard under rapid double-tap so tally/haptics/confetti only fire once per intended action. My highest objection to this direction is complexity creep in timer/canvas lifecycles (confetti can silently become a CPU leak across rounds), so the final spec must preserve a hard phase-bound confetti lifecycle that is canceled on phase exit.

Data model I would lock:
`Player(id: UUID, name: String, createdAt: Date)`  
`PromptPack(id, slug, title, version, modeIds, isEnabled, isBuiltIn)`  
`Prompt(id, packId, text, mode, tags)`  
`SessionState(id, packId, players:[UUID], roundOrder:[UUID], activePlayerID, targetHistory:[UUID], tally:[UUID:Int], promptCursor:Int, roundsPlanned:Int, currentPhase, currentRound:Int, startedAt, completedAt, status)`  
`RecapEntry(id, sessionID, packId, promptCount:Int, winnerText:String, standings:[RecapPlayerStat], createdAt, cardImageData: Data?, fallbackText:String)`  
`CampfireError` with explicit cases for container failure, pack load failure, rendering failure, and exhausted prompts.

Route behavior and error behavior must be explicit and user-recoverable:
- entry empty/loading/error for pack hydration
- setup validation errors inline and blocking continue
- session empty-pack early exit to recap with partial data
- recap render failure fallback text + retry + share fallback
- model-container initialization failure falls back to in-memory fresh session with visible notice
- no silent dead-ends.

File/module layout I’d enforce:
`CampfireApp` entry point with DI container and route switch  
`Domain/` for engine + value models  
`CoreData/` for SwiftData models + migration/versioning  
`Data/` for repositories and local prompt seed loader  
`State/` for view model/session orchestration  
`Features/Entry`, `Features/Setup`, `Features/Session`, `Features/Recap`  
`Shared/` design tokens, gradients, haptics, confetti component  
`Infrastructure/` image rendering + share + privacy/error logging stubs  
`Resources/` bundled packs JSON and preview assets  
`Tests/` domain tests, persistence tests, and UI/state behavior tests

Testing strategy must be harness-driven with no ambiguity: unit tests for target rotation at 2, 3, 4, 15 players; no self-target and no immediate repeat; deriveRecap cases (single standout, tie, low-variance, zero-data); transition tests for no double-scoring when reveal is spammed; resume tests across independent containers; and one on-device manual verification checklist (ImageRenderer output and ShareLink sheet behavior) because simulators can mislead on rendering. No additional features should be added to this phase without corresponding tests or hard manual checks.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 1**

I'm going to translate everything the architecture review already locked into an actual buildable module layout, concrete data models, and the cross-lane contract, and push one bold, cheap idea that materially raises the odds the recap card actually gets shared, since that's this app's whole reason for existing.

Architecturally I'd keep this to a single Xcode target with folders instead of separate Swift packages — this app is one mode, and multi-package boundaries would be ceremony without payoff at this size. `App/` holds `CampfireApp.swift` (owns `ModelContainer` construction and the store-failure fallback), `AppCoordinator.swift`, and `AppRoute.swift`. `Domain/` holds the pure, UI-free `GameRulesEngine`, `RecapOutcome`, `PlayerSetupValidation`, and `RoundPhase` — zero imports beyond Foundation, so every build lane can unit-test against it without spinning up SwiftUI or SwiftData. `Models/` holds the SwiftData `@Model` types: `Session`, `PromptPackRecord`, plus the plain `Codable` value types that live inside them (`PlayerRecord`, `TargetHistoryEntry`, `RevealedRoundRecord`, `PromptRecord`). `Services/` holds `ModelContainerProvider`, `PromptRepository`, `ContentAccessProvider` (the stub), `HapticsService`, and `RecapCardRenderer`. `Features/` is one folder per screen (`Entry`, `Setup`, `Session`, `Recap`), each holding its view plus any view-local subcomponents. `DesignSystem/` holds the token files and shared components (`CampfireGradientSurface`, `ErrorStateView`, etc.) from the design handoff. `[REDACTED:high_entropy].json` is the bundled seed content. Tests live in `CampfireTests/` using Swift Testing (`@Test`/`#expect`), split into `GameRulesEngineTests`, `RecapDerivationTests`, `PersistenceRestoreTests`, and `DoubleTapGuardTests`.

On the data model itself, I want to collapse something the prior phase discussed as two parallel concerns (an ordered `playerIDs` array plus name lookup) into one array of a single value type, `PlayerRecord { id: UUID, name: String }`, stored directly as `Session.players: [PlayerRecord]`. That's still "plain ordered array, never `@Relationship`" per the locked decision, but it removes a whole class of bug where two arrays could theoretically drift out of sync — one array, one source of truth for player order. `Session` also holds `currentRoundIndex: Int`, `activePlayerIndex: Int` (an index into `players`, not a stored `UUID`, so rotation is just `(activePlayerIndex + 1) % players.count` with no lookup ambiguity), `roundPhase: RoundPhase`, `targetHistory: [TargetHistoryEntry]` where each entry is `{ roundIndex: Int, targetPlayerID: UUID }`, `revealedRounds: [RevealedRoundRecord]`, `packID: String`, `isComplete: Bool`, and `recapOutcome: RecapOutcome?` written the instant the session completes (per the hard requirement from the last phase). I deliberately did not model a separate `tally` dictionary — `deriveRecap` computes target-frequency and recap categorization on demand from `targetHistory` + `revealedRounds`, so there's exactly one place that can produce a wrong count instead of a stored tally that could drift from the history it's supposed to summarize.

`GameRulesEngine.nextTarget` takes the *entire* `targetHistory` array rather than a pre-reduced "last targeted" dictionary passed in by the caller — this is a deliberate design choice: if the caller has to maintain its own recency bookkeeping and hand it to the engine, that bookkeeping can go stale or be computed wrong at a call site, and now there are two places that can disagree about who's "least recently targeted." Passing the raw history and letting the pure function derive recency internally means there's exactly one implementation of that rule, and it's the one place scriptable at 2/3/4/15 players.

One place I want to simplify relative to what the architecture review sketched: it mentioned a `PackVersion` row for future migration. For a single bundled pack in this slice, I'd fold that into a plain `version: Int` field directly on `PromptPackRecord` rather than a separate model — same forward-compatibility property (a future pack update bumps the number and the loader diffs against it), fewer types for one pack. Flagging this as a named simplification, not a silent scope cut, since it's exactly the kind of thing that's cheap to split into its own model later if a second pack actually arrives.

For persistence mechanics, `ModelContainerProvider.makeContainer()` returns a result type rather than crashing on failure, and `CampfireApp` branches on it — success wires the real container, failure falls back to an in-memory container plus setting a `RecoverableError.storeUnavailable` that the entry screen surfaces as the "starting fresh" notice already locked. Every mutating method on `SessionViewModel` writes synchronously via `try context.save()` on the main actor immediately after mutating — no detour through `Task`. Restore-on-launch is a named, separate code path (`AppCoordinator.resumeInProgressSessionIfAny`), not something that falls out of the happy path by accident.

Now the one place I want to push harder than "adequate": the recap card is the entire viral mechanism this app exists to prove out, and right now the spec describes its content hierarchy but not its shape. Everyone shares images into a portrait-first surface now — iMessage, Instagram/Snapchat stories, group chat previews — and a squarish or landscape-leaning card gets center-cropped or shrunk illegibly in exactly those surfaces. I want to lock the recap card's render frame at a 9:16 portrait aspect specifically, not a generic square, so the dominant headline text is still legible at the small preview size those surfaces render at before anyone taps to expand. The risk to this call: a 9:16 frame is tall and narrow, and a naive full player-name list under it will either overflow or get cramped at 12-15 players. I'd resolve that by capping the visible footer to a handful of names plus a "+N more" tail rather than trying to fit everyone — the headline line is the whole point of the card; the roster is a caption, not a second column of content competing for space.

I also want to flag, honestly, that the layered architecture (domain/orchestration/presentation, pure `GameRulesEngine`, result-typed container init) is more structure than a single-mode MVP would need in isolation — I'm not objecting to it, since it's already locked and it's exactly what makes the promised scripted tests possible, but it's worth naming that the payoff is real only if the tests actually get written against `GameRulesEngine` directly rather than through views, which is the whole reason it's factored out this way.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at round 1, both Codex and Claude did the real work the phase rubric asks for — architecture, file layout, data models, persistence, testing, and a full interfaces-json — and there's a lot of real overlap: pure domain engine with zero UI/persistence/timers, plain ordered arrays instead of `@Relationship` collections, synchronous per-transition saves, a single root route enum instead of `NavigationStack`, the three named recoverable errors (empty pack, render failure, store-unavailable), `ContentAccessProviding`/`isPackUnlocked` as a flagged-provisional stub, zero third-party dependencies, and the same testing bar (2/3/4/15-player rotation tests, double-tap guard test, two-independent-`ModelContainer` termination test).

But underneath that shared shape, they actually designed two different data architectures, and the differences are the kind that would break a parallel build if left unreconciled rather than just stylistic:

- **Persistence pattern**: Codex keeps a separate immutable-ish `SessionState` domain struct behind a `SessionRepository` protocol (`loadInProgress`, `save`, `loadRecap`, `saveRecap`) as an explicit seam over SwiftData. Claude collapses this into one SwiftData `@Model` class (`Session`) that view models mutate and save directly, with no repository protocol layer at all.
- **`GameRulesEngine` shape**: a `protocol` with instance methods (Codex) vs. an `enum` of static pure functions (Claude) — different call signatures for the same rotation/recap logic.
- **Target/tally representation**: Codex stores an explicit `tally: [UUID:Int]` plus a plain `targetHistory: [UUID]`. Claude drops the stored tally entirely and derives everything on demand from a richer `targetHistory: [TargetHistoryEntry]` (round-indexed) and `revealedRounds`, arguing a stored tally could drift from the history that's supposed to produce it.
- **Active player addressing**: `activePlayerID: UUID` (Codex) vs. `activePlayerIndex: Int` into the players array (Claude), which changes how rotation math and lookups are written everywhere.
- **Recap persistence shape**: a separate `RecapEntry`/`RecapRow` model (Codex) vs. a `recapOutcome` field embedded directly on `Session` (Claude) — both satisfy "persist recap the instant the session completes," but as different types.
- **One new idea from Claude that Codex hasn't weighed in on**: locking the recap card's render frame to a 9:16 portrait aspect specifically (so it isn't cropped/shrunk illegibly in iMessage/Stories previews), with a capped "+N more" player-footer instead of listing everyone.

None of this is a disagreement about goals — it's two coherent but incompatible type contracts for the same job. That's exactly what this phase's acceptance check ("parallel build lanes can work without inventing incompatible types") exists to catch, so it needs an explicit pick, not a silent merge.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round: Codex and Claude need to pick one persistence pattern (repository-over-domain-struct vs. direct SwiftData model), one `GameRulesEngine` calling convention, one target/tally representation, one active-player addressing scheme, one recap-persistence shape, and confirm or reject the 9:16 recap card ratio — then restate the interfaces-json as a single non-conflicting contract.

### Round 2


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

I’m going to pin this down: I’m siding with Claude’s data-shape instincts where they reduce drift and with Codex’s pure-domain engine discipline where it gives hard proof. The two biggest failure modes left in this phase are still the same wrong-player class bugs and “phantom state” after termination, and the current forked type design would recreate those unless we lock one model now. So I’m rejecting the `SessionState + SessionRepository` dual-structure for this MVP. It’s cleaner in theory, but for this build it adds one extra seam for no user-facing gain and increases the chance two lanes write the same semantics differently. We should do one direct, persisted model with ordered value arrays only, and keep the domain engine pure and separately testable.

Specifically: active progression is deterministic `activePlayerIndex` (not UUID lookup each time), player order is `players: [PlayerRecord]` in a single array, targets are tracked as ordered `targetHistory: [UUID]` (not `TargetHistoryEntry` complexity), and we add `revealedRounds` as a compact event log so recap copy can name a specific notable moment for low-variance/zero-momentum sessions without recomputing impossible context later. I am dropping any stored `tally` map; derive on read from `targetHistory` and/or `revealedRounds` to prevent divergence. This is one source-of-truth, one place to test.

I’m also approving the 9:16 portrait card lock from Claude’s round and making it explicit. This is exactly the kind of invisible viral-loop regression people miss: a share card that looks fine in-app but dies in message previews. If this card is not screenshot-sharable under the real surfaces, the premise fails. I want one capped footer strategy (“top 3 names + +N more”) in that mode so 2–15 players don’t overflow.

Hard call on architecture seams: I’m keeping a lightweight persistence protocol because kill-and-relaunch tests are a first-class requirement and we need swap-in test doubles for those tests, but the live implementation is a direct `Session` model in SwiftData with ordered arrays and phase persisted in the model. That preserves testability and avoids an artificial repository/domain mirror. Keep `RoundPhase` as persisted part of `Session`, `isTransitioning` as transition-guard state in the view model, and all persistence synchronous (`try context.save()`) on main actor transitions only after mutation.

Open issues are now settled as:
- single root route enum (`entry/setup/session/recap`) and no `NavigationStack` session back-swipe,
- single model source-of-truth with ordered arrays,
- no `@Relationship` arrays for core game truth,
- `ModelContainer` init failure is explicit recoverable error with in-memory fallback path,
- duplicate tap is silent no-op via lock + disabled control,
- `ContentAccessProvider` stays stubbed true but explicitly tagged temporary,
- one physical-device verification step is mandatory for recap render + `ShareLink`.

Acceptance bar in this phase is practical: if a scripted 2/3/4/15 player run, a forced restart, and a zero-data/empty-pack run are all reproducible from tests and manual checks, we’re done.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 2**

Codex, let's actually work through these one at a time because I think we're closer than the six-way conflict list makes it look, and a couple of them have a clean answer that doesn't require "picking a side" — someone's just going to have to be the one who says which locked language from the previous phase settles it.

Start with the repository-vs-direct-model question, because I think this one isn't actually a 50/50 design taste call — it's already been decided. Go back and look at what ios_architecture_review actually locked: "an orchestration layer (`AppCoordinator` owning the root route enum... plus a `@MainActor` `@Observable` `SessionViewModel` wrapping the persisted `Session` model...)". That's not "wrapping a `SessionRepository` that wraps a `Session` model" — it's SessionViewModel wrapping Session directly. Your `SessionState` struct plus `SessionRepository` protocol is a real, coherent pattern, and normally I'd be into it for the testability story, but here it adds a layer that the prior phase's own wording didn't ask for, and the thing you'd normally buy with that seam — being able to fake persistence in a test — is exactly the thing we already agreed NOT to do, because the two-independent-`ModelContainer` test methodology only proves anything if it goes through real SwiftData, not a protocol stub. So the repository layer doesn't earn its complexity here. I'd rather keep `SessionViewModel` holding the live `Session` `@Model` object directly and calling `context.save()` itself — one less indirection, and it matches what was already signed off on.

On `GameRulesEngine` as a protocol with instance methods versus my enum of static functions — I want to push on this a bit harder than "taste." A protocol implies there's a reason to swap implementations or inject a fake one. There isn't: this is pure math with zero side effects, zero state, and (per the locked scope) exactly one game mode for this slice. Making it a protocol means every call site needs a concrete instance (`DefaultGameRulesEngine()`) threaded through init or DI just to call `nextTarget(...)`, for something Swift Testing can call as a bare static function with no setup at all. I don't see what the protocol buys us here — if you've got a concrete case where a second implementation would plug in, I'm open to hearing it, but "we might want to swap it later" isn't a real requirement we've locked anywhere, and I'd rather not add polymorphism for a hypothetical.

Now the interesting one — target history and tally — because I think there's actually a better answer than either of our first drafts, and it comes out of taking your simpler `targetHistory: [UUID]` seriously. You're right that I over-engineered `TargetHistoryEntry` with an explicit `roundIndex` — that index is redundant, since array position already encodes "how many completed reveals ago" this was, and `Session.currentRoundIndex` already tracks the separate concept of total rounds played (including any that got skipped without a reveal, e.g. the empty-pack mid-round exit). So drop `TargetHistoryEntry` entirely — good catch, I'm taking that.

But here's where I want to push back on the *tally* question, because there's a hard requirement neither of our tally designs actually satisfies yet: the locked recap copy rules from detailed_discussion say the low-variance case has to surface "the single funniest/most notable reveal moment from the session" — not a stat, an actual specific moment. Your `deriveRecap(players:, tally: [UUID:Int], totalRounds:, promptsPlayed:) -> RecapResult` literally cannot produce that, because a frequency count has no memory of which prompt or which specific round it's referring to. That's not a style gap, it's a missing capability — the fallback copy needs a specific prompt + specific target from a specific round, and a `tally` dictionary throws that information away the moment it increments a counter. So I don't think we can settle for either "stored tally" or "derived tally" alone — we need `revealedRounds: [RevealedRoundRecord]` (round index, active, target, promptID, promptText) to exist regardless, because that's the only thing that can answer "which moment." And once that array exists, it already contains every target ID in order, which means it can do double duty as the recency data `nextTarget` needs — no separate `targetHistory` array required at all, `revealedRounds.map(\.targetPlayerID)` is exactly the same information. That also kills the exact sync-bug shape I flagged for `playerIDs`+names last round: two parallel arrays that have to grow together every single round is precisely the kind of thing that quietly drifts. One array, both jobs. I think this is a strictly better answer than either of our original designs, not a compromise — happy to hear a counter-argument but I think this one's clean.

On active player addressing — I'm sticking with `activePlayerIndex: Int` over your `activePlayerID: UUID`, and here's the concrete reason rather than just preference: the roster is locked immutable for the whole session (no mid-session add/remove, already decided way back), so an index into a fixed array is always valid by construction — rotation is just `(index + 1) % count`, full stop, nothing can fail. A UUID-based "current active player" requires `players.firstIndex(where: { $0.id == activePlayerID })` every time you want to compute "next," which is a lookup that returns an `Optional` you then have to force-unwrap or handle a "shouldn't happen but technically could" branch for — an unnecessary failure mode for state that's mathematically guaranteed not to fail if you just use the index. I don't think this is inconsistent with the fact that `RevealedRoundRecord`/recap fields store UUIDs for historical target/active players — those are portable identity references into a record that outlives "the live rotation pointer," which is a genuinely different job than "whose turn is it right now within a session-scoped fixed array."

Recap persistence is where I want to actually change my answer, prompted by looking hard at your `RecapEntry` with `cardImageData: Data?`. I don't think we should persist rendered image bytes at all — a 9:16 image at 3x scale is a multi-megabyte PNG, and SwiftData isn't a great place to store that as an inline blob. The render is deterministic given the session's data, so instead of persisting pixels, we persist the *inputs* (which we already have to, for termination-survival to mean anything) and just re-invoke `RecapCardRenderer.renderImage(for: session)` fresh whenever the recap screen appears — including on resume-from-kill. That means `Session.recapOutcome: RecapOutcome?`, written synchronously the instant the session completes, is both the render's headline content AND the always-available fallback text if `ImageRenderer` fails (the enum's associated `line`/`momentLine` string already is the fallback copy) — one type covering both jobs instead of a separate `RecapEntry` row duplicating text fields. I think this is a real simplification, not a punt: it avoids a whole model, avoids blob storage, and the "hard requirement" from last phase (recap survives a kill between session-end and share-tap) is fully satisfied because the *data to produce the card* survives, and the card itself is cheaply reproducible from it.

Nobody's touched my 9:16 portrait-card pitch yet, so I'm going to just restate it plainly and ask for an explicit yes rather than let it die by default: recap cards get shared into iMessage previews and vertical story surfaces almost exclusively now, and a card shaped like a squarish stat sheet gets cropped or shrunk illegibly in exactly those places. Locking the render frame to 9:16 with a capped "+N more" name footer instead of a full roster is cheap, it's a rendering decision not a new feature, and it's directly in service of the one thing this whole project has said twice now is the actual bar ("the share flow works and the card is worth sending"). If nobody has a concrete objection, I want this locked as decided, not left dangling into build.

Last thing — error naming. I'll just take your `CampfireError` name over my `RecoverableError` since it reads better as an actual Swift `Error` type, but I want to keep it to exactly the three cases we've both actually locked across prior phases — `storeUnavailable`, `emptyPromptPack`, `recapRenderFailure` — and not add your `promptExhausted`/`persistenceFailure`, since those aren't separately named recoverable states anywhere in the locked history and I don't want scope creeping in through the error enum.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Real progress this round — Codex moved a lot of ground toward Claude's design rather than defending the round-1 split. He dropped the `SessionState`-struct-plus-full-repository pattern, switched `GameRulesEngine` from a protocol to a static-function enum, adopted `activePlayerIndex: Int` over `activePlayerID: UUID`, dropped the stored `tally` dictionary, dropped the separate `RecapEntry` row in favor of `recapOutcome` embedded directly on `Session`, and explicitly signed off on the 9:16 portrait recap card with a capped "+N more" footer. That's five of six round-1 forks landing on (or very near) exactly what Claude proposed — genuine convergence, not a rubber stamp, since Codex gave his own reasoning for each move rather than just conceding.

Two things are still genuinely open, though, and they're small enough that I think this closes next round rather than needing a big renegotiation:

1. **Target history shape.** Claude went further than Codex and collapsed `targetHistory`/`TargetHistoryEntry` entirely into `revealedRounds` (arguing the round-indexed array already encodes recency via array position, and that keeping two arrays in sync every round is exactly the kind of drift bug this project keeps naming as a risk). Codex's draft, written in the same round before he could see that specific consolidation, still carries three overlapping fields (`targetHistory: [UUID]`, `targetHistoryEntries: [TargetHistoryEntry]`, `revealedRounds: [...]`) — which looks like a leftover from his in-progress edit rather than a real disagreement, since he separately agreed tally should be derived, not stored. This needs an explicit "yes, drop the redundant fields" from Codex, not an assumption.
2. **Persistence seam.** Codex still wants a thin `SessionStoreProviding` protocol in front of the real SwiftData model ("kill-and-relaunch tests need swap-in test doubles"). Claude's position is that `SessionViewModel` should hold `Session` and call `context.save()` directly, with no protocol layer, since the actual termination-survival test has to run against real SwiftData through two independent `ModelContainer` instances anyway — a protocol seam wouldn't be exercised by that test and isn't asked for anywhere in the locked history. Nobody's explicitly ruled on this one yet; it's a real fork in the interfaces-json (a `SessionStoreProviding` protocol that would or wouldn't exist), not a stylistic nit.

Everything else — route enum, `RoundPhase` persisted on the model, `PlayerRecord` as the single ordered roster array, `PromptPackRecord.version` instead of a separate pack-version row, `CampfireError`'s three named cases, `ContentAccessProviding` stub, `TransitionGuard`, `ConfettiOverlay` lifecycle cap — is settled and consistent across both agents' latest drafts.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round just needs two yes/no calls from Codex: drop `targetHistory`/`targetHistoryEntries` in favor of `revealedRounds` alone, and whether `SessionStoreProviding` exists as a real protocol seam or gets cut in favor of direct `ModelContext` access in `SessionViewModel`. Everything else is ready to lock as-is.
