### Round 3


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 3**

I want to settle both open forks decisively.

Codex, I agree with you where you shifted in round 2, but your lingering `SessionStoreProviding` seam is the wrong seam here. We already have a hard requirement that restart/kill survival is proven with a real two-container SwiftData re-open test; adding an abstraction over persistence weakens that by encouraging fake-store tests and making the core contract less obvious at call sites. Claude’s point was right: this app’s correctness risk is not “pluggability,” it’s strict persistence fidelity under force-quit, and the only way to keep that honest is to keep the `SessionViewModel` bound to `ModelContext` and `Session` directly. A protocol seam adds one more thing to mock while deleting none of the failure modes we care about.

On the data model, I’m fully aligned with the `revealedRounds`-only consolidation and no separate `targetHistory`/`TargetHistoryEntry`. Keeping both would create the exact drift you both were already trying to eliminate across session state (ordered arrays that can diverge). If we always derive recency from `revealedRounds.map(\.targetPlayerID)`, we get target rotation and recap narrative from one source of truth, and it directly enables the low-variance copy rule (“funniest moment”) without recomputing from a lossy tally. So yes, remove redundant target history fields. Also, use `activePlayerIndex` instead of `activePlayerID` to prevent lookups and force unwrap branches, while storing historical player references in revealed rounds as UUIDs.

I’m also keeping `RecapOutcome` on `Session` and rendering card image on demand from that model data instead of persisting image blobs. This is better for stability and storage. Persisting the data that defines the result (players, rounds, prompt content, outcome line) satisfies the force-quit-to-share requirement even if we restart and regenerate the card in a different process.

For failure behavior, we only keep the three recoverable states everyone already locked: `storeUnavailable`, `emptyPromptPack`, `recapRenderFailure`. No extra custom cases that look cleaner in code but never map to user flows. `storeUnavailable` must fallback to in-memory session with a clear notice before a broken launch can be hidden; `emptyPromptPack` must route to a valid recallable recap path with partial data; `recapRenderFailure` must always expose fallback text share and retry. Keep all domain logic pure and side-effect-free in `GameRulesEngine`, and keep mutations serialized by `@MainActor` with synchronous `context.save()` on every transition.

I want this phase locked as the narrowest reliable architecture that can actually be proven in edge-case play: 2-player strict alternation, 3-player forced rotation, 4+ distribution, no self-target, no target-repeat immediate; kill/relaunch from any of `handoff/ready/revealed` and exact restore; single tap reveals only once with silent lock; recap card generated from persisted outcome and re-rendered when needed.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 3**

Codex, good — you moved further than I expected and I want to close both of the two things the coordinator flagged rather than let them linger into a fourth round, because I think both have clean answers once you look at what each side actually needs to prove.

On `targetHistory`/`targetHistoryEntries` versus just `revealedRounds` — your prose in round 2 already said "I am dropping any stored `tally` map; derive on read from `targetHistory` and/or `revealedRounds`," which tells me you'd already halfway made the same move I was arguing for, but your `interfaces-json` still carried all three arrays (`targetHistory: [UUID]`, `targetHistoryEntries: [TargetHistoryEntry]`, and `revealedRounds`) side by side on the same `Session`. I think that's just the JSON lagging behind your own prose, not a real disagreement — three overlapping arrays that all have to stay in lockstep every single round is the exact failure shape we've both spent two rounds arguing against for `players`/`playerIDs`. So let's just finish the move: `revealedRounds: [RevealedRoundRecord]` is the only array, `nextTarget` derives recency from `revealedRounds.map(\.targetPlayerID)`, and `deriveRecap` gets everything it needs (frequency counts, tie detection, and — critically — the actual prompt text for a specific round when the low-variance case needs to name a real moment) from that same array. One append point per completed reveal, one place a bug could hide instead of three.

On `SessionStoreProviding` — I hear the actual concern behind it, which is "we need swap-in test doubles for kill-and-relaunch tests," but I don't think the protocol buys that. Walk through what the termination test actually has to prove: state written through one `ModelContainer`, then read back through a *second, independent* `ModelContainer` against the same file URL. If `SessionStoreProviding` gets tested against a fake/in-memory conforming type, that test proves nothing about real disk persistence — it's testing your fake, not SwiftData. If it gets tested against the real SwiftData-backed implementation, the protocol layer is just unused ceremony sitting in front of the thing you're actually testing, and now there are two names for "the way you save a session" instead of one. Either way the seam doesn't earn its keep. And for the narrower worry — testing `SessionViewModel`'s phase transitions (the double-tap guard, `revealTarget`, etc.) without booting a full production container every time — SwiftData already solves that natively: construct the test's `Session` against `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)`. That gives you a completely real `Session`/`ModelContext` with `context.save()` actually running, zero setup cost, and it's honestly a *stronger* test than mocking a protocol, because it exercises the exact code path production hits — the only difference is where the bytes physically live. So I'd cut `SessionStoreProviding` entirely, and for the same reason I'd make `AppCoordinator` a concrete `@Observable` class rather than your protocol version — there's exactly one real implementation, nothing anywhere in this project's locked history calls for a second one, and we're not going to fake `ModelContext`-backed state in tests anyway now that we have the in-memory-container pattern. Consistency of principle: no protocol seam anywhere in this app unless something real is going to conform to it twice.

One more thing I want to put on the table before we call this closed, because I think it's a genuine gap in both of our drafts, not a nitpick: neither of us actually specified how the app picks *which* prompt shows up each round. You've got `promptCursor: Int` in your `Session` init, but a cursor into what — the pack's prompts in their original bundled-JSON order? If so, every single session plays out with the identical prompt sequence, which is a real product problem for a party game that's supposed to feel different each time you play it, not a technicality. What we actually need is a `promptOrder: [String]` — the specific prompt IDs shuffled once at session creation and then locked for that session's whole lifetime (same reasoning as everything else here: decide the order once, up front, then never recompute it) — and the "cursor" into that order is just `revealedRounds.count`, so we don't need a separate stored cursor field that could drift from the array that's supposed to track progress. I'd rather catch this now than have two build lanes independently guess at how prompt selection works and end up with mismatched assumptions about replay variety.

I think with those three things — `revealedRounds` as the sole history/tally/recap source, no `SessionStoreProviding` or `AppCoordinator` protocol, and `promptOrder` added to `Session` — everything in this contract is actually settled now, not just converged-in-spirit. Locking `CampfireError` with no associated payload strings (the UI layer owns copy per case, not the error type) since that's a one-line call and not worth another round.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

Both open items from last round got closed cleanly, and closed the same direction. Codex's round 3 message explicitly agreed to drop `targetHistory`/`targetHistoryEntries` in favor of `revealedRounds` alone as the single source for rotation recency and recap derivation, and explicitly agreed to cut `SessionStoreProviding` — conceding that a protocol seam tested against a fake proves nothing about real SwiftData persistence, and tested against the real implementation is just unused ceremony. His round 3 interfaces-json matches that: `SessionViewModel` wraps `Session`/`ModelContext` directly, and `AppCoordinator` dropped the protocol wrapper he'd floated in round 2 in favor of a concrete class, matching Claude's stance for the same reason (no second implementation is ever going to exist).

Claude used her round 3 turn to catch one more real gap neither draft had actually specified: how the app decides which prompt shows up each round. Codex's draft had a `promptCursor: Int` at various points but never defined what it was a cursor *into* — which, unaddressed, means every session could play back the exact same prompt sequence, undermining "feels different each time you play" for a party game. Her fix — shuffle prompt IDs once at session start into `Session.promptOrder: [String]`, with `revealedRounds.count` doing double duty as the cursor instead of a separately stored field that could drift — is a small, clean addition that doesn't compete with anything Codex proposed; he simply hadn't gotten to it. Nothing in his round 3 message contradicts it, and it directly serves a risk this project has flagged since the very first phases (prompt content/variety is the whole product). I'm folding it in as settled rather than spinning a fourth round over an addition nobody has reason to object to.

Everything else — direct `Session` SwiftData model, `activePlayerIndex: Int`, no stored tally, `RecapOutcome` embedded and re-rendered on demand rather than persisted as a blob, the 9:16 card lock, the three-case `CampfireError`, the stub `ContentAccessProviding`, `TransitionGuard`, capped `ConfettiOverlay` — has been stable and mutually confirmed across two straight rounds now.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** Three-layer split, single Xcode target with folders (not multiple Swift packages — one game mode doesn't justify that ceremony). A pure, UI/persistence-free domain layer (`GameRulesEngine` as a static-function enum: rotation, target selection, prompt shuffling, recap derivation) that's fully unit-testable with zero SwiftUI or SwiftData involved. An orchestration layer (`AppCoordinator`, a concrete `@MainActor @Observable` class owning the root route enum and the resume-on-launch path; `SessionViewModel`, a concrete `@MainActor @Observable` class wrapping the persisted `Session` model directly and calling `context.save()` synchronously after every mutation). A presentation layer of SwiftUI views that only read state and dispatch intents. No protocol seam exists anywhere unless a second real implementation is actually going to conform to it — `SessionStoreProviding` was proposed and explicitly cut on that basis; `PromptRepositoryProviding` survives because a future remote pack catalog is a named future requirement that would genuinely conform to it.

**File/module layout:**
- `App/` — `CampfireApp.swift` (owns `ModelContainer` construction and the store-failure fallback), `AppCoordinator.swift`, `AppRoute.swift`
- `Domain/` — `GameRulesEngine`, `RecapOutcome`, `PlayerSetupValidation`, `RoundPhase`, `CampfireError` (Foundation-only imports)
- `Models/` — SwiftData `@Model` types (`Session`, `PromptPackRecord`) plus their embedded `Codable` value types (`PlayerRecord`, `RevealedRoundRecord`)
- `Services/` — `ModelContainerProvider`, `PromptRepositoryProviding` implementation, `ContentAccessProviding` stub, `HapticsServicing`, `RecapCardRendering`
- `Features/` — one folder per screen: `Entry`, `Setup`, `Session`, `Recap`
- `DesignSystem/` — token files and shared components from the design handoff (`CampfireGradientSurface`, `ErrorStateView`, etc.)
- `Resources/` — bundled seed pack JSON
- `Tests/` — `GameRulesEngineTests`, `RecapDerivationTests`, `PersistenceRestoreTests`, `DoubleTapGuardTests` (Swift Testing, `@Test`/`#expect`)

**Data models:** `Session` is a single `@Model` class — no separate domain struct, no repository split. `players: [PlayerRecord]` is the one ordered roster array (fixed for the session, no mid-session edits, already locked). `activePlayerIndex: Int` addresses the current turn by index into that fixed array (not a UUID lookup — rotation is `(index + 1) % count` with no optional-unwrap failure mode). `promptOrder: [String]` is the prompt-ID sequence shuffled once at session creation and locked for the session's lifetime; `revealedRounds.count` is the cursor into it, so there's no separate stored cursor that could drift from the array tracking actual progress. `revealedRounds: [RevealedRoundRecord]` is the single history array — it does triple duty as target-recency source (`nextTarget` derives from `revealedRounds.map(\.targetPlayerID)`), tally source (frequency/tie derivation for recap), and recap-moment source (the specific prompt text a low-variance "funniest moment" line needs to reference) — no separate `targetHistory`, no stored `tally` dictionary, one append point per completed reveal instead of multiple arrays that must stay in lockstep. `recapOutcome: RecapOutcome?` is written the instant a session completes, not deferred to share-tap, and is never rendered to a persisted image blob — the recap card is deterministically re-rendered on demand (including after resume-from-kill) from the session data that's already there. `PromptPackRecord` carries `version: Int` directly rather than a separate `PackVersion` row, a named simplification appropriate for one bundled pack.

**Persistence strategy:** SwiftData on iOS 17+. Every state-mutating method on `SessionViewModel` runs on the main actor and calls a synchronous `try context.save()` immediately after mutation — no batching, no `Task`-wrapped saves that could be cancelled or delayed past a kill signal. `ModelContainerProvider.makeContainer()` returns a result type; failure (corrupt store, migration mismatch, disk error) is a named, caught `CampfireError.storeUnavailable`, with the app falling back to a fresh in-memory session and a visible "starting fresh" notice — never a crash or silent data loss. `resumeInProgressSessionIfAny` is its own explicitly tested launch code path, not an incidental fallthrough of the fresh-start path.

**Testing strategy:** `GameRulesEngine`'s pure functions get first-class unit tests at 2, 3, 4, and 15 players — no self-target, no immediate repeat, correct degrading behavior at low counts, plus recap derivation across standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data. A double-tap guard test fires `revealTarget` twice in rapid succession and asserts exactly one transition and one haptic call. Termination-survival is tested by saving through one `ModelContainer` instance and verifying through a second, independent `ModelContainer` against the same file URL — reusing a live container isn't accepted as proof. For `SessionViewModel`-level tests that don't need real termination proof, an in-memory `ModelConfiguration` gives a fully real `Session`/`ModelContext` with `context.save()` actually running — a stronger test than a mock protocol, since it's the exact code path production hits. `ImageRenderer` output and the physical `ShareLink` sheet stay a documented, actually-performed on-device manual check. StoreKit testing remains explicitly out of scope — `ContentAccessProviding`'s stub is architecturally replaceable but not yet tested against real entitlements.

## Feedback
This closes the phase cleanly. All six required outputs are present and concrete: architecture overview (three-layer split — pure `GameRulesEngine` domain layer, concrete `AppCoordinator`/`SessionViewModel` orchestration, presentation views that only dispatch intents), file/module layout (folder-per-concern in a single target, explicit test file list), data models (a single direct `Session` `@Model` with no repository/state-struct duplication, `revealedRounds` doing triple duty as rotation/tally/recap-moment source), persistence strategy (synchronous per-mutation saves, named `ModelContainerProvider` failure path, resume-on-launch as its own tested path), and testing strategy (pure-function tests at 2/3/4/15 players, double-tap guard test, two-independent-`ModelContainer` termination proof, explicit on-device-manual carve-out for `ImageRenderer`/`ShareLink`, explicit StoreKit-out-of-scope statement).

The round-3 exchange shows real convergence, not restatement: both agents explicitly agreed to collapse `targetHistory`/`targetHistoryEntries`/`tally` into the single `revealedRounds` array, and both explicitly agreed to cut the `SessionStoreProviding` protocol seam with a correct, shared rationale (a fake proves nothing about real SwiftData persistence; the real implementation makes the protocol unused ceremony) — while correctly keeping `PromptRepositoryProviding` since a future remote pack catalog gives it a real second conformer. Claude's round-3 addition (`promptOrder` shuffled once at session creation, with `revealedRounds.count` as the cursor) closes a real, previously-unspecified gap — without it every session would replay prompts in identical bundled-JSON order, undermining the product's core replay-variety value — and nothing in Codex's draft contradicts it.

The output is consistent with every earlier locked phase: the six-screen/no-`NavigationStack` constraint, the exclude-active/exclude-last-target/least-recently-targeted rotation rule, the three named recoverable `CampfireError` cases, the `ContentAccessProviding` stub marked provisional, and the synchronous-save/two-container termination-test bar from `ios_architecture_review`. The `interfaces-json` block is complete, correctly lane-tagged, and gives parallel build lanes a single consistent type contract to code against. No repair needed.

## Final Output

Both open items from last round got closed cleanly, and closed the same direction. Codex's round 3 message explicitly agreed to drop `targetHistory`/`targetHistoryEntries` in favor of `revealedRounds` alone as the single source for rotation recency and recap derivation, and explicitly agreed to cut `SessionStoreProviding` — conceding that a protocol seam tested against a fake proves nothing about real SwiftData persistence, and tested against the real implementation is just unused ceremony. His round 3 interfaces-json matches that: `SessionViewModel` wraps `Session`/`ModelContext` directly, and `AppCoordinator` dropped the protocol wrapper he'd floated in round 2 in favor of a concrete class, matching Claude's stance for the same reason (no second implementation is ever going to exist).

Claude used her round 3 turn to catch one more real gap neither draft had actually specified: how the app decides which prompt shows up each round. Codex's draft had a `promptCursor: Int` at various points but never defined what it was a cursor *into* — which, unaddressed, means every session could play back the exact same prompt sequence, undermining "feels different each time you play" for a party game. Her fix — shuffle prompt IDs once at session start into `Session.promptOrder: [String]`, with `revealedRounds.count` doing double duty as the cursor instead of a separately stored field that could drift — is a small, clean addition that doesn't compete with anything Codex proposed; he simply hadn't gotten to it. Nothing in his round 3 message contradicts it, and it directly serves a risk this project has flagged since the very first phases (prompt content/variety is the whole product). I'm folding it in as settled rather than spinning a fourth round over an addition nobody has reason to object to.

Everything else — direct `Session` SwiftData model, `activePlayerIndex: Int`, no stored tally, `RecapOutcome` embedded and re-rendered on demand rather than persisted as a blob, the 9:16 card lock, the three-case `CampfireError`, the stub `ContentAccessProviding`, `TransitionGuard`, capped `ConfettiOverlay` — has been stable and mutually confirmed across two straight rounds now.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** Three-layer split, single Xcode target with folders (not multiple Swift packages — one game mode doesn't justify that ceremony). A pure, UI/persistence-free domain layer (`GameRulesEngine` as a static-function enum: rotation, target selection, prompt shuffling, recap derivation) that's fully unit-testable with zero SwiftUI or SwiftData involved. An orchestration layer (`AppCoordinator`, a concrete `@MainActor @Observable` class owning the root route enum and the resume-on-launch path; `SessionViewModel`, a concrete `@MainActor @Observable` class wrapping the persisted `Session` model directly and calling `context.save()` synchronously after every mutation). A presentation layer of SwiftUI views that only read state and dispatch intents. No protocol seam exists anywhere unless a second real implementation is actually going to conform to it — `SessionStoreProviding` was proposed and explicitly cut on that basis; `PromptRepositoryProviding` survives because a future remote pack catalog is a named future requirement that would genuinely conform to it.

**File/module layout:**
- `App/` — `CampfireApp.swift` (owns `ModelContainer` construction and the store-failure fallback), `AppCoordinator.swift`, `AppRoute.swift`
- `Domain/` — `GameRulesEngine`, `RecapOutcome`, `PlayerSetupValidation`, `RoundPhase`, `CampfireError` (Foundation-only imports)
- `Models/` — SwiftData `@Model` types (`Session`, `PromptPackRecord`) plus their embedded `Codable` value types (`PlayerRecord`, `RevealedRoundRecord`)
- `Services/` — `ModelContainerProvider`, `PromptRepositoryProviding` implementation, `ContentAccessProviding` stub, `HapticsServicing`, `RecapCardRendering`
- `Features/` — one folder per screen: `Entry`, `Setup`, `Session`, `Recap`
- `DesignSystem/` — token files and shared components from the design handoff (`CampfireGradientSurface`, `ErrorStateView`, etc.)
- `Resources/` — bundled seed pack JSON
- `Tests/` — `GameRulesEngineTests`, `RecapDerivationTests`, `PersistenceRestoreTests`, `DoubleTapGuardTests` (Swift Testing, `@Test`/`#expect`)

**Data models:** `Session` is a single `@Model` class — no separate domain struct, no repository split. `players: [PlayerRecord]` is the one ordered roster array (fixed for the session, no mid-session edits, already locked). `activePlayerIndex: Int` addresses the current turn by index into that fixed array (not a UUID lookup — rotation is `(index + 1) % count` with no optional-unwrap failure mode). `promptOrder: [String]` is the prompt-ID sequence shuffled once at session creation and locked for the session's lifetime; `revealedRounds.count` is the cursor into it, so there's no separate stored cursor that could drift from the array tracking actual progress. `revealedRounds: [RevealedRoundRecord]` is the single history array — it does triple duty as target-recency source (`nextTarget` derives from `revealedRounds.map(\.targetPlayerID)`), tally source (frequency/tie derivation for recap), and recap-moment source (the specific prompt text a low-variance "funniest moment" line needs to reference) — no separate `targetHistory`, no stored `tally` dictionary, one append point per completed reveal instead of multiple arrays that must stay in lockstep. `recapOutcome: RecapOutcome?` is written the instant a session completes, not deferred to share-tap, and is never rendered to a persisted image blob — the recap card is deterministically re-rendered on demand (including after resume-from-kill) from the session data that's already there. `PromptPackRecord` carries `version: Int` directly rather than a separate `PackVersion` row, a named simplification appropriate for one bundled pack.

**Persistence strategy:** SwiftData on iOS 17+. Every state-mutating method on `SessionViewModel` runs on the main actor and calls a synchronous `try context.save()` immediately after mutation — no batching, no `Task`-wrapped saves that could be cancelled or delayed past a kill signal. `ModelContainerProvider.makeContainer()` returns a result type; failure (corrupt store, migration mismatch, disk error) is a named, caught `CampfireError.storeUnavailable`, with the app falling back to a fresh in-memory session and a visible "starting fresh" notice — never a crash or silent data loss. `resumeInProgressSessionIfAny` is its own explicitly tested launch code path, not an incidental fallthrough of the fresh-start path.

**Testing strategy:** `GameRulesEngine`'s pure functions get first-class unit tests at 2, 3, 4, and 15 players — no self-target, no immediate repeat, correct degrading behavior at low counts, plus recap derivation across standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data. A double-tap guard test fires `revealTarget` twice in rapid succession and asserts exactly one transition and one haptic call. Termination-survival is tested by saving through one `ModelContainer` instance and verifying through a second, independent `ModelContainer` against the same file URL — reusing a live container isn't accepted as proof. For `SessionViewModel`-level tests that don't need real termination proof, an in-memory `ModelConfiguration` gives a fully real `Session`/`ModelContext` with `context.save()` actually running — a stronger test than a mock protocol, since it's the exact code path production hits. `ImageRenderer` output and the physical `ShareLink` sheet stay a documented, actually-performed on-device manual check. StoreKit testing remains explicitly out of scope — `ContentAccessProviding`'s stub is architecturally replaceable but not yet tested against real entitlements.

```interfaces-json
  {"name": "ConfettiOverlay", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct ConfettiOverlay: View { let isActive: Bool }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "LOCKED: hard-capped 500-700ms lifecycle, torn down immediately after burst, never redraws into later rounds."}
]}
```

---

CONSENSUS: YES

title: Project Plan
output:
Good news — this round actually lands the plane. Codex adopted the 8.0-day target and rewrote the gate breakdown as 8 gates (0.5 / 1.0 / 0.5 / 1.5 / 1.5 / 1.5 / 1.0 / 0.5), and that itemization genuinely sums to exactly 8.0 — the arithmetic finally checks out on its own, not just as an asserted round number. Claude's round-5 message raises the same arithmetic concern she's been pushing for two rounds, but it's checking an earlier (round-4) set of numbers rather than Codex's corrected round-5 breakdown — and her own proposed fix (itemized sum plus a named contingency, landing at 8.0) arrives at the identical total Codex already produced independently. Both sides are at the same number via compatible reasoning, and Claude explicitly says she's ready to lock.

Everything structural is genuinely settled now, confirmed independently by both agents across multiple rounds: domain logic (`GameRulesEngine`/`RecapOutcome`) stays untouched no matter what the SwiftData spike finds, with any persistence-shape fallback living strictly behind a conversion step inside `SessionViewModel` (Codex stated this in his own words this round, not just non-objection); gates 1, 3, and 4 are hard non-skippable failure gates before any reveal UI is considered done; error-injection hooks for the two forced-failure states are their own real line item; editorial prompt QA has a named owner, a concrete signed artifact, and explicitly gates recap-copy release without being on the critical path; and `ContentAccessProviding`/`isPackUnlocked` is wired into the real prompt-load call site starting at gate one, not declared-and-deferred.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Milestones (8 gates, 8.0 engineer-days critical path):**

- **Gate 1 — Contract & content truth lock (0.5d):** Freeze the final `Session`/`RevealedRoundRecord`/`RecapOutcome`/etc. shapes exactly as locked in tech_specs. Lock design-token baseline (gradients, typography, motion timings). Import the seed prompt pack JSON into project scaffolding. Wire `ContentAccessProviding.isPackUnlocked` into the real prompt-retrieval call site today (still returns `true`, but it's actually called, not just declared). Kick off editorial Pack QA with a named owner, running in parallel from here through gate 6 — output is a signed markdown doc (duplicate check, tone matrix, family-safe edge-case review) that must be complete before recap copy can be marked done.
- **Gate 2 — Pure domain correctness (1.0d):** Build and fully test `GameRulesEngine` (rotation, target selection, `shufflePromptOrder`, `deriveRecap`) and `PlayerSetupValidation`. Test matrix: 2/3/4/15 players, no self-target, no immediate target repeat, deterministic recap derivation across standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data. Zero SwiftData, zero UI dependency — fully provable in isolation.
- **Gate 3 — SwiftData persistence spike (0.5d):** Write a real `Session` (including `RecapOutcome` and `revealedRounds`) through one `ModelContainer`, read it back through a second independent container. Named exit branch: round-trips clean → keep current shape; fails → adopt a conversion layer inside `SessionViewModel` only, `GameRulesEngine` never changes. Decision recorded either way before gate 4 starts.
- **Gate 4 — Container & orchestration proof (1.5d):** `ModelContainerProvider` with in-memory fallback + visible "starting fresh" notice on store failure, resume-in-progress launch path, `SessionViewModel` transition methods with synchronous `context.save()` after every mutation. Hard go/no-go proof: write mid-round in container A, reopen container B from the same file URL, assert exact `roundPhase`/`activePlayerIndex`/cursor/`revealedRounds` equivalence. Failure here halts all downstream gates until repaired.
- **Gate 5 — Transition mechanics & anti-regression (1.5d):** Handoff/ready/revealed transitions, silent one-shot double-tap guard, haptics binding, prompt-exhaustion early-exit into recap. All proven via in-memory `ModelConfiguration` tests before any SwiftUI view exists.
- **Gate 6 — Core screens + recap pipeline (1.5d):** Entry/Setup/Session/Recap screens, single-route no-`NavigationStack` shell, hidden-until-reveal target, resume route, `isIdleTimerDisabled` scoped to active rounds, full empty/loading/success/error states. Recap card: fixed 9:16 render at 3x, capped "+N more" footer, deterministic text fallback on render failure, physical-device share verification.
- **Gate 7 — Error-injection, accessibility & stability sweep (1.0d):** Debug-only forced-failure toggles for `emptyPromptPack` and `recapRenderFailure` (real engineering, not just verification), confirming all three `CampfireError` recovery paths are actually reachable. Accessibility pass: 44pt targets, Dynamic Type truncation on the two-column header, VoiceOver state-order narration.
- **Gate 8 — Integration audit & release package (0.5d):** Signed pass/fail log covering 2-player and 12–15-player sessions with background + force-quit at every round phase, on-device share success, and a written known-limitations note (StoreKit entitlement explicitly deferred). Go/no-go handoff to the next phase.

**Dependencies:** Strictly linear on the risk-bearing spine — Gate 1 unlocks everything; Gate 2 (domain) has zero dependency on Gates 3/4 and can run fully in parallel with design-token/component work; Gate 3 depends only on Gate 1's frozen model shapes; Gate 4 depends on Gates 2 and 3 passing; Gate 5 depends on Gate 4's proven persistence contract; Gate 6 depends on Gate 5; Gate 7 depends on Gate 6 existing to sweep; Gate 8 depends on all prior gates. Editorial QA runs parallel from Gate 1, gating only recap-copy release (by Gate 6), never blocking logic/persistence work.

**Risk mitigations:** Gates 1, 3, and 4 are named hard-stop gates — if any fails, feature work pauses until repaired rather than continuing on an unproven foundation. The `RecapOutcome`/`GameRulesEngine` domain contract is explicitly protected from being "fixed" by reaching backward into it if SwiftData proves awkward — any fallback lives only in `SessionViewModel`'s save/load conversion step. Error-injection hooks and editorial QA are both costed as real work with named owners rather than folded silently into "polish." `ImageRenderer`/`ShareLink` and the two-container termination proof are both treated as documented, actually-performed checks, not simulator-only assumptions.

**Verification plan:** Scripted — `GameRulesEngine` unit tests at 2/3/4/15 players; double-tap guard test asserting exactly one transition/haptic; two-independent-`ModelContainer` termination-restore test. Manual but documented — 2-player and 12–15-player full playthroughs with a background and a force-quit at each of handoff/ready/revealed; empty-pack and recap-render-failure runs via the debug injection hooks; physical-device share-sheet check. Final acceptance: one complete offline session reaches a working, shareable recap card with zero unreachable or dead-end error states, and Gate 8's signed pass/fail log is the record of it.

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce milestones, sequencing, estimates, dependencies, and a risk-managed delivery plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Delivery Lead · Visionary) — Round 1**

This should be treated as a single, aggressive but disciplined milestone train: deliver a genuinely fun, pass-and-play reveal/confession core loop first, then harden it with persistence and reveal quality, then only then wrap the production scaffolding (ShareLink, accessibility, and launch safety). I’d bias schedule toward correctness of session state and replayable social delight over superficial features.

Milestones:
- Milestone 1 — Contract-to-code lock (0.5 day): finalize one source-of-truth technical spec for this slice in the repo’s `initial_prompt.md` lineage and codify the locked interfaces exactly as agreed (`Session`, `RevealedRoundRecord`, `RoundPhase`, `GameRulesEngine`, `AppCoordinator`, `SessionViewModel`, `promptOrder`, three `CampfireError` cases). Deliverable: interfaces JSON + task breakdown in a `project_plan_notes.md` entry with explicit “done” acceptance for every interface.
- Milestone 2 — Foundation and data layer (1.0 day): scaffold Xcode target in `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/campfire`, configure SwiftData models, `ModelContainerProvider`, `PromptRepository` seed loader, and seeded pack asset. Add startup fallback path for container failures and explicit in-memory recovery path. Deliverable: app launches to Entry even on store failure with visible recovery message.
- Milestone 3 — Core domain + deterministic game engine (1.0 day): implement pure `GameRulesEngine` and tests first (this is the hardest correctness gate): target selection (exclude active and last target, least-recently-targeted), active-rotation, prompt shuffle at session creation (`promptOrder`), and recap derivation (standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data) from `revealedRounds`. Deliverable: passing unit tests for synthetic sessions at 2, 3, 4, 15 players.
- Milestone 4 — Session orchestration + persistence reliability (1.0 day): implement `SessionViewModel` and `AppCoordinator` with persisted round-state machine (`handoff/ready/revealed`), synchronous `context.save()` on each state transition, resume-from-kill proof path, and end-of-session recap outcome persistence. Deliverable: clean recovery after app termination mid-round with no duplicated prompts/targets/tally; deterministic resume into exact phase.
- Milestone 5 — Reveal flow UI (1.5 days): build entry/setup/reveal/recap screens and components with robust state/error states, oversized controls, handoff delay + skip, delayed target reveal, transition guard, confetti, haptics, and no back-swipe state leaks. Deliverable: one full reveal session from 2 and 12–15 players that reaches recap without ambiguity.
- Milestone 6 — Shareable recap pipeline (1.0 day): implement `RecapCardView`, `RecapCardRenderer` via `ImageRenderer` at fixed 9:16 scale (3x), copy-safe tie/low-variance copy, fallback text summary + retry, ShareLink integration, and privacy note on share. Deliverable: physical-device verified recap generation and share sheet.
- Milestone 7 — Accessibility, reliability hardening, and polish sweep (1.0 day): dynamic-type wrapping tests in UI components, contrast-safe token checks, VoiceOver role/state order, row-level validation copy, and deterministic no dead-end behavior for all named errors (`empty pack`, `render failure`, `store unavailable`). Deliverable: all four states on every reachable screen (empty/loading/success/error) and explicit recover actions.
- Milestone 8 — Final integration + delivery audit (0.5 day): run build + schema checks, verify screen set complete, confirm no placeholder-only flows in active screens, validate paywall seam stub is clearly temporary (`isPackUnlocked` true-for-now), and produce launch-ready summary with known known-limits and next-up work list. Deliverable: local compiles, deterministic session, signed-off milestone handoff for next phase.

Dependencies:
- Milestone 2 depends on Milestone 1; Milestone 3 and tests can start immediately after schema lock and run independently of UI implementation.
- Milestone 4 depends on Milestone 2 and consumes Milestone 3 outputs.
- Milestone 5 depends on Milestone 4 for persisted round model and transition semantics.
- Milestone 6 depends on Milestone 5 for `SessionViewModel` stability and recap outcomes.
- Milestone 7 depends on Milestones 2–6 and should parallelize with final QA of 5 and 6 where safe.
- Milestone 8 depends on all prior milestones.

Time model and sequencing rationale:
- Total target window: ~6.5 engineer-days for v1 slice, with about 35–40% front-loaded into correctness gates and persistence reliability.
- Mainline dependency chain is domain logic → persistence orchestration → UI → output generation.
- Parallelizable workstreams: UI component styling can overlap with domain unit tests, but no parallel on state model evolution once core `Session` shape is locked.
- Keep one integration branch; merge only at milestone boundaries to reduce drift risk from state-machine edits.

Risk-managed controls:
- Biggest risk is state drift (wrong turn/target/recap). Mitigation: single history source (`revealedRounds`) and derive everything from it; never maintain separate target/prompt cursor state that can diverge.
- Biggest risk is social quality failure (no one wants to share). Mitigation: design recap card first as a real artifact before optional mechanics or paywall expansion; block “finished” acceptance until copy + visual pass creates one clear standout card moment.
- Biggest technical risk is false confidence from simulators. Mitigation: at least one planned on-device check for `ImageRenderer` + ShareLink and physical pass/fail notes in milestones, not assumptions.
- Biggest local resilience risk is termination mid-round. Mitigation: explicit two-container restoration test and manual force-quit rehearsal from each round phase.
- Biggest scope-risk is creeping into unreleased modes/paywall. Mitigation: Coming Soon tiles are non-interactive informational placeholders only; all coding energy stays on one mechanic until all green gates pass.

Verification plan (must pass before marking done):
- Scripted/automated:
  - `GameRulesEngine`: unit tests for target and active-turn invariants at 2/3/4/15 players; no self-target, no immediate repeat.
  - `SessionViewModel`: double-tap guard test for exactly one transition and one haptic call; round completion writes recap outcome and state save synchronously.
  - persistence restore test: write via first `ModelContainer`, reopen via separate container instance from same file URL, assert exact active player/phase/prompt cursor/round count/reveal tally equivalence.
- Manual but documented:
  - 2-player and 12–15-player real playthroughs with one background and one force-quit at each phase (`handoff`, `ready`, `revealed`) with seamless resume.
  - empty pack run and recap-render-failure fallback run.
  - physical-device share flow for rendered 9:16 card plus share sheet launch.
  - accessibility pass for 44pt targets, row error inline validation, and clear state narration.
- Final acceptance criteria:
  - one complete offline reveal session completes to recap with valid data; shareable card generation works; and no known reachable state has no recovery action.

Deliverable preservation requirements for this plan:
- Keep model and routing exactly single-route and non-navigation-stack.
- Keep prompt content fully local, no accounts, no networking for core loop.
- Keep reveal mode only, with two Coming Soon placeholders non-interactive and explicit in copy/accessibility.
- Keep `isPackUnlocked` seam but explicit temporary stub, never silent free-behavior.
- Keep deterministic local-first behavior and avoid committing to StoreKit validation until a dedicated paywall phase.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Looking at this from the persistence/data-model side, the interfaces are genuinely locked and clean, but a project plan built purely off the interfaces-json risks scheduling by "which type exists" instead of by "what can actually break and when do we find out." A few things I want on record before anyone drafts milestones.

First, the single riskiest item in this entire build is the two-independent-ModelContainer termination test, and it's exactly the kind of thing that's easy to leave until "integration polish" near the end — at which point discovering a real bug in it means reworking `Session`'s save discipline after five screens have already been built against it. I want that test built and passing against a bare-bones `Session`/`SessionViewModel` before a single round-screen view exists, not after. If the persistence contract is wrong, everything downstream inherits the bug, so proving it early is the highest-leverage sequencing move available, not a nice-to-have.

Second, nobody has actually verified that SwiftData can cleanly persist what we've locked into `Session`: `players: [PlayerRecord]` and `revealedRounds: [RevealedRoundRecord]` as arrays of plain Codable structs are fine, but `recapOutcome: RecapOutcome?` is an enum with associated values of mixed types (`UUID`, `String`, `[UUID]`) sitting as a stored property directly on an `@Model` class. Swift can synthesize `Codable` for that shape, and SwiftData generally handles Codable-conforming properties via external storage, but "generally handles" is not the same as "verified against this exact shape on this exact SwiftData version." This needs a 30-minute spike — write one, kill the process, reopen a second container, read it back — before it gets baked into five interconnected view models. If it turns out SwiftData chokes on the associated-value enum, the fallback (flatten to a small set of optional scalar fields, or store as a separate `Data` blob) is cheap now and expensive after the fact.

Third, I want the build sequenced strictly by dependency, not by "screen order," because the two don't match here: domain layer (`GameRulesEngine`, `PlayerSetupValidation`, `CampfireError`) has zero dependencies and should be first and fully unit-tested — 2/3/4/15 players, no self-target, no immediate repeat — before any SwiftData or SwiftUI code exists at all. Data models and the prompt-pack seed loader come next, since the domain layer needs real `PlayerRecord`/`RevealedRoundRecord` shapes to test against meaningfully. `SessionViewModel` plus the termination-survival proof comes third, still with zero UI. Only after that should screens get built, and design-system tokens/components can be built in parallel with all of the above since they have no dependency on any of it. Recap rendering and the physical-device `ShareLink` check should not be scheduled as "last polish" — rendering is dependent only on `Session` data existing, so it can start as soon as step two is done, in parallel with round-screen UI work, which buys back the calendar time that on-device flakiness (`ImageRenderer` gradient/text-layering issues, already flagged in earlier phases) will eat into if it's discovered late.

Fourth — and this is the one I think is most likely to get silently dropped under time pressure — the debug-only simulation hooks for triggering `emptyPromptPack` and `recapRenderFailure` are real engineering work, not test-writing. Someone has to build an actual seam to force those states on demand; if that seam doesn't exist, "verify the error state is reachable" quietly turns into "assume the error state is reachable," which is precisely the failure mode this whole project has been trying to design out. I want that scaffolding as its own line item with its own estimate, not folded into "error handling" as if it's free.

Fifth, the editorial prompt-pack QA pass got explicitly named a "should, not a testable criterion" two phases ago, with a warning attached that skipping it is a real risk, not a neutral cut. A project plan that doesn't put a calendared task and an owner against that is quietly overruling that warning. I want it scheduled as a real milestone with a defined output (a reviewed, deduplicated, tone-checked prompt list signed off before it's treated as launch content), sitting in parallel with UI build so it doesn't become the thing that gets cut when the schedule slips.

Sixth, on the `ContentAccessProviding` stub: it's locked as always-`true` and explicitly provisional, but I want the plan to require that it's actually wired into every prompt-loading call site now — not declared and left unused while prompt loading bypasses it directly. If the seam isn't exercised by real call sites today, "drop in real StoreKit later" becomes "go find every place that should have checked entitlement and didn't," which defeats the entire point of building the seam early.

The biggest objection to my own framing here: front-loading domain-and-persistence-before-any-UI risks a long stretch where nothing visible exists to demo or sanity-check against the design handoff, and if the rules-engine or persistence work runs long, there's schedule pressure to skip straight to UI with an unproven foundation anyway. I think that's an acceptable trade given how many prior phases have flagged wrong-player/wrong-target/lost-state as the specific failure class this app keeps almost shipping with, but it should be named as a real trade-off in the plan, with an explicit checkpoint ("domain + persistence proven, or we stop and descope") rather than an assumed default that everything fits before UI work starts.

What the final plan has to preserve, concretely: the termination test as a gating milestone before UI, not a final-week check; the SwiftData-enum-persistence spike as an explicit early task with a named fallback if it fails; the debug error-simulation hooks costed as real work; the editorial QA pass calendared with an owner and output artifact, not left as an implicit "should"; and the `ContentAccessProviding` seam wired into real call sites as part of the initial build, not deferred.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where the discussion actually stands after round 1: Codex laid out a full 8-milestone train (contract lock → data/foundation scaffolding → pure domain engine with tests → session orchestration/persistence → reveal UI → recap/share pipeline → accessibility/polish → final audit), roughly 6.5 engineer-days, with dependencies chained sensibly (domain and design tokens can run in parallel with nothing else, UI can't start until persistence is proven, recap/share depends on UI stability). The risk register and verification plan are solid and map cleanly onto everything locked in earlier phases.

Claude came at it from the systems/persistence side and didn't reject the shape of the plan, but flagged five concrete things that change how it should be sequenced or scoped, not just how it reads:

1. The two-container termination-survival test is the single highest-risk item in the build and needs to be proven against a bare-bones `Session`/`SessionViewModel` *before* any round-screen UI exists — not folded into "integration polish" near the end, where a discovered bug would mean reworking everything built on top of it.
2. Nobody's actually verified SwiftData can persist `RecapOutcome` (an enum with mixed-type associated values) as a stored property on an `@Model` class — this needs an early 30-minute spike with a named fallback (flatten to scalars, or a separate `Data` blob) if it turns out SwiftData chokes on it.
3. Build order should follow dependency, not screen order: domain layer first (zero dependencies, fully unit-testable alone), then data models/persistence, then `SessionViewModel` with the termination proof, only then UI — while design-system tokens/components can build in parallel the whole time since they depend on none of it. Recap rendering can also start as soon as `Session` data exists, in parallel with round-screen UI, to buy back calendar time against known `ImageRenderer` flakiness.
4. The debug-only hooks to force `emptyPromptPack`/`recapRenderFailure` on demand are real engineering work and need their own line item and estimate, not a "free" subtask of error handling.
5. The editorial prompt-pack QA pass (already flagged as a real risk two phases ago if skipped) needs an actual calendared milestone with an owner and a concrete output artifact, running in parallel with UI so it isn't the thing quietly cut when schedule slips — plus `ContentAccessProviding` needs to be wired into every real prompt-loading call site now, not just declared and left unused.

# campfire — Task Assignments

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 13:01:58._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: campfire

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Campfire

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **3**.

## App Name

Campfire

## Category

Social Party / Icebreaker Game

## One-Sentence Promise

Pass one phone around a group and Campfire runs the icebreaker game, tallies the votes, and hands you a shareable results card.

## Target User

Groups of friends, family at a holiday gathering, a first date, new roommates, a work team at happy hour — anyone in a room together who wants a quick, no-download, no-account party game.

## Painful Moment Solved

Awkward silence at a group hangout, or reaching for a party-game app only to realize everyone needs to download it and make an account before the game can even start.

## Why It Is Different

Single-device pass-and-play — zero accounts, zero other installs, zero networking. One person opens it, picks a game mode, and the phone gets passed around; results render into a shareable recap card that acts as its own invite to next time.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier includes a rotating set of prompt packs; Campfire+ unlocks all prompt packs (family-friendly, spicy, work-safe, couples), custom prompt creation, and unlimited saved session recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $2.99/mo or $19.99/yr — deliberately cheap as an impulse/occasion purchase.

## Local-First MVP Scope

All prompt packs and session history stored locally; no accounts or networking needed for the core loop at all.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows an optional future server-hosted seasonal prompt pack downloader and, later, an opt-in synchronous multi-device mode — neither required for v1 value.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None — intentionally excluded; LLM-generated prompts would break the offline-anywhere promise for no real gain over a well-curated static prompt library.

## Design Direction

High-energy saturated party palette — campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab type, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful bounce transitions, confetti-style haptic-synced reveals.

## App Store Positioning

The party game that starts in 5 seconds on one phone — no downloads for your friends, no account, just pass it around.

## Invalidation Criteria

If session recap cards get generated but essentially never shared, the viral loop isn't firing and the card design/moment needs rework before anything else.

## v1 Build Scope

3-4 game modes with real curated prompt packs, pass-and-play flow, end-of-session recap card + ShareLink, Campfire+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- pick a game mode and player count/names
- run pass-the-phone rounds with prompts
- tally votes/answers per round
- generate & share end-of-session recap card
- browse/unlock prompt packs

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for the recap card
- haptics tuned per game mode
- fully on-device, airplane-mode-proof by design

## Key Risks

- Prompt content quality/appropriateness is the entire product — needs genuinely well-written, tested prompt packs at launch, not placeholder text
- One-off occasion-based usage makes retention harder than daily-habit apps — recap-card sharing is the main lever to bring people back

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a high-energy party/icebreaker game app called Campfire: saturated campfire-orange to hot-pink gradients, bold rounded slab typography, oversized tap targets for pass-the-phone play, playful spring/bounce transitions, a confetti-style reveal moment at the end of each round, feels like a fun physical party prop, not a utility app.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce concrete workstreams, file ownership, forbidden edit zones, branch names, merge strategy, testing responsibilities, communication protocol, and a conflict-prevention plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Here’s how I’d run this round so we don’t quietly reintroduce state bugs or schema debt: keep app scaffolding and architecture in `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/campfire` (Xcode project at `app_build/Campfire.xcodeproj`), with one strict ownership split by lane and explicit handoffs between lanes. I’ll treat `data_domain` as the only source of game truth, `services_utilities` as persistence/access/render adapters, `primary_ui` as coordinator + screen state machine, and `polish_resilience` as tests/accessibility/error-injection/release verification. Biggest risk I’m guarding against is cross-file overlap causing two people to change state flow logic simultaneously; the practical antidote is each lane owning disjoint files and only consuming public APIs/contracts from others.

The concrete handoff sequence is: lock contracts/content + seed assets first, then prove domain correctness, then prove persistence compatibility, then wire orchestration/transition behavior, then build UI, then recap/render/accessibility, then integration proof. Nobody should edit `SessionViewModel`, `AppCoordinator`, or round-state persistence code at the same time as UI composition unless they’re in the same primary lane task and on the same dependency edge. Forbidden cross-edits: `primary_ui` must not modify `GameRulesEngine`, `RevealedRoundRecord`, `RecapOutcome` math; `data_domain` should not own SwiftData container setup or ShareLink rendering; `services_utilities` should not own screen-specific state transitions except via exposed methods. Manual pass/fail proof is mandatory at milestones: two-container restore, 2-player and 12–15-player round-trip with force-quit, duplicate-target rejection, and actual share sheet verification on device.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Here's my read on how this actually gets built, coming at it from the persistence/data-model side and trying to keep the scope honest about what's real engineering versus what looks free but isn't.

The app lives at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/campfire/app_build/Campfire` — one Xcode project (`Campfire.xcodeproj`, app target `Campfire`, test target `CampfireTests`), matching the sibling `app_build` layout every other app in this workspace already uses. Nobody invents a second location or a workspace-of-packages; one target is enough for one game mode.

I'm mapping the four fixed lanes onto the folder layout that's already locked from `ios_architecture_review`/`tech_specs`, because re-deriving that now would just waste time: `data_domain` owns `Domain/` and `Models/` — `GameRulesEngine`, `PlayerSetupValidation`, `RoundPhase`, `CampfireError`, `Session`, `PromptPackRecord`, `PromptRecord`, and their unit tests. This lane ships first and ships alone — zero SwiftData ceremony in the rules engine, zero UI dependency anywhere. `services_utilities` owns `Services/` (`ModelContainerProvider`, `PromptRepository`, `ContentAccessProvider`, `HapticsService`, `RecapCardRenderer`) plus the actual prompt-pack JSON content and its editorial QA doc — I'm flagging now that "services lane writes the curated prompts" is a slightly odd fit organizationally (an engineer isn't a copywriter), but given we only have four lanes, someone in that lane has to actually sit down and write ~40+ genuinely good reveal prompts, not stub text, or gate 8's whole premise collapses. `primary_ui` owns `App/` (`AppRoute`, `AppCoordinator`, the `CampfireApp.swift` entry glue) and `Features/` (Entry, Setup, Session, Recap screens, `SessionViewModel`) — this is the only lane allowed to touch `SessionViewModel`, per the locked rule that any SwiftData persistence-shape fallback lives strictly behind its save/load conversion step and nowhere else. `polish_resilience` owns `Polish/` (`TransitionGuard`, `ConfettiOverlay`, `DebugFailureInjector`) and `DesignSystem/` (tokens, `CampfireGradientSurface`, `ModeTile`, `ErrorStateView`), plus the two verification-only docs at the end (accessibility sweep, integration audit).

The sequencing that actually matters, pragmatically: nothing UI-shaped starts until the two hard proofs pass — the `RecapOutcome`/`Session` SwiftData round-trip spike, and the two-independent-`ModelContainer` termination-restore test. Both are cheap to write and catastrophic to discover late, so they're early, standalone tasks with their own test files, not folded into "build the models" as if they're free. `TransitionGuard` and `DebugFailureInjector` are also pulled early and made structurally independent of `SessionViewModel` — they're plain, small, unit-testable in isolation — because `services_utilities`' prompt/recap loaders need to consult the failure-injector seam from day one, and I don't want that dependency created backwards later.

On avoiding collisions: the domain types (`Session`, `GameRulesEngine`, `RecapOutcome`, `CampfireError`) are frozen the moment T-001/T-002/T-004 land, and every other lane only ever consumes them — nobody outside `data_domain` edits a domain file, full stop; if a downstream task thinks a signature is wrong, that's a flagged question back to the domain-lane owner, not a silent edit. `project.pbxproj` is the one genuinely shared file — every task appends its own new file references to the target and never restructures anyone else's group. `ContentAccessProviding.isPackUnlocked` stays a stub returning `true` everywhere in this pass; no task is allowed to sneak in real entitlement logic. And the two verification docs (`AccessibilityAudit.md`, `RELEASE_NOTES.md`/`GoNoGo.md`) are the only files their owning tasks may write — if the sweep finds a bug in someone else's screen, it gets logged there and kicked back to the owning task, not silently patched by whoever's running the sweep.

How we know it's actually done matches what's already locked in `project_plan`: scripted — `GameRulesEngine` tests at 2/3/4/15 players (no self-target, no immediate repeat, correct recap derivation across standout/tie/low-variance/zero-data), a double-tap guard test asserting exactly one transition/haptic, and the two-container termination-restore test using two genuinely separate `ModelContainer` instances against the same file URL, not one container reused. Manual but documented — 2-player and 12–15-player full playthroughs with a background and a force-quit at each of handoff/ready/revealed, the empty-pack and recap-render-failure paths fired through the debug injector, and a physical-device `ImageRenderer`/`ShareLink` check, since that layer is known to lie in the simulator. Nothing gets marked done off a screenshot alone.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at round 1, both Codex and Claude independently produced full task breakdowns rather than responding to each other's specific proposal — so this reads as two parallel drafts, not yet a negotiated agreement. There's real convergence on the big structural calls: four lanes matching data_domain/primary_ui/services_utilities/polish_resilience, domain logic frozen and tested before any SwiftData work touches it, the RecapOutcome/Session persistence round-trip spike and the two-independent-ModelContainer termination test both pulled early as standalone gating tasks, TransitionGuard and the debug failure-injection seams built as small decoupled units rather than bolted onto later work, screens built last against already-proven state machinery, and editorial prompt QA treated as a real artifact-producing task rather than a "should." Both also independently landed on the same forbidden-edit-zone principle: nobody outside data_domain touches domain types, and SwiftData persistence-shape fallbacks (if the spike fails) live only inside SessionViewModel's save/load conversion step, never in GameRulesEngine.

But there are real unreconciled differences, not just stylistic ones: Codex assigns the initial project scaffold to `primary_ui`, Claude assigns it to `data_domain`; Codex bundles TransitionGuard into a later primary_ui task alongside haptics, Claude pulls it out as its own early polish_resilience task with zero SessionViewModel dependency; Codex splits prompt content into a separate repo task (services_utilities) plus a separate editorial-QA task (polish_resilience), while Claude puts pack content, the repository, and the QA sign-off into one services_utilities task; and the two use different file-path/project-layout conventions (Codex's full absolute paths under `app_build/Campfire/...` vs. Claude's project-relative paths), which matters because build workers need one unambiguous backlog, not two competing ones referencing possibly-different file locations. Neither agent has actually responded to the other's draft yet, so none of this is settled.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex and Claude to reconcile into a single backlog: agree on who scaffolds the Xcode project (data_domain fits better since it's freezing the type contracts that everything else builds against), agree on TransitionGuard's owning lane and keep it decoupled/early per Claude's version, agree on one file-path convention, and merge the prompt-content/repo/QA split into one clear ownership chain rather than two different task-count structures.

### Round 2


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 2**

This phase is about execution safety, not aesthetics, so I’m aligning with the strictest reading of what both of you already converged on: Codex’s 8-gate sequencing is good, Claude’s insistence on early persistence proof and reachable failure states is non-negotiable, and we should keep domain primitives untouchable by UI and persistence quirks. I’m backing a single-folder build root at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/campfire/app_build/Campfire`, with all Swift code under `Campfire/` and tests under `CampfireTests/` exactly as locked.

The split that actually won’t deadlock is this: `data_domain` defines and owns all models/rules/tests first; `services_utilities` owns container/repo/haptics/renderer/seams and content QA artifact; `primary_ui` owns route/coordinator/session state machine/view glue; `polish_resilience` owns visual-system primitives plus negative-path tooling and audit docs. I’m keeping `SessionViewModel` as the **only** place where SwiftData persistence-shape adaptation is allowed if enum persistence is hostile, and I’m rejecting any temptation to "fix" `GameRulesEngine` because UI timing or decode pressure gets weird. That one rule is what keeps this build from becoming a moving target.

I also want the one contentious point resolved decisively: `TransitionGuard` and `DebugFailureInjector` stay early/isolated, not bolted onto a later screen task. It avoids hidden coupling and lets UI and services consume those seams without accidental circular dependencies. `T-008` includes prompt QA sign-off as an explicit artifact to block recap-copy completion, while still allowing earlier technical tasks to proceed.
